│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_9                                                                                                                         │
│  ├──   Input:              After LangChain, should I study LangGraph, MCP, agent AI, then LLM eval/Ops?                                 │
│  │     Actual Output:      Yes. Per the session, the recommended progression is:                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Start with LangChain (prerequisite)                                                                        │
│  │                         - Then study LangGraph (workflows)                                                                           │
│  │                         - Next cover DPI bar and agentic AI topics (they mentioned MCT and LangGraph-based agentic work)             │
│  │                         - After that move to LLM evaluations                                                                         │
│  │                         - Finally study LLM Ops (paid course by Himanshu)                                                            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │

│  │                         3. **MCP**                                                                                                   │
│  │                         4. **Agent AI**                                                                                              │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_10                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How do GenAI topics compare with ML/DL for AI-engineer roles and interviews?                                 │
│  │     Actual Output:      GenAI topics are the primary, day-to-day focus for AI-engineer roles that build LLM-based applications       │
│  │                         (RAG, agents, prompt engineering, app design, evaluation, infra, safety). You generally do not need deep     │
│  │                         ML/DL fundamentals to make LLM-based applications.                                                           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         That said, interviews vary by company. Some will still ask ML/DL questions even for “AI engineer” roles.     │
│  │                         So practical approach:                                                                                       │
│  │                         - Prioritize core GenAI/AI-engineering topics first (RAG, agent design, evaluation, deployment, prompt       │
│  │                         engineering, safety).                                                                                        │
│  │                         - Start giving interviews early to learn what each company expects.                                          │
│  │                         - If a company asks ML/DL questions, go back and study those fundamentals — prepare ML/DL as needed, but     │
│  │                         it’s not typically the main day-to-day work once you’re an AI engineer.                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Note: common interview questions many struggle with are “How will you evaluate a RAG application?” and       │
│  │                         “How will you evaluate an agent?” — make sure you can answer evaluation-related GenAI questions.             │
│  │     Expected Output:    For an **AI engineer role**, **GenAI topics are the priority**.                                              │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Focus first on **LangChain, LangGraph, MCP, LLM evals, and LLMOps**                                        │
│  │                         - For day-to-day work, **ML/DL is usually not the main thing**                                               │
│  │                         - If **interviewers ask ML/DL questions**, then go back and revise those topics                              │
│  │                         - In the current market, many roles still expect a mix, but for **AI engineering execution**, GenAI comes    │
│  │                         first                                                                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So the practical order is: **GenAI stack first, ML/DL later if needed for interviews**.                      │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│  │                               "description": "Model Context Protocol: a standard for connecting AI apps to tools, data, and          │
│  │                         external systems."                                                                                           │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             {                                                                                                        │

│  │                             },                                                                                                       │

│  │                               "description": "Learn how to evaluate LLM outputs and measure quality properly."                       │

│  │                             }                                                                                                        │

│  │                         interviews.",                                                                                                │

│  │                           ]                                                                                                          │

│  │                         ```                                                                                                          │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_12                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How does quantization reduce decimals, shrink model size, and affect accuracy?                               │
│  │     Actual Output:      - What it does: quantization rounds/truncates the model’s numerical weights so they have fewer decimal       │
│  │                         places (e.g., cutting a long value like 0.989872... that goes to 16 places down to only 4–5 decimal          │
│  │                         places).                                                                                                     │
│  │                         - Why size shrinks: each weight stores fewer digits (fewer bits), so the total storage for all weights       │
│  │                         falls — the model file becomes much smaller and uses less memory at runtime.                                 │
│  │                         - Effect on accuracy: reducing precision introduces small approximation error in the weights, so accuracy    │
│  │                         typically drops slightly (example given: a model that was 90% might go to about 87%).                        │
│  │                         - Practical benefit: because the model is smaller it can run on smaller hardware and use less                │
│  │                         compute/memory during inference.                                                                             │
│  │     Expected Output:    Quantization reduces the decimal precision of model parameters, usually keeping only a few decimal places    │
│  │                         instead of many. This shrinks the model size because fewer digits need to be stored, so it uses less         │
│  │                         memory and can run on smaller hardware. The trade-off is a slight drop in accuracy—for example, a model      │
│  │                         might go from 90% to 87%.                                                                                    │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, student-friendly, and directly answers how       │

│              │                            │       │           │ accuracy. It uses simple language, explains fewer bits and smaller      │
│              │                            │       │           │ storage without heavy jargon, and gives an accessible example of        │
│              │                            │       │           │ slight accuracy loss, making it very easy to understand.                │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_13                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How does pruning remove NN branches to shrink size, enable small hardware, and affect performance?           │
│  │     Actual Output:      - What pruning does: it removes certain branches (parts) of the neural network — keeping only the            │
│  │                         weights/paths you need for the target use. In the example, from a full 10 GB Mistral model you would keep    │
│  │                         only the parts relevant for coding and discard the rest.                                                     │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - How that shrinks size and enables small hardware: by deleting parameters/branches the overall model        │
│  │                         file becomes much smaller, so it can fit and run on lower-memory, lower-compute devices that otherwise       │
│  │                         couldn’t host the full model.                                                                                │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - How it affects performance: you trade capacity for size. Removing branches can reduce the model’s          │
│  │                         ability on discarded tasks or reduce overall accuracy; the model will still work for the retained task(s)    │
│  │                         but may be somewhat less capable than the full model. (The context gives an analogous quantization           │
│  │                         example where accuracy fell from 90% to 87% after size reduction.)                                           │
│  │     Expected Output:    Pruning means removing certain branches or parts of a neural network that are not needed for the task. By    │
│  │                         keeping only the useful sections, the model size decreases, so it can run on smaller hardware. The           │
│  │                         trade-off is that performance may drop slightly.                                                             │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_14                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How will AI integrate into Aadhaar, UPI, ONDC, and Bheeshni as core digital footprints?                      │
│  │     Actual Output:      Short version                                                                                                │
│  │                         AI will be embedded into the deep digital footprint (digital identity, payments, commerce) so it becomes     │
│  │                         a service layer across Aadhaar, UPI, ONDC and Bheeshni — enabling things like voice-based Aadhaar updates    │
│  │                         and voice payments, automation of commerce flows, and AI-driven decisioning.                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         How that integration looks (based on the context)                                                            │
│  │                         - AI as a common service layer: AI models/labs provide capabilities (NLP, voice, decisioning,                │
│  │                         recommendations) that are integrated into platform products rather than being standalone chatbots.           │
│  │                         - Identity linkage: Aadhaar will be linked so AI can act on identity-related tasks (e.g., update/profile     │
│  │                         actions via voice or conversational interfaces) while relying on the Aadhaar authentication/database for     │
│  │                         identity verification.                                                                                       │
│  │                         - Payments: UPI will be an AI-accessible payment gateway so users can initiate and authorize payments via    │
│  │                         AI-driven interfaces (voice/assistant).                                                                      │
│  │                         - Commerce networks: ONDC/Bheeshni and similar platforms will allow AI to manage search, discovery, order    │
│  │                         placement, negotiation, personalization and fulfillment orchestration.                                       │
│  │                         - Labs vs platforms: Labs (Entropy lab, Open AI lab, Gemini, etc.) create AI products; government intends    │
│  │                         to embed those AI products into the core digital platforms.                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Key system design implications you must focus on                                                             │
│  │                         - Integration points: front-end (voice/chat UI) ↔ AI layer ↔ back-end services (authentication DB,           │
│  │                         payment gateway, commerce APIs).                                                                             │
│  │                         - Authentication & dataflow: AI actions must be tied to Aadhaar authentication and the authentication        │
│  │                         database; front-end, back-end and auth DB must be coordinated.                                               │
│  │                         - Transaction flow: AI-driven requests must be routed securely to payment gateways (UPI) and commerce        │
│  │                         networks (ONDC/Bheeshni) with proper sanity checks, consent and audit trails.                                │
│  │                         - Security & privacy: strong cybersecurity, access control, consent management, audit logging and secure     │
│  │                         database management are essential because AI will operate on sensitive identity/payment data.                │
│  │                         - Systems thinking: focus on system design (how components connect, APIs, data schemas, failure modes,       │
│  │                         monitoring) rather than only model development.                                                              │
│  │                         - Operational concerns: database management, secure authentication flows, and end-to-end integration are     │
│  │                         critical so AI can “do the work” safely and reliably.                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         In short: government plans to make AI a built-in part of digital identity, payments and commerce by          │
│  │                         embedding AI services into the front-end/back-end stacks of Aadhaar, UPI, ONDC and Bheeshni. To enable       │
│  │                         this, concentrate on system design, authentication and database integration, payment flow integration,       │
│  │                         and cybersecurity.                                                                                           │
│  │     Expected Output:    AI will be built into India’s core digital identity and payment layers, so it won’t just be a chatbot. It    │
│  │                         can become part of Aadhaar, UPI, ONDC, and Bheeshni, enabling things like voice-command updates,             │
│  │                         voice-based payments, and AI-assisted digital commerce.                                                      │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_15                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How do Siri on-device AFM3 Core models differ from cloud AFM3 models?                                        │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Location and role                                                                                          │
│  │                           - On‑device: AFM3 Core (3 billion parameters) and AFM3 Core Advanced run locally on your device (e.g.,     │
│  │                         Siri’s on‑device brain on Mac).                                                                              │
│  │                           - Cloud: AFM3 Cloud family (AFM3 Cloud, AFM3 Cloud Pro) plus AFM3 Cloud Image (ADM — Apple Diffusion       │
│  │                         Model) run in Apple’s cloud.                                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Capabilities                                                                                               │
│  │                           - On‑device models are smaller/limited (AFM3 Core = 3B) and handle everyday/interactive Siri tasks         │
│  │                         locally.                                                                                                     │
│  │                           - Cloud models are more capable (cloud pro is the advanced version) and support tasks the on‑device        │
│  │                         models cannot, including heavy workloads like image generation (handled by ADM).                             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Behavior                                                                                                   │
│  │                           - Siri uses the on‑device AFM3 Core models by default but will automatically route requests to the         │
│  │                         appropriate cloud model when a task exceeds on‑device capabilities (e.g., “generate an image…”).             │
│  │     Expected Output:    Siri’s **on-device AFM3 Core models** run directly on your iPhone/Mac, so they work **offline**, keep        │
│  │                         data **on your device**, and handle tasks like Siri’s personal assistant functions with privacy intact.      │
│  │                         The **cloud AFM3 models** are used for **heavier tasks** like image generation or more complex requests,     │
│  │                         and the request is sent to Apple’s cloud infrastructure, which in this case is hosted on **Google data       │
│  │                         centers**. Apple says these cloud requests are **not stored or saved** after processing.                     │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ execution and some privacy-oriented routing, but it does not            │
│              │                            │       │           │ explicitly mention offline use or keeping data on the device.           │

│              │                            │       │           │ for heavier tasks like image generation and sending requests to         │

│              │                            │       │           │ cloud, and no node says cloud requests are not stored or saved after    │

│              │                            │       │           │ on-device AFM3 Core models differ from cloud AFM3 models, but it        │

│              │                            │       │           │ models: on-device AFM3 Core runs locally for everyday Siri tasks,       │

│              │                            │       │           │ explaining location, capabilities, and behavior, which makes it         │
│              │                            │       │           │ student friendly. However, it still uses several technical terms and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ model names like AFM3 Core, AFM3 Cloud Pro, and ADM without much        │
│              │                            │       │           │ plain-language simplification, so it is not the simplest possible       │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_16                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Siri on-device vs cloud: offline use, privacy, and functional limits?                                        │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Offline/on-device: Siri AI is described as “on‑device assisted” — it runs on your phone/laptop and         │
│  │                         processes on the device. Some tasks can be handled locally; in some cases Siri may use cloud assistance      │
│  │                         (a data‑center response), but the system is primarily device‑centered.                                       │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Privacy: Your data “never leaves your device” and is not leaked or exposed, so security is not             │
│  │                         compromised according to the context.                                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Functional limits:                                                                                         │
│  │                           - On‑screen awareness: Siri can inspect whatever’s on your screen when you ask (e.g., pause a video and    │
│  │                         “Find this jacket for me”).                                                                                  │
│  │                           - You cannot directly pick or use cloud models for your own general‑purpose apps — Siri decides which      │
│  │                         model to use internally and you don’t choose the model.                                                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    Siri’s on-device model works offline, so it can still function even if the internet is off. It also keeps    │
│  │                         data on your device, so privacy is stronger.                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Cloud models are used for heavier tasks like image generation or more complex requests. Those may require    │
│  │                         internet, and the request goes to Apple’s cloud setup, but Apple says it isn’t stored.                       │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Limits:                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - On-device: smaller models, faster, private, but can’t do everything                                        │
│  │                         - Cloud: more powerful, but needs internet and is used only when Siri decides it’s needed                    │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ and that data never leaves the device, but they do not mention          │
│              │                            │       │           │ offline operation or the internet being off. Sentence 2 is similarly    │
│              │                            │       │           │ only partly supported by node 3, which matches the idea that the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ request/data is not stored, but it does not mention cloud models,       │
│              │                            │       │           │ heavier tasks, image generation, Apple’s cloud setup, or internet       │
│              │                            │       │           │ requirements. Sentence 3 is largely unsupported because no node in      │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context covers the on-device vs cloud limits, speed, power,   │

│              │                            │       │           │ ranked: the first node in retrieval contexts says Siri is "on device    │

│              │                            │       │           │ there are no irrelevant nodes ahead of them, the score cannot be        │

│              │                            │       │           │ and functional limits. It is not higher because it adds stronger        │
│              │                            │       │           │ privacy/security claims than the prompt asked for, including an         │

│              │                            │       │           │ requested comparison.                                                   │

│              │                            │       │           │ data never leaves the phone or device.                                  │

│              │                            │       │           │ with lines like 'Siri AI is on device assisted,' 'So your data never    │
│              │                            │       │           │ leaves your device,' and 'Siri AI decides which model to use,' which    │
│              │                            │       │           │ relate to offline/privacy and model limits. However, it also includes   │

│              │                            │       │           │ being used for heavier tasks like image generation or complex           │
│              │                            │       │           │ requests, and it adds unsupported details like on-screen awareness      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and app-model selection. It also changes the privacy phrasing from      │
│              │                            │       │           │ data staying on-device and cloud requests not being stored to a         │

│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response is organized and easy to follow with short bullets, but    │
│              │                            │       │           │ it uses several technical or product-specific terms such as             │
│              │                            │       │           │ “on-device assisted,” “cloud assistance,” and “general-purpose apps.”   │
│              │                            │       │           │ It also adds functional details like screen awareness that are          │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant, yet the wording is not especially simple or                   │
│              │                            │       │           │ student-friendly for the prompt about offline use, privacy, and         │
│              │                            │       │           │ limits.                                                                 │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_17                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How does MOE enable Siri to load only task-specific params while saving RAM?                                 │
│  │     Actual Output:      When you ask Siri, the on‑device AFM‑3 MOE model doesn’t load the whole network into working memory. The     │
│  │                         LLM (the router/gating part of the MOE) determines which expert/stage is appropriate for that specific       │
│  │                         task and only that small component’s parameters are loaded into RAM and executed. If the task can’t be       │
│  │                         handled on‑device the system redirects to the advanced/core cloud models. By loading only task‑specific      │
│  │                         expert params instead of the entire model, the device saves working memory and compute.                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    MOE, or Mixture of Experts, lets Siri activate only the part of the model that is relevant to your task.     │
│  │                         So instead of loading the full 20B model into RAM, it loads just the expert parameters needed for that       │
│  │                         specific query.                                                                                              │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         This saves RAM, reduces processing load, and makes on-device Siri faster and more efficient.                 │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 0.67  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.67 because sentence 1 is supported by node 1 and node    │
│              │                            │       │           │ 3 in retrieval context, which describe MOE loading only the needed      │
│              │                            │       │           │ expert parameters or small component instead of the whole model.        │
│              │                            │       │           │ Sentence 2 is also supported by node 3, which says the whole model      │
│              │                            │       │           │ will not be loaded into working memory, so it saves RAM and reduces     │
│              │                            │       │           │ load. However, sentence 3 is not fully attributable because no node     │
│              │                            │       │           │ in retrieval context explicitly says Siri is “faster and more           │
│              │                            │       │           │ efficient,” only that it saves compute/RAM.                             │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 0.83  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.83 because the relevant retrieval context nodes are      │
│              │                            │       │           │ ranked well overall: the 1st node says "the whole model will never be   │
│              │                            │       │           │ loaded in your working memory" and that "a small component ... will     │
│              │                            │       │           │ be loaded," directly matching the question, and the 3rd node repeats    │
│              │                            │       │           │ the same idea about loading only a small component and "saving"         │
│              │                            │       │           │ memory. It is not higher because the 2nd node in the retrieval          │
│              │                            │       │           │ contexts is an irrelevant node ranked above the 3rd relevant node; it   │
│              │                            │       │           │ only talks about "3 cloud models" and tasks that "can’t be done on      │
│              │                            │       │           │ device model," so it should be ranked lower than the relevant nodes.    │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.80 because the answer is mostly relevant to how MOE      │
│              │                            │       │           │ helps reduce memory use, but it includes some unrelated discussion      │
│              │                            │       │           │ about fallback to cloud handling instead of focusing fully on how MOE   │
│              │                            │       │           │ loads only task-specific parameters to save RAM. It is still fairly     │
│              │                            │       │           │ high because the core idea is addressed, just not as precisely as it    │
│              │                            │       │           │ could be.                                                               │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.40  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.40 because the actual output adds unsupported details    │
│              │                            │       │           │ about AFM-3, loading the whole network, and task-specific expert        │
│              │                            │       │           │ parameters being loaded into RAM. The context only says Siri uses an    │
│              │                            │       │           │ on-device model and that MOE is an architecture where the LLM knows     │
│              │                            │       │           │ which stage or expert is needed, but it does not mention working        │
│              │                            │       │           │ memory, compute savings, or executing only a small component for the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ chosen task.                                                            │
│        FAIL  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.19  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.19 because most of the retrieval context is filler or    │
│              │                            │       │           │ off-topic, such as "How is Apple operating," "Generate image," and      │
│              │                            │       │           │ "Made cloud models," which do not explain the question. However, a      │
│              │                            │       │           │ few lines are relevant, like "Because it is following MOE               │
│              │                            │       │           │ architecture You give it any query The whole model Will never be        │
│              │                            │       │           │ loaded in your working memory It is a small component That will be      │
│              │                            │       │           │ loaded in your working memory," which supports how MOE loads only       │
│              │                            │       │           │ task-specific parts to save RAM.                                        │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response matches the main idea that MOE activates only              │
│              │                            │       │           │ task-relevant parts of the model, saving RAM and compute for            │
│              │                            │       │           │ on-device Siri. However, it adds extra unsupported specifics such as    │
│              │                            │       │           │ AFM-3, a router/gating LLM, expert/stage loading details, and           │
│              │                            │       │           │ redirection to cloud models, which are not in the expected output. It   │
│              │                            │       │           │ also omits the explicit mention of the full 20B model and the claim     │
│              │                            │       │           │ that on-device Siri becomes faster and more efficient.                  │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.60  │ 0.50      │ The response is understandable but uses several technical terms such    │
│              │                            │       │           │ as "on-device AFM-3 MOE," "LLM," "router/gating," and "expert/stage,"   │
│              │                            │       │           │ which makes it less student-friendly. It does answer the question       │
│              │                            │       │           │ about loading only task-specific parameters to save RAM, but the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ explanation is not very simple or jargon-free.                          │
│                                                                                                                                         │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_18                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              What’s the 1–5 helpfulness rubric: 5, 3, and 1 meanings?                                                     │
│  │     Actual Output:      5 — Fully helpful: correct, accurate, complete and in the right tone. Explains links, provides               │
│  │                         mechanisms, cites examples, and gives a balanced conclusion (meets all five rubric dimensions).              │
│  │                         3 — Partially helpful: provides some useful information but is incomplete or only partly                     │
│  │                         correct/appropriate (missing one or more key dimensions).                                                    │
│  │                         1 — Not helpful: fails to answer usefully (incorrect, irrelevant, or lacking the necessary content/tone).    │
│  │     Expected Output:    5 = correct, accurate, complete, and in the right tone.                                                      │
│  │                         3 = partially helpful.                                                                                       │
│  │                         1 = not helpful.                                                                                             │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because node 1 in retrieval context explicitly        │
│              │                            │       │           │ supports all three sentences in the expected output: sentence 1         │
│              │                            │       │           │ matches the rubric for 5 as “correct, accurate, complete, and in the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ right tone,” sentence 2 matches 3 as “partially helpful,” and           │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 3 matches 1 as “not helpful,” with no unsupported parts.       │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the relevant information is ranked first in   │
│              │                            │       │           │ the 1st node in the retrieval contexts, which directly quotes the       │
│              │                            │       │           │ rubric meanings: '5 it means ... correct accurate complete and in the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ right tone', '3 means partially helpful', and '1 means not helpful'.    │
│              │                            │       │           │ The irrelevant nodes are already lower-ranked, and they should stay     │
│              │                            │       │           │ below because the 2nd node only gives a separate rubric about           │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'explains the link', 'cites examples', and 'balanced conclusion',       │
│              │                            │       │           │ while the 3rd node says 'There is no correct answer defined here' and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ still does not define 5, 3, and 1.                                      │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response directly answers the rubric      │
│              │                            │       │           │ meanings for 5, 3, and 1 with no irrelevant content. It can’t be        │
│              │                            │       │           │ higher because 1.00 is already a perfect match.                         │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.17  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.17 because the actual output added unsupported details   │
│              │                            │       │           │ about links, mechanisms, examples, a balanced conclusion, and ‘all      │
│              │                            │       │           │ five rubric dimensions,’ while the context only says a score of 5 is    │
│              │                            │       │           │ correct, accurate, complete, and in the right tone. It also             │
│              │                            │       │           │ overstates the meanings of scores 3 and 1 by calling them incomplete,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ partly correct, or incorrect/irrelevant, which the context does not     │
│              │                            │       │           │ explicitly define.                                                      │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.62  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.62 because the retrieval context does contain the core   │
│              │                            │       │           │ rubric meanings — "5 it means that the answer from the chat bot is      │
│              │                            │       │           │ first of all correct accurate complete and in the right tone", "3       │
│              │                            │       │           │ means partially helpful", and "1 means not helpful" — but much of it    │
│              │                            │       │           │ is off-topic, like "We are giving this question to the chatbot" and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ "The statement is about a data set of questions," so only part of the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ context directly answers the input.                                     │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The actual output conflicts with the expected rubric labels and         │
│              │                            │       │           │ descriptions. It adds unsupported qualifiers like "provides             │
│              │                            │       │           │ mechanisms" and "balanced conclusion," which are not present in the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ expected output, and it omits the exact phrasing that 5 is "correct,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ accurate, complete, and in the right tone" while 3 and 1 are defined    │
│              │                            │       │           │ more simply. However, the overall mapping of 5/3/1 as helpfulness       │
│              │                            │       │           │ levels is preserved, so the mismatch is limited to wording and added    │
│              │                            │       │           │ detail rather than the core structure.                                  │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response directly answers the rubric meanings for 5, 3, and 1,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ but it uses fairly technical wording such as "mechanisms," "balanced    │
│              │                            │       │           │ conclusion," and "rubric dimensions," which makes it less               │
│              │                            │       │           │ student-friendly than a very simple explanation. It is still clear      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and organized, so it fits the moderately simple range rather than the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ easiest level.                                                          │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_19                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Compare human vs programmatic vs LLM eval methods: pros, cons, and use cases.                                │
│  │     Actual Output:      Human evaluation                                                                                             │
│  │                         - Pros: highest-quality judgment; humans can read nuance and provide detailed reports and qualitative        │
│  │                         feedback (context says human judgment is still better than any kind of AI).                                  │
│  │                         - Cons: costly, slow, and requires effort to produce detailed reports — not practical to keep humans as      │
│  │                         the sole evaluators for large-scale or continuous evaluation.                                                │
│  │                         - Use cases: final QA, subjective judgments, complex safety/ethics assessments, or any setting where         │
│  │                         nuance and human values must be judged.                                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Programmatic (formulaic/automated) evaluation                                                                │
│  │                         - Pros: cheap, automated, fast, and repeatable — uses mathematical formulas and works well when outputs      │
│  │                         and metrics are deterministic (traditional ML-style evaluation).                                             │
│  │                         - Cons: limited for LLMs because LLM outputs are probabilistic and non-deterministic; simple formulas may    │
│  │                         miss quality, relevance, or subtle errors that require judgment.                                             │
│  │                         - Use cases: large-scale automated checks, regression testing, deterministic metrics (e.g., accuracy on      │
│  │                         fixed-label tasks), fast continuous monitoring where ground-truth comparisons suffice.                       │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         LLM-based evaluation methods                                                                                 │
│  │                         - Pros: can run systematic, repeatable tests tailored to LLM behavior (the context calls LLM evals           │
│  │                         “systematic repeatable tests” for LLMs and LLM-powered systems); can automate richer, text-focused           │
│  │                         assessments beyond simple formulas.                                                                          │
│  │                         - Cons: LLM outputs and evaluations inherit probabilistic/non-deterministic properties, so designing         │
│  │                         reliable automated LLM evaluators is challenging; meaningful evaluation still often needs proper judgment    │
│  │                         and detailed reports (which are costly).                                                                     │
│  │                         - Use cases: model evals (evaluating the LLM itself) and application evals (evaluating LLM-powered           │
│  │                         systems); intermediate automation where programmatic rules are insufficient but full human review is too     │
│  │                         expensive.                                                                                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Practical guideline (from context)                                                                           │
│  │                         - Use programmatic evaluation where outputs are deterministic and metrics suffice.                           │
│  │                         - Use human evaluation for high-stakes, subjective, or final-quality judgments despite cost.                 │
│  │                         - Use LLM-based evals for scalable, richer testing of LLM behavior, but expect to combine them with human    │
│  │                         review and careful design because of non-determinism and the need for judgment.                              │
│  │     Expected Output:    Here’s a concise comparison of the three eval methods:                                                       │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Programmatic / deterministic**                                                                           │
│  │                           - **Pros:** fast, cheap, repeatable, easy to scale.                                                        │
│  │                           - **Cons:** works only when the metric is clearly definable; misses nuance.                                │
│  │                           - **Use cases:** retriever evaluation with metrics like **Recall@K**, accuracy for classification,         │
│  │                         exact-match checks.                                                                                          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Human evaluation**                                                                                       │
│  │                           - **Pros:** best for nuanced judgment, quality, helpfulness, tone, completeness.                           │
│  │                           - **Cons:** slow, expensive, subjective; rubric ambiguity can cause disagreement.                          │
│  │                           - **Use cases:** chatbot helpfulness scoring, red teaming, golden dataset creation, human-in-the-loop      │
│  │                         review.                                                                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **LLM-graded evaluation**                                                                                  │
│  │                           - **Pros:** more scalable than humans, can handle nuanced outputs better than rigid rules.                 │
│  │                           - **Cons:** can be inconsistent, biased, or drift without good prompts and calibration.                    │
│  │                           - **Use cases:** grading answer quality, comparing outputs, situations where human review is costly but    │
│  │                         exact programmatic metrics are hard.                                                                         │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         In short: **programmatic for clear metrics, human for nuanced judgment, LLM for scalable approximate         │
│  │                         judgment**.                                                                                                  │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the expected output is fully covered by       │
│              │                            │       │           │ node(s) in retrieval context: sentence 1 on programmatic evaluation’s   │
│              │                            │       │           │ speed, cost, repeatability, and use cases is supported by nodes 1 and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 3; sentence 2 on human evaluation’s strengths, drawbacks, and use       │
│              │                            │       │           │ cases is supported by nodes 1 and 2; sentence 3 on LLM-graded           │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation’s scalability, limits, and use cases is supported by nodes   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 1 and 3; and the final summary sentence aligns with the combined        │
│              │                            │       │           │ points from nodes 1, 2, and 3. Great match.                             │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the retrieval contexts are perfectly          │
│              │                            │       │           │ ordered: the 1st node, 2nd node, and 3rd node are all relevant to       │
│              │                            │       │           │ comparing human, programmatic, and LLM eval methods, with no            │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant nodes placed above any relevant ones. The quoted cues like   │
│              │                            │       │           │ "evaluation is cheap and automated," "human judgment" as "the best      │
│              │                            │       │           │ way to evaluate any LLM based application," and "systematic             │
│              │                            │       │           │ repeatable tests used to judge an LLM" all align directly with the      │
│              │                            │       │           │ input, so there is no need for lower-ranking nodes to be moved down.    │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addresses the comparison   │
│              │                            │       │           │ of human, programmatic, and LLM evaluation methods with relevant        │
│              │                            │       │           │ pros, cons, and use cases, without any irrelevant content.              │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.29  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.29 because the actual output adds many unsupported       │
│              │                            │       │           │ claims: it says human judgment provides the highest-quality, nuanced    │
│              │                            │       │           │ reports, is slow, effortful, and impractical at scale; it also brings   │
│              │                            │       │           │ in final QA, subjective safety/ethics judgments, large-scale            │
│              │                            │       │           │ automated checks, regression testing, continuous monitoring, and        │
│              │                            │       │           │ deterministic programmatic-eval guidelines that the context never       │
│              │                            │       │           │ states. It further claims LLM-based evals are a scalable middle         │
│              │                            │       │           │ ground, automate richer text-focused assessments, and should be         │
│              │                            │       │           │ combined with human review, none of which is explicitly supported by    │
│              │                            │       │           │ the retrieval context.                                                  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.75  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.75 because the context is partly relevant: it includes   │
│              │                            │       │           │ useful points like "Evaluation is cheap and automated," "You need       │
│              │                            │       │           │ Proper judgment," and "First is human judgment," which support          │
│              │                            │       │           │ comparing evaluation approaches. However, some parts are still too      │
│              │                            │       │           │ vague or incomplete, such as "There are four different ways" and "Now   │
│              │                            │       │           │ it is clear Why LLM based applications," so it does not fully cover     │
│              │                            │       │           │ the requested pros, cons, and use cases for human vs programmatic vs    │
│              │                            │       │           │ LLM eval methods.                                                       │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response covers the three evaluation methods and their main pros,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ cons, and use cases, so the overall structure aligns well with the      │
│              │                            │       │           │ expected answer. However, it introduces several extra claims not        │
│              │                            │       │           │ present in the expected output, such as “final QA,” “subjective         │
│              │                            │       │           │ judgments,” “complex safety/ethics assessments,” and the emphasis on    │
│              │                            │       │           │ LLM evals as “systematic repeatable tests” with “detailed reports,”     │
│              │                            │       │           │ which shifts the framing beyond the concise comparison requested. It    │
│              │                            │       │           │ also omits the explicit short summary tying each method to clear        │
│              │                            │       │           │ metrics, nuanced judgment, and scalable approximate judgment.           │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear and well-structured, with separate sections for   │
│              │                            │       │           │ human, programmatic, and LLM evaluation methods. It uses mostly         │
│              │                            │       │           │ simple language and directly addresses pros, cons, and use cases from   │
│              │                            │       │           │ the prompt. However, it includes several technical phrases like         │
│              │                            │       │           │ "probabilistic," "non-deterministic," "regression testing," and         │
│              │                            │       │           │ "ground-truth comparisons," which make it slightly less                 │
│              │                            │       │           │ student-friendly and not maximally simple.                              │
│                                                                                                                                         │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_20                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How do success criteria, dataset coverage, and human rubrics differ in eval pipelines?                       │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Success criteria: from the context, these are the explicit conditions you set so the pipeline can tell     │
│  │                         whether a component/workflow/application is “working properly” — i.e., the pass/fail or scoring rules you    │
│  │                         use to judge outputs.                                                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Dataset coverage: this refers to which cases the evaluation actually exercises. The context emphasizes     │
│  │                         that evaluation pipelines vary by application (RAG vs agent, etc.) and that you will typically need more     │
│  │                         than one pipeline to cover different behaviors — so dataset coverage is about ensuring the evaluation        │
│  │                         data span the relevant scenarios and edge cases for your application.                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Human rubrics: as described, when there is no single correct answer (reference-free evaluation), you       │
│  │                         rely on human judgment and an explicit rubric that tells human evaluators how to score outputs. Human        │
│  │                         rubrics are one of the three primary evaluation methods (programmatic/deterministic, human, model-graded)    │
│  │                         and are used when the golden/correct answer is not present in the dataset.                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         How they differ / relate:                                                                                    │
│  │                         - Success criteria are the what (the goals/thresholds).                                                      │
│  │                         - Dataset coverage is the where/which examples you test those criteria on.                                   │
│  │                         - Human rubrics are one way of applying the success criteria when examples lack a gold answer — they         │
│  │                         define how humans should judge outputs across the covered dataset.                                           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         (Also: to classify a pipeline as reference-free vs reference-based, you ask whether a correct answer is      │
│  │                         present in your golden dataset.)                                                                             │
│  │     Expected Output:    Success criteria define **what “good” means** for the eval pipeline, like accuracy, recall@K, or             │
│  │                         helpfulness.                                                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Dataset coverage defines **what cases you test**, such as normal questions, difficult cases, edge cases,     │
│  │                         and random user queries.                                                                                     │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Human rubrics define **how humans score outputs**, for example a 1–5 scale for correctness, completeness,    │
│  │                         and tone.                                                                                                    │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is fully supported by node 1 in    │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context for success criteria, and sentence 2 is supported     │
│              │                            │       │           │ by nodes 1 and 2 for dataset coverage, while sentence 3 is supported    │
│              │                            │       │           │ by node 1 for human rubrics; all expected sentences are accounted       │
│              │                            │       │           │ for, so the recall is complete and strong.                              │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the first node in retrieval contexts is       │
│              │                            │       │           │ directly relevant, with the quoted phrases "Here we are relying on      │
│              │                            │       │           │ human judgment and defining this rubric" and "reference free            │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation" matching human rubrics. The two irrelevant nodes are        │
│              │                            │       │           │ ranked lower as they only discuss general evaluation approaches like    │
│              │                            │       │           │ "evaluation metrics at each step" and "programmatic or                  │
│              │                            │       │           │ deterministic", "human", and "model graded" without distinguishing      │
│              │                            │       │           │ success criteria, dataset coverage, and human rubrics.                  │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addresses the              │
│              │                            │       │           │ differences between success criteria, dataset coverage, and human       │
│              │                            │       │           │ rubrics without any irrelevant content. Great job staying focused and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ complete.                                                               │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.20  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.20 because the actual output adds unsupported claims     │
│              │                            │       │           │ about explicit success criteria, dataset coverage, relevant             │
│              │                            │       │           │ scenarios/edge cases, and “three primary evaluation methods,” while     │
│              │                            │       │           │ the context only mentions reference-free evaluation, human judgment,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ a rubric, and checking whether the correct answer is in the golden      │
│              │                            │       │           │ dataset.                                                                │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.56  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.56 because the context only partially addresses          │
│              │                            │       │           │ eval-pipeline concepts: it says, 'Here we are relying on human          │
│              │                            │       │           │ judgment and defining this rubric' and 'Simply you have to ask if the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ correct answer is given in your golden data set or not,' but it never   │
│              │                            │       │           │ clearly contrasts success criteria, dataset coverage, and human         │
│              │                            │       │           │ rubrics. Much of it is off-topic or vague, such as 'yeah it depends,'   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'application to application,' and 'the main workflow too many evals     │
│              │                            │       │           │ seem eval pipeline is more complex than creating workflows.'            │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The actual output captures the general roles of success criteria,       │
│              │                            │       │           │ dataset coverage, and human rubrics, but it adds extra framing not      │
│              │                            │       │           │ present in the expected answer and slightly shifts the definitions.     │
│              │                            │       │           │ The expected output gives concise examples: success criteria as         │
│              │                            │       │           │ metrics like accuracy/recall@K/helpfulness, dataset coverage as test    │
│              │                            │       │           │ case types, and human rubrics as 1–5 scoring for                        │
│              │                            │       │           │ correctness/completeness/tone. The response is mostly consistent in     │
│              │                            │       │           │ meaning, but it is more expansive and does not match the expected       │
│              │                            │       │           │ phrasing exactly.                                                       │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear and well-structured, using simple bullet points   │
│              │                            │       │           │ and direct comparisons for success criteria, dataset coverage, and      │
│              │                            │       │           │ human rubrics. It stays student-friendly overall, though it includes    │
│              │                            │       │           │ some technical phrases like "reference-free evaluation,"                │
│              │                            │       │           │ "programmatic/deterministic," and "golden dataset," which make it       │
│              │                            │       │           │ slightly less simple than the easiest category.                         │
│                                                                                                                                         │
