╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_33                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Traceability vs observability in RAG/LLM Ops: input-to-output journey tracking?                              │
│  │     Actual Output:      Both terms are correct.                                                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Traceability (tracing): follow a specific input end-to-end through the entire RAG/LLM workflow. For        │
│  │                         each step/component you record: what input it received, what processing it did, what output it produced,     │
│  │                         and which component that output went to next. This is a per-request, ordered journey from input →            │
│  │                         intermediate outputs → final output.                                                                         │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Observability: the capability to "open the black box" of your application and see that journey. It’s       │
│  │                         the broader practice/ability to monitor and inspect the whole pipeline so you can answer how a given         │
│  │                         output was produced from a given input.                                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Key distinctions from the context:                                                                           │
│  │                         - Tracing is an added step in LLM Ops/RAG that you typically don’t have in traditional MLOps (which has      │
│  │                         logging and experiment tracking but not per-input tracing).                                                  │
│  │                         - In RAG workflows you must capture each component’s input→output because each output becomes the next       │
│  │                         component’s input.                                                                                           │
│  │                         - Together this traceability/observability lets you inspect the full input-to-output journey; the next       │
│  │                         step after that is defining guardrails.                                                                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    Traceability and observability in RAG/LLM Ops mean tracking the full input-to-output journey through         │
│  │                         every component of the workflow.                                                                             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Traceability**: follows how a specific input moves through the pipeline, what each component does,       │
│  │                         and what output it passes to the next step.                                                                  │
│  │                         - **Observability**: is the broader ability to inspect and understand the system end to end.                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         In a RAG app, this helps you see:                                                                            │
│  │                         - which component handled the input,                                                                         │
│  │                         - what processing happened,                                                                                  │
│  │                         - what output was produced at each step,                                                                     │
│  │                         - and how that output became the next input.                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So, it’s basically opening the black box and tracking the journey of the request through the whole           │
│  │                         application.                                                                                                 │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ fully support all sentences: sentence 1 matches the end-to-end          │
│              │                            │       │           │ journey of input to output, sentence 2 aligns with tracing a specific   │
│              │                            │       │           │ input through each pipeline step, sentence 3 is covered by the          │
│              │                            │       │           │ black-box/observability explanation, sentence 4 is supported by         │
│              │                            │       │           │ tracking which component handled the input and what output it           │
│              │                            │       │           │ produced, sentence 5 is supported by output becoming the next input,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ and sentence 6 is directly reinforced by the opening-the-black-box      │
│              │                            │       │           │ framing.                                                                │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the retrieval contexts are perfectly          │
│              │                            │       │           │ ordered: the first node in retrieval contexts, second node in           │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval contexts, and third node in retrieval contexts are all        │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant and each directly supports traceability as 'track the whole    │
│              │                            │       │           │ journey of output from your input' and 'tracing the entire journey of   │
│              │                            │       │           │ every input' with 'what output did it give? which component did it go   │
│              │                            │       │           │ into next?'. Since there are no irrelevant nodes placed above           │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant ones, the ranking is already optimal, so it cannot be          │
│              │                            │       │           │ higher.                                                                 │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 0.64  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.64 because the response includes some relevant           │
│              │                            │       │           │ discussion of traceability and observability in RAG/LLM Ops, but it     │
│              │                            │       │           │ also drifts into generic framing, traditional MLOps comparisons, and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ guardrail recommendations that do not directly answer the               │
│              │                            │       │           │ input-to-output journey tracking question. It’s reasonably relevant     │
│              │                            │       │           │ overall, but not higher since several parts are tangential rather       │
│              │                            │       │           │ than focused on the specific distinction asked.                         │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.55  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.55 because the actual output goes beyond the retrieval   │
│              │                            │       │           │ context by saying tracing records each component’s received input,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ processing, output, and next input, treating observability as broader   │
│              │                            │       │           │ monitoring of the whole pipeline, and comparing LLMOps tracing to       │
│              │                            │       │           │ traditional MLOps—none of which is explicitly stated. It also adds      │
│              │                            │       │           │ guardrails as a next step, which the context does not mention.          │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.87  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.87 because most of the context directly matches the      │
│              │                            │       │           │ query about tracing the input-to-output journey, e.g. it says 'you      │
│              │                            │       │           │ track the whole journey of output from your input' and 'here what are   │
│              │                            │       │           │ you doing here you are tracing the entire journey of every input.'      │
│              │                            │       │           │ The small mismatch comes from a couple of off-topic bits like 'next     │
│              │                            │       │           │ is' and 'next step is your guardrails what are guardrails?', which      │
│              │                            │       │           │ are unrelated to 'Traceability vs observability in RAG/LLM Ops:         │
│              │                            │       │           │ input-to-output journey tracking?'.                                     │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the main idea that traceability follows a         │
│              │                            │       │           │ specific input through the RAG/LLM workflow and that observability is   │
│              │                            │       │           │ the broader ability to inspect the whole system, including the “black   │
│              │                            │       │           │ box” framing and component-by-component input/output flow. However,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ it adds several unsupported details not present in the expected         │
│              │                            │       │           │ answer, such as tracing being an added step not typical in              │
│              │                            │       │           │ traditional MLOps and guardrails being the next step, which             │
│              │                            │       │           │ introduces inaccuracies relative to the reference.                      │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, well-structured, and student friendly. It        │
│              │                            │       │           │ directly answers the input by explaining the input-to-output journey    │
│              │                            │       │           │ tracking in RAG/LLM Ops, distinguishes traceability from                │
│              │                            │       │           │ observability, and uses only light technical terms with simple          │
│              │                            │       │           │ examples like input → intermediate outputs → final output.              │
│                                                                                                                                         │

╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_34                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Explain guardrails as semi-permeable boundary; mention blocked malicious inputs and transparency.            │
│  │     Actual Output:      Guardrails act like a semi-permeable boundary around your application: they are filters that let correct,    │
│  │                         intended inputs pass through seamlessly while blocking malicious or bad inputs. For legitimate users the     │
│  │                         guardrails should be invisible — the right user shouldn’t even notice they exist — but for attackers or      │
│  │                         malformed requests the guardrails fully block the attempt. Apply guardrails in ways that match the end       │
│  │                         user’s knowledge and expectations; if users can infer or understand the guardrails, they may try to          │
│  │                         bypass them. Internal workings of the guardrails (what is used internally) should remain hidden from both    │
│  │                         good and bad users. Even if you use a system prompt, it isn’t 100% secure, so guardrails serve as a final    │
│  │                         check/filtration step (and tie into tracing and evaluation during deployment).                               │
│  │     Expected Output:    Guardrails are like a **semi-permeable boundary** around your application: they let **good, correct          │
│  │                         inputs** pass smoothly, but **block malicious or bad inputs**. Their purpose is to protect the app from      │
│  │                         hacking, wrong answers, and exposing personal data. Also, they should be **transparent** to the end user,    │
│  │                         so the user does not know they are there.                                                                    │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is fully supported by nodes 1      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and 2 in the retrieval context describing guardrails as a               │
│              │                            │       │           │ semi-permeable boundary that lets correct inputs pass and blocks        │
│              │                            │       │           │ malicious ones, sentence 2 is supported by nodes 1, 2, and 3 covering   │
│              │                            │       │           │ protection from hacking, wrong answers, and data exposure, and          │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 3 is supported by nodes 1 and 2 explaining that the user       │
│              │                            │       │           │ should not notice the guardrail. Great recall!                          │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the two relevant nodes in retrieval           │
│              │                            │       │           │ contexts are ranked first and second, and they directly support the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ input with quotes like "your filters" that "block malicious activity"   │
│              │                            │       │           │ and "your semi-permeable boundary" that lets "correct inputs" pass      │
│              │                            │       │           │ while "malicious input" is "completely block," plus transparency. The   │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant node is correctly placed lower at rank 3 because, even       │
│              │                            │       │           │ though it mentions a "final check" and "final filtration," it does      │
│              │                            │       │           │ not clearly cover the semi-permeable boundary idea, blocked malicious   │
│              │                            │       │           │ inputs, or transparency.                                                │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 0.92  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.92 because the response is highly aligned with the       │
│              │                            │       │           │ input, covering the semi-permeable boundary idea, blocked malicious     │
│              │                            │       │           │ inputs, and transparency. It is not higher because it also adds         │
│              │                            │       │           │ deployment tracing/evaluation details that are outside the requested    │
│              │                            │       │           │ explanation.                                                            │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.60  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.60 because the actual output goes beyond the retrieval   │
│              │                            │       │           │ context by claiming guardrails should match the end user’s knowledge    │
│              │                            │       │           │ and expectations, that attackers can infer and use understanding of     │
│              │                            │       │           │ guardrails to bypass them, and that internal workings must stay         │
│              │                            │       │           │ hidden from all users; it also adds tracing/evaluation during           │
│              │                            │       │           │ deployment, none of which is stated in the context.                     │

│              │                            │       │           │ it says guardrails are "your filters," form a "semi-permeable           │
│              │                            │       │           │ boundary," and "will completely block your bad inputs," which aligns    │
│              │                            │       │           │ with blocked malicious inputs. However, much of the surrounding text    │
│              │                            │       │           │ is irrelevant, like "when you are asked about guardrails in an          │
│              │                            │       │           │ interview," "towards deployment part," and "tracing, evaluation," and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ it does not clearly address transparency or the full explanation        │
│              │                            │       │           │ requested.                                                              │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the core idea of guardrails as a semi-permeable   │
│              │                            │       │           │ boundary that lets correct inputs pass and blocks malicious ones, and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ it also notes transparency/invisibility to users. However, it adds      │
│              │                            │       │           │ several unsupported details not present in the expected output, such    │
│              │                            │       │           │ as attackers inferring guardrails, internal workings needing to         │
│              │                            │       │           │ remain hidden, system prompts not being 100% secure, and                │
│              │                            │       │           │ tracing/evaluation during deployment. These additions go beyond the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ requested content and make it not an exact match.                       │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response explains guardrails as a semi-permeable boundary and       │
│              │                            │       │           │ clearly mentions blocking malicious or malformed inputs, which          │
│              │                            │       │           │ matches the prompt. It is fairly understandable, but it includes        │

│              │                            │       │           │ simple than required.                                                   │

╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_35                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How does exp. tracking differ from traceability in LLM Ops, incl. tracked items?                             │
│  │     Actual Output:      Experiment tracking                                                                                          │
│  │                         - Purpose: track experiments/components/moving parts of the application (the “road/highway” and its          │
│  │                         pillars) to know which configurations and components were used and whether they’re working.                  │
│  │                         - Typical items tracked (RAG / LLM Ops examples given in context):                                           │
│  │                           - which document(s) were used                                                                              │
│  │                           - parameters/configuration you set                                                                         │
│  │                           - chunking strategy (chunk size, chunk overlap)                                                            │
│  │                           - embedding model used                                                                                     │
│  │                           - model used and provider                                                                                  │
│  │                           - other experiment-level metadata (versions, overall metrics, logs)                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Traceability                                                                                                 │
│  │                         - Purpose: trace the end-to-end journey of every single input to its output — show how the output of each    │
│  │                         input was produced.                                                                                          │
│  │                         - What is tracked: the per-input lineage through the pipeline — i.e., which document and which chunk(s)      │
│  │                         were selected for that input, the intermediate steps and components hit, and the exact calls/parameters      │
│  │                         that produced the output (the sequence of retrieval/embedding/model steps, timestamps/ordering).             │
│  │                         - Role difference: experiment tracking records what components/configs exist and were used across runs;      │
│  │                         traceability links each individual input to the exact path and components that produced its specific         │
│  │                         output.                                                                                                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    Experiment tracking records the **components and configuration** of your application — for example,          │
│  │                         models used, vector store, retriever, parameters, and their values.                                          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Traceability records the **journey of the input through the workflow** — what component it went into,        │
│  │                         what output it produced, and how that output became the next input.                                          │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ configuration such as document, parameters, chunking strategy,          │
│              │                            │       │           │ embedding model, and provider; sentence 2 is fully supported by node    │
│              │                            │       │           │ 2 and node 3 in retrieval context, which describe tracing as            │
│              │                            │       │           │ following the journey from input to output and how output becomes the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ next input. Great coverage overall.                                     │

│              │                            │       │           │ experiment-tracking details with tracked items like "which document     │
│              │                            │       │           │ you used", "what were the parameters", "chunking strategy", "chunk      │
│              │                            │       │           │ overlap", "chunk size", and "which embedding model you used"; the       │
│              │                            │       │           │ second node in retrieval contexts cleanly covers traceability as        │
│              │                            │       │           │ "looking at how is the journey from input to output" and "tracing the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ entire journey of every input"; and the third node in retrieval         │
│              │                            │       │           │ contexts reinforces the contrast by saying experiment tracking          │
│              │                            │       │           │ follows application components while traceability follows the           │
│              │                            │       │           │ input-to-output journey. There are no irrelevant nodes placed above     │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant ones, so the ranking is ideal.                                 │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response is fully relevant to the         │
│              │                            │       │           │ question and includes the needed distinction between experiment         │
│              │                            │       │           │ tracking and traceability in LLM Ops, along with the tracked items,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ without any irrelevant content. Great job.                              │

│              │                            │       │           │ supported by the context, such as describing the application as a       │
│              │                            │       │           │ "road/highway" with "pillars." It also goes beyond the context by       │
│              │                            │       │           │ claiming experiment tracking includes overall metrics, logs, and        │
│              │                            │       │           │ other metadata, and by asserting per-input lineage details like         │
│              │                            │       │           │ selected chunks, intermediate steps, exact calls, and                   │

│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.86  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.86 because the context mostly answers the question: it   │
│              │                            │       │           │ says experiment tracking is about “whatever components of your          │
│              │                            │       │           │ application whatever moving parts” and specific tracked items like      │
│              │                            │       │           │ “which document you used,” “parameters,” “chunk overlap,” “chunk        │
│              │                            │       │           │ size,” and “which embedding model you used,” while traceability         │
│              │                            │       │           │ “tracks the journey of your input to output” and is “an added step”     │
│              │                            │       │           │ with “ML Ops has logging experiment tracking but no tracing.” A small   │
│              │                            │       │           │ amount is still irrelevant since it “focuses on ‘model                  │
│              │                            │       │           │ explainability’ after deployment” and includes the vague phrase “this   │
