│              │                            │       │           │ 'Okay?' that do not specifically address enterprise AI safety,          │
│              │                            │       │           │ guardrails, or governance.                                              │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the main theme that enterprises need AI safety,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ guardrails, and governance to reduce operational and security risk,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ and it correctly mentions prompt injection/jailbreaks, shadow AI, and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ silent interpretation errors. However, it adds several extra claims     │
│              │                            │       │           │ not present in the expected answer, such as red teams,                  │
│              │                            │       │           │ bias/legal/ethical staffing, and detailed deployment controls, and it   │
│              │                            │       │           │ slightly distorts the original framing by replacing the specific        │
│              │                            │       │           │ point about open-source models with broader discussion of autonomous    │
│              │                            │       │           │ AI and governance. The core meaning is mostly preserved, but it is      │
│              │                            │       │           │ not an exact match.                                                     │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear and organized with bullet points, and it          │
│              │                            │       │           │ directly addresses prompt injection, jailbreaks, shadow AI, and         │
│              │                            │       │           │ silent interpretation errors. However, it uses several technical        │
│              │                            │       │           │ terms like "LLM evaluations," "agentic/autonomous AI," and "cyber       │
│              │                            │       │           │ law," which makes it less student-friendly and only moderately simple   │
│              │                            │       │           │ rather than very simple.                                                │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_45                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Why do LLM apps need multiple eval pipelines?                                                                │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Different applications require different evaluation pipelines: e.g., RAG apps and agent-based apps need    │
│  │                         different types of evals and metrics.                                                                        │
│  │                         - Evaluation metrics are needed at each step/components of the system, so one pipeline can’t capture all     │
│  │                         step-level behavior.                                                                                         │
│  │                         - A single eval can’t capture every failure mode — you must think about how the LLM application can fail     │
│  │                         and build separate pipelines to detect those failures.                                                       │
│  │                         - Multiple pipelines let you measure different objectives (quality, retrieval accuracy, agent                │
│  │                         decision-making, etc.), which vary by application.                                                           │
│  │                         - In practice there will be many evals and the eval infrastructure often becomes more complex than the       │
│  │                         workflow itself.                                                                                             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Summary: because apps are heterogeneous, multi-step, and have many failure modes, you need more than one     │
│  │                         evaluation pipeline to adequately assess them.                                                               │
│  │     Expected Output:    LLM apps need multiple eval pipelines because they can fail in different places and in different ways.       │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - In a RAG app, the retriever can fetch wrong documents and the generator can hallucinate.                   │
│  │                         - Different risk categories need different checks: correctness, relevance, faithfulness, safety, latency,    │
│  │                         cost, etc.                                                                                                   │
│  │                         - The right evals depend on the app type, like RAG, agents, or multi-turn chatbots.                          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So one eval pipeline cannot capture everything.                                                              │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ and 3, which mention RAG-specific failures like bad retrieval and       │
│              │                            │       │           │ hallucinations; sentence 3 is supported by nodes 1 and 2, which cover   │
│              │                            │       │           │ different risk checks and app-type-specific evals; sentence 4 is        │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by node 3, which states one pipeline cannot capture           │
│              │                            │       │           │ everything. All sentences are accounted for, so the recall is           │
│              │                            │       │           │ complete.                                                               │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the top 3 nodes in retrieval contexts are     │

│              │                            │       │           │ than one evaluation pipeline" and that it "depends ... application to   │
│              │                            │       │           │ application," the 2nd says "there is not just one single Eval           │
│              │                            │       │           │ Pipeline" and asks "why do we need multiple Eval Pipelines," and the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ 3rd explains that "only one single evaluation pipeline can't capture    │
│              │                            │       │           │ everything." Since there are no irrelevant nodes ahead of relevant      │
│              │                            │       │           │ ones, the ranking is fully precise.                                     │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addressed why LLM apps     │
│              │                            │       │           │ need multiple eval pipelines with no irrelevant statements. It stays    │
│              │                            │       │           │ completely on-topic and directly answers the question, so there’s no    │
│              │                            │       │           │ basis for a higher relevancy score.                                     │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.50  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.50 because the actual output goes beyond the context     │
│              │                            │       │           │ by claiming step-level behavior, specific objectives like quality,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval accuracy, and agent decision-making, and that                 │

│              │                            │       │           │ which are explicitly stated in the retrieval context.                   │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.76  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.76 because most of the context directly supports the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ question, including that there are "different type of evaluation        │
│              │                            │       │           │ pipelines" and that "only one single evaluation pipeline can't          │
│              │                            │       │           │ capture everything". However, some parts are irrelevant, like "any      │
│              │                            │       │           │ other question at this point," "With this statement we will start our   │
│              │                            │       │           │ session today," and "some more questions are coming," which lowers      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the score a bit.                                                        │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The response matches the expected core message that LLM apps need       │
│              │                            │       │           │ multiple eval pipelines because different app types and failure modes   │
│              │                            │       │           │ require different checks. It covers RAG and agent-based apps,           │
│              │                            │       │           │ step-level/component evaluation, and multiple objectives like quality   │
│              │                            │       │           │ and retrieval accuracy. The only minor difference is that it adds       │
│              │                            │       │           │ extra commentary about eval infrastructure complexity, but this does    │
│              │                            │       │           │ not contradict the expected output.                                     │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, student-friendly, and easy to understand. It     │
│              │                            │       │           │ explains with simple examples that different apps like RAG and          │
│              │                            │       │           │ agent-based systems need different metrics, and it avoids heavy         │
│              │                            │       │           │ jargon while covering multiple failure modes and step-level             │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation needs.                                                       │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_46                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Compare retriever vs generator failures in RAG: root cause, impact, mitigation, JSON mode.                   │
│  │     Actual Output:      {                                                                                                            │
│  │                           "retriever": {                                                                                             │
│  │                             "root_cause": "Retriever fetched wrong documents or built an incorrect context.",                        │
│  │                             "impact": "Generator receives wrong context and therefore produces wrong answers (downstream             │
│  │                         incorrect responses).",                                                                                      │
│  │                             "mitigation": [                                                                                          │
│  │                               "Use retriever-specific metrics to detect and diagnose incorrect retrievals.",                         │
│  │                               "Use pipeline/end-to-end metrics to observe downstream effects of retrieval errors.",                  │
│  │                               "Verify how the context is being built during the injection phase to ensure correct documents are      │
│  │                         provided to the generator."                                                                                  │
│  │                             ]                                                                                                        │
│  │                           },                                                                                                         │
│  │                           "generator": {                                                                                             │
│  │                             "root_cause": "Generator fails to produce the expected or sufficiently specific output (e.g., returns    │
│  │                         a different format than expected).",                                                                         │
│  │                             "impact": "Application receives outputs in an unexpected format or lacking required specificity,         │
│  │                         causing generator-related failures for the app.",                                                            │
│  │                             "mitigation": [                                                                                          │
│  │                               "Use generator-specific metrics to detect formatting and specificity issues.",                         │
│  │                               "Inspect generator outputs and adjust prompts/configuration so the generator produces the expected     │
│  │                         format and level of specificity.",                                                                           │
│  │                               "Use pipeline/end-to-end metrics to confirm whether generator fixes resolve application-level          │
│  │                         issues."                                                                                                     │
│  │                             ]                                                                                                        │
│  │                           },                                                                                                         │
│  │                           "general_advice": "Read individual metrics to understand which metrics are retriever-specific,             │
│  │                         generator-specific, or pipeline-level, and use the appropriate metrics to diagnose and fix each failure      │
│  │                         type."                                                                                                       │
│  │                         }                                                                                                            │
│  │     Expected Output:    In RAG, retriever and generator fail in different ways:                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Retriever failure**                                                                                      │
│  │                           - **Root cause:** fetches irrelevant or wrong chunks from the vector database.                             │
│  │                           - **Impact:** generator gets bad context, so the final answer is likely wrong.                             │
│  │                           - **Mitigation:** evaluate **context relevance**, **retrieval recall**, **precision**, and **reranker      │
│  │                         quality**.                                                                                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Generator failure**                                                                                      │
│  │                           - **Root cause:** even with correct context, the LLM may ignore it or hallucinate.                         │
│  │                           - **Impact:** answer is not grounded in the retrieved documents.                                           │
│  │                           - **Mitigation:** evaluate **faithfulness / groundedness** and **citation accuracy**.                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **JSON mode**                                                                                              │
│  │                           - The context does not mention JSON mode specifically, so no direct comparison is available here.          │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 0.89  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.89 because sentence 1 is well supported by nodes 1 and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 3, which mention the two failure modes: retriever failure and           │
│              │                            │       │           │ generator failure. Sentence 2 is also supported by node 2, which        │
│              │                            │       │           │ describes retriever failure as fetching wrong/irrelevant documents      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and leading to bad context, and nodes 2–3 support the impact that bad   │
│              │                            │       │           │ context causes wrong answers. Sentence 3 is only partially supported    │
│              │                            │       │           │ by nodes 1 and 2, since they mention metrics for retriever/generator    │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation, but not the specific metrics listed. Sentence 4 is          │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported in part by node 1 and node 3, which frame generator failure   │
│              │                            │       │           │ as occurring even when retrieval works, but the specific idea of        │
│              │                            │       │           │ hallucinating or ignoring context is not explicit. Sentence 5 is        │
│              │                            │       │           │ weakly supported by node 2, which mentions output format issues, but    │
│              │                            │       │           │ no node directly states that the answer is not grounded in the          │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieved documents, and nodes 1–3 do not mention JSON mode             │
│              │                            │       │           │ specifically.                                                           │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because all retrieval context nodes are relevant      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and are ranked well: the 1st node directly frames the two failure       │
│              │                            │       │           │ modes in RAG, the 2nd node explains generator vs retriever failure      │
│              │                            │       │           │ causes and impacts, and the 3rd node gives a concrete example of        │
│              │                            │       │           │ wrong documents leading to wrong answers. Since there are no            │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant nodes above relevant ones, the ranking is already optimal.   │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response is fully relevant to the         │
│              │                            │       │           │ request and contains no irrelevant statements, so it directly           │
│              │                            │       │           │ addresses the comparison of retriever vs. generator failures in RAG     │
│              │                            │       │           │ along with root cause, impact, mitigation, and JSON mode.               │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.58  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.58 because the actual output overstates the retrieval    │
│              │                            │       │           │ context by claiming generator root causes and impacts that are not      │
│              │                            │       │           │ stated there: it says the generator fails to produce the expected       │
│              │                            │       │           │ format or sufficient specificity, mentions inspecting generator         │
│              │                            │       │           │ outputs or adjusting prompts/configuration, and recommends pipeline     │
│              │                            │       │           │ or end-to-end metrics to confirm fixes, none of which the context       │
│              │                            │       │           │ explicitly supports.                                                    │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.88  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.88 because the context does cover the core contrast      │
│              │                            │       │           │ between failures, noting that “there are generally two types of         │
│              │                            │       │           │ failure modes,” namely “the failure of the retriever” and “the          │
│              │                            │       │           │ failure of the generator.” However, it is still only partially          │
│              │                            │       │           │ aligned since the irrelevancy notes say it “does not address the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ input's requested comparison of ‘root cause, impact, mitigation, JSON   │
│              │                            │       │           │ mode’” and that the other piece is incomplete and “does not provide     │
│              │                            │       │           │ information connected to the input’s comparison.”                       │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.50  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the broad idea that retriever errors lead to      │
│              │                            │       │           │ bad context and generator errors affect outputs, but it misses key      │
│              │                            │       │           │ rubric details from the expected output. It does not mention the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ specific retriever mitigations of context relevance, retrieval          │
│              │                            │       │           │ recall, precision, and reranker quality, nor the generator              │
│              │                            │       │           │ mitigations of faithfulness/groundedness and citation accuracy. It      │
│              │                            │       │           │ also adds unsupported claims about formatting and specificity, and      │
│              │                            │       │           │ omits the JSON mode note that no direct comparison is available.        │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, well-structured, and easy to follow with         │
│              │                            │       │           │ simple labels for retriever and generator failures. It explains root    │
│              │                            │       │           │ cause, impact, and mitigation in plain language, and briefly mentions   │
│              │                            │       │           │ metric types and JSON mode/formatting issues without heavy jargon.      │
│              │                            │       │           │ The only minor drawback is that it is a bit detailed rather than        │
│              │                            │       │           │ максимально simple, but it still fits the student-friendly              │
│              │                            │       │           │ requirement well.                                                       │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_47                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Key LLM risk categories for gen, RAG, agents, multi-turn chatbots, safety, ops?                              │
│  │     Actual Output:      Here are compact, actionable risk categories mapped to each area you asked about, with typical failure       │
│  │                         points and quick checks (drawn from your context).                                                           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         1) General generation (gen)                                                                                  │
│  │                         - Correctness / factuality: hallucinations, wrong facts or invented details. Check: human or automated       │
│  │                         eval vs ground truth; A/B prompts (Did prompt V2 improve over V1?).                                          │
│  │                         - Prompt regressions / prompt engineering risk: small prompt changes degrade output quality. Check:          │
│  │                         prompt version comparisons, automated regression tests.                                                      │
│  │                         - Relevance / task completion: output doesn’t meet the requested task (e.g., wrong format, missing           │
│  │                         steps). Check: unit tests / rubric-based scoring.                                                            │
│  │                         - Safety / policy compliance: toxic, biased, or disallowed content. Check: safety filters and red-team       │
│  │                         tests.                                                                                                       │
│  │                         - Performance / latency: slow or unpredictable response times. Check: latency SLAs and load tests.           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         2) RAG (retrieval-augmented generation)                                                                      │
│  │                         - Grounding / citation risk: model hallucinates beyond retrieved context or misattributes sources. Check:    │
│  │                         “Is the RAG answer grounded in retrieved context?”                                                           │
│  │                         - Retrieval quality: missing, irrelevant, or low-quality documents returned. Check: retrieval                │
│  │                         precision/recall metrics and QA over retrieved docs.                                                         │
│  │                         - Index freshness and coverage: stale or incomplete knowledge leading to wrong answers. Check: index         │
│  │                         update processes and coverage audits.                                                                        │
│  │                         - Mapping errors / context-window truncation: relevant passages omitted during generation. Check:            │
│  │                         end-to-end tests that verify source-to-answer traceability.                                                  │
│  │                         - Pipeline failures: retrieval, vector store, or connector outages producing empty or incorrect context.     │
│  │                         Check: health checks and fallbacks.                                                                          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         3) Agents (tool-using systems)                                                                               │
│  │                         - Tool-selection and invocation errors: agent calls wrong tool or wrong API params. Check: sandboxed tool    │
│  │                         executions and unit tests of action selection.                                                               │
│  │                         - Incorrect tool output handling: misinterprets tool results or fails to validate them. Check:               │
│  │                         validation/assertion on tool responses.                                                                      │
│  │                         - Task incompletion / loop or hang: agent fails to finish or gets stuck in loops. Check: action timeouts,    │
│  │                         step limits, deterministic replay tests.                                                                     │
│  │                         - Unsafe or unintended actions: agent performs harmful side-effects via tools. Check: RBAC, action           │
│  │                         authorization, simulated dry-runs.                                                                           │
│  │                         - Chain-of-trust / provenance: inability to trace which tool produced what. Check: structured logging of     │
│  │                         actions and results.                                                                                         │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         4) Multi-turn chatbots                                                                                       │
│  │                         - Context loss / state drift: forgetting earlier user instructions or mixing contexts across                 │
│  │                         conversations. Check: conversation-history tests and summaries consistency.                                  │
│  │                         - Inconsistent persona or answers: contradictory replies over turns. Check: persona and consistency          │
│  │                         tests.                                                                                                       │
│  │                         - Ambiguity / misaligned intent tracking: misinterprets follow-ups or implicit requests. Check:              │
│  │                         intent-slot tracking and clarifying-question policies.                                                       │
│  │                         - Escalation failures: not handing off to human when needed. Check: escalation triggers and workflow         │
│  │                         tests.                                                                                                       │
│  │                         - Safety over time: gradual policy erosion across long conversations (e.g., jailbreak sequences). Check:     │
│  │                         chained-turn adversarial testing.                                                                            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         5) Safety (cross-cutting)                                                                                    │
│  │                         - Harmful outputs: offensive, violent, illegal, medical/legal unsafe advice. Check: safety classifiers,      │
│  │                         red-team scenarios.                                                                                          │
│  │                         - Privacy / data leakage: leaking PII or secret data in responses. Check: PII detection, data                │
│  │                         minimization, training-data audits.                                                                          │
│  │                         - Jailbreaks / prompt injection: users bypass restrictions. Check: prompt-injection tests and input          │
│  │                         sanitization.                                                                                                │
│  │                         - User trust / reputational risk: misleading confidence or lack of disclaimers. Check: phrasing policies     │
│  │                         and uncertainty calibration.                                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         6) Ops / reliability / observability                                                                         │
│  │                         - Latency and scalability: slow responses or failure under load. Check: load tests and latency monitoring    │
│  │                         (Is the latency under control?).                                                                             │
│  │                         - Monitoring and alerting gaps: undetected failures or silent degradations. Check: end-to-end SLOs, error    │
│  │                         budgets, health dashboards.                                                                                  │
│  │                         - Versioning and rollout risk: model or prompt rollouts cause regressions. Check: canarying, A/B tests,      │
│  │                         rollback procedures (Did prompt V2 improve over V1?).                                                        │
│  │                         - Logging, privacy, and retention: over-logging sensitive content or insufficient logs for debugging.        │
│  │                         Check: redaction, access controls, retention policies.                                                       │
│  │                         - Data quality and drift: model performance degrading due to changing distributions. Check: drift            │
│  │                         detection and periodic re-evaluation.                                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Quick prioritized checks you already hinted at (good for gating release):                                    │
│  │                         - Is the system producing expected outputs (accuracy / task completion)?                                     │
│  │                         - Is the RAG output grounded in retrieved context?                                                           │
│  │                         - Did prompt or model changes improve or regress (A/B)?                                                      │
│  │                         - Is the agent completing tasks correctly and safely?                                                        │
│  │                         - Is the chatbot safe for real users (red-team + classifiers)?                                               │
│  │                         - Is latency and reliability within acceptable bounds?                                                       │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         If you want, I can expand any section with concrete failure-mode examples, test cases, or                    │
│  │                         monitoring/mitigation suggestions for each risk.                                                             │
│  │     Expected Output:    Key LLM risk categories by application:                                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - General generation: correctness, accuracy, relevance, completeness, instruction following                  │
│  │                         - RAG: context relevance, retrieval recall/precision, groundedness/faithfulness, citation accuracy           │
│  │                         - Agents: tool selection, parameter correctness, task completion, error recovery                             │
│  │                         - Multi-turn chatbots: context retention, clarification behavior                                             │
│  │                         - Safety: toxicity, harmful content, bias, PII leakage, prompt injection/jailbreak resistance                │
│  │                         - Operations: latency, cost per request, token efficiency, failure rate, latency under load                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         These are why a single eval pipeline is usually not enough.                                                  │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is fully supported by node 1 in    │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context through the listed application risk areas, and        │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 2 is supported by node 3’s point that LLM apps have multiple   │
│              │                            │       │           │ failure points, explaining why a single eval pipeline is usually not    │
│              │                            │       │           │ enough. No sentence is left unsupported, and the relevant coverage      │
│              │                            │       │           │ comes from nodes 1, 2, and 3.                                           │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because all three retrieval context nodes are         │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant and correctly ranked at the top: the 1st node directly asks    │
│              │                            │       │           │ about "risk categories" for normal LLM applications, the 2nd node       │
│              │                            │       │           │ gives concrete examples like "Is the rag answer grounded in retrieved   │
│              │                            │       │           │ context?" and "Is the agent completing the task correctly?", and the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ 3rd node explains "multiple failure points" across applications.        │
│              │                            │       │           │ There are no irrelevant nodes placed above relevant ones, so the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ ordering is ideal.                                                      │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addresses the requested    │
│              │                            │       │           │ LLM risk categories for gen, RAG, agents, multi-turn chatbots,          │
│              │                            │       │           │ safety, and ops, with no irrelevant content to detract from its         │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevance. Great job staying on-topic and complete.                     │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.04  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.04 because the actual output appears to invent a large   │
│              │                            │       │           │ number of risk categories and checks that are not in the retrieval      │
│              │                            │       │           │ context, such as compact/actionable mapping, hallucinations, prompt     │
│              │                            │       │           │ regressions, safety filters, latency SLAs, grounding and retrieval      │
│              │                            │       │           │ quality metrics, tool failures, privacy/PII, jailbreaks, monitoring,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ rollout risk, and drift detection. It also adds claims about            │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation methods like human/automated ground-truth checks, red-team   │
│              │                            │       │           │ tests, unit tests, and A/B or canary rollouts that the context never    │
│              │                            │       │           │ mentions.                                                               │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.58  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.58 because the context does include some directly        │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant risk prompts like 'what are the specific risk categories',     │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'agent specific risk categories', 'what are the risk categories in      │
│              │                            │       │           │ multi turn chatbot', and 'Is the rag answer grounded in retrieved       │
│              │                            │       │           │ context?', but it is also diluted by irrelevant shipping/readiness      │
│              │                            │       │           │ remarks such as 'Is this system good enough to ship?' and 'Can we put   │
│              │                            │       │           │ it into production?'. It is therefore partially relevant, but not       │
│              │                            │       │           │ tightly focused on the requested key LLM risk categories for gen,       │
│              │                            │       │           │ RAG, agents, multi-turn chatbots, safety, and ops.                      │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response covers all the main application areas and mostly aligns    │
│              │                            │       │           │ with the expected categories, but it adds several extra items not       │
│              │                            │       │           │ present in the expected output, such as prompt regressions, index       │
│              │                            │       │           │ freshness, pipeline failures, escalation failures, and                  │
│              │                            │       │           │ monitoring/versioning details. It also slightly shifts the ops          │
│              │                            │       │           │ category to broader reliability/observability terms and adds safety     │
│              │                            │       │           │ and performance checks beyond the listed rubric, so it is mostly        │
│              │                            │       │           │ correct but not an exact match to the provided key risks.               │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response is organized and easy to follow, but it is not             │
│              │                            │       │           │ especially simple for a student-friendly answer. It uses many           │
│              │                            │       │           │ technical terms like 'grounding', 'RBAC', 'provenance', 'canarying',    │
│              │                            │       │           │ and 'drift detection', and includes a long multi-section breakdown      │
│              │                            │       │           │ rather than a very plain summary. It does answer the input broadly by   │
│              │                            │       │           │ listing key risk categories for gen, RAG, agents, multi-turn            │
│              │                            │       │           │ chatbots, safety, and ops, but the language is moderately technical.    │
│                                                                                                                                         │

╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_48                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How does sequence-based fine-tuning coverage compare with a separate industry master class?                  │
│  │     Actual Output:      Sequence-based coverage                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Pros: Full control of content and sequencing; builds necessary foundations (e.g., how LLMs are             │
│  │                         pre-trained) before introducing fine-tuning so students can understand it properly; less risk of             │
│  │                         confusing or overwhelming learners; aligns with the instructor’s plan to teach in order.                     │
│  │                         - Cons: Delays direct coverage of fine-tuning; students who want immediate depth won’t get it right away.    │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Separate industry master class                                                                               │
│  │                         - Pros: Can provide immediate, practical/industry perspective; can be run as a free master class and put     │
│  │                         on YouTube to gather feedback; useful if there’s strong demand and you want a quick standalone treatment.    │
│  │                         - Cons: Less control because you don’t have data on how that external instructor teaches; may not align      │
│  │                         with your sequence and could confuse learners who lack prior background; harder to ensure consistent         │
│  │                         depth and pedagogy.                                                                                          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Recommendation (from the context)                                                                            │
│  │                         - Follow the sequence for the core course so students learn prerequisites first. If demand is strong,        │
│  │                         bring an industry expert to run a separate master class (free/YouTube) as a supplement, collect feedback,    │
│  │                         and then decide whether to arrange a fuller course.                                                          │
│  │     Expected Output:    Sequence-based fine-tuning coverage will come later, around December-January and likely by the end of the    │
│  │                         year, while a separate industry master class can be arranged sooner if there is enough demand. The master    │
│  │                         class would be taught by an industry expert, but the sequence-based approach is preferred for proper         │
│  │                         learning.                                                                                                    │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is fully supported by nodes 1      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and 3 in the node(s) in retrieval context, which together indicate      │
│              │                            │       │           │ sequence-based coverage later and a separate master class sooner;       │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 2 is supported by nodes 1 and 3 as they mention bringing       │
│              │                            │       │           │ someone from the industry and conducting the topic separately; and      │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 3 is supported by nodes 2 and 3, which state                   │
│              │                            │       │           │ fine-tuning/that branch is not yet covered and that covering topics     │
│              │                            │       │           │ out of sequence is not effective, matching the preference for           │
│              │                            │       │           │ sequence-based learning.                                                │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the retrieval contexts are perfectly          │
│              │                            │       │           │ ordered: the first node in the retrieval contexts says 'I will cover    │
│              │                            │       │           │ in a sequence' and 'if someone teaches now then I will not teach fine   │
│              │                            │       │           │ tuning,' which directly supports sequence-based coverage; the second    │
│              │                            │       │           │ node says 'I can bring someone else and I can conduct this topic        │
│              │                            │       │           │ separately' and 'if you get so much demand what I can do is I can       │
│              │                            │       │           │ arrange,' supporting a separate industry master class; and the third    │
│              │                            │       │           │ node reinforces this with 'we can bring someone from the industry'      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and 'I can arrange... and I can conduct this topic separately.' There   │
│              │                            │       │           │ are no irrelevant nodes placed above relevant ones, so nothing needs    │
│              │                            │       │           │ to be ranked lower.                                                     │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response is fully relevant to the         │
│              │                            │       │           │ question and contains no irrelevant statements. It directly addresses   │
│              │                            │       │           │ the comparison asked for, so it cannot reasonably be higher.            │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because there are no contradictions, so the actual    │
│              │                            │       │           │ output appears fully faithful to the retrieval context. Great job!      │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.64  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.64 because the context is partly on-topic: it includes   │
│              │                            │       │           │ sequence-based teaching and a separate session, such as "if you push    │
│              │                            │       │           │ so much... I can arrange I can bring someone else and I can conduct     │
│              │                            │       │           │ this topic separately," which loosely relates to a separate master      │
│              │                            │       │           │ class. However, it still misses the exact comparison the input asks     │
│              │                            │       │           │ for, since the irrelevant notes say it "does not directly address how   │
│              │                            │       │           │ sequence-based fine-tuning coverage compares with a separate industry   │
│              │                            │       │           │ master class" and "does not compare sequence-based fine-tuning          │
│              │                            │       │           │ coverage with a separate industry master class."                        │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the main preference for sequence-based learning   │
│              │                            │       │           │ and mentions a separate industry master class as a supplement, which    │
│              │                            │       │           │ aligns with the expected direction. However, it omits the key timing    │
│              │                            │       │           │ detail that sequence-based fine-tuning coverage comes later around      │
│              │                            │       │           │ December-January/end of year, and it adds unsupported specifics like    │
│              │                            │       │           │ a free YouTube master class and feedback collection. These extra        │
│              │                            │       │           │ details make it only mostly correct rather than exact.                  │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response is fairly easy to understand and student-friendly          │
│              │                            │       │           │ because it uses clear pros/cons and a simple recommendation. However,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ it is somewhat long and includes a few technical terms like "LLLMs      │
│              │                            │       │           │ are pre-trained," "fine-tuning," and "pedagogy," which makes it         │
│              │                            │       │           │ slightly less simple than a very basic explanation. It compares the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ sequence-based approach and a separate industry master class            │
│              │                            │       │           │ directly, matching the input well.                                      │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_49                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Why teach fine-tuning after transformers, pretraining, and related LLM topics?                               │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Because fine‑tuning builds on concepts that must be understood first: transformer architecture,            │
│  │                         transformer variants, and how LLMs are pre‑trained. Without those foundations you can't teach or learn       │
│  │                         fine‑tuning properly.                                                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Fine‑tuning is often misunderstood (people call it "retraining"); you need the pretraining context to      │
│  │                         see why it’s called fine‑tuning and how it differs from full retraining.                                     │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Compute considerations (both for training and for inference) become important in fine‑tuning; those        │
│  │                         implications are clearer after covering training/inference basics.                                           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Practical fine‑tuning requires knowing the model internals and the pretraining process so you can          │
│  │                         choose appropriate techniques and workflows and understand when to fine‑tune repeatedly.                     │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Sequence matters for learning: covering user‑side topics (RAG, agents, evaluation, LLM Ops) and the        │
│  │                         underlying model concepts first makes fine‑tuning teachable and useful; if demand is high, it can be run     │
│  │                         as a dedicated session.                                                                                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    Because fine-tuning is hard to understand properly without the basics first. You need to know                │
│  │                         transformers, transformer variants, and how LLMs are pretrained before fine-tuning really makes sense. If    │
│  │                         taught too early, it won’t be received well and won’t be taught well either.                                 │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is fully supported by node 1 in    │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context, which says fine-tuning is hard to teach without      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the basics and that it won’t be received or taught well if introduced   │
│              │                            │       │           │ too early. Sentence 2 is also supported by nodes 1 and 2, which state   │
│              │                            │       │           │ that transformers, transformer variants, and how LLMs are pretrained    │
│              │                            │       │           │ must come first before fine-tuning makes sense. Sentence 3 is           │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by nodes 1 and 2 as well, matching the point that the         │
│              │                            │       │           │ material must be covered in sequence and that jumping ahead makes no    │
│              │                            │       │           │ sense. Great retrieval coverage.                                        │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the relevant nodes in retrieval contexts      │
│              │                            │       │           │ are ranked first and second, and they directly explain that             │
│              │                            │       │           │ fine-tuning should come after transformers and pretraining: the first   │
│              │                            │       │           │ node supports the idea that 'if things are not covered in a sequence    │
│              │                            │       │           │ then there is no point' and that skipping ahead would make teaching     │
│              │                            │       │           │ ineffective, while the second node says you need to know                │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'transformer, transformer variants, how LLMs are pre-trained' before    │
│              │                            │       │           │ fine-tuning. The only irrelevant node is ranked third, which is         │
│              │                            │       │           │ appropriate because it merely says 'we do fine tune the model'          │
│              │                            │       │           │ without explaining why it belongs later, so it should sit below the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant nodes.                                                         │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response is fully relevant to the         │
│              │                            │       │           │ question and contains no irrelevant statements. It directly addresses   │
│              │                            │       │           │ why fine-tuning is taught after transformers and pretraining, so it     │
│              │                            │       │           │ could not reasonably be higher.                                         │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.33  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.33 because the actual output overstates the context:     │
│              │                            │       │           │ it treats fine-tuning as requiring prior training/inference and         │
│              │                            │       │           │ broader LLM foundations, and claims compute, workflow, evaluation,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and teachability benefits that the retrieval context does not           │
│              │                            │       │           │ explicitly support. It also says fine-tuning can be run as a            │
│              │                            │       │           │ dedicated session if demand is high, which the context only hints at    │
│              │                            │       │           │ by mentioning more content was requested, not that such a session was   │
│              │                            │       │           │ proposed.                                                               │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.75  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.75 because most of the context supports the              │
│              │                            │       │           │ sequence-based teaching rationale, such as “how LLMs are pre-trained    │
│              │                            │       │           │ then you get the fine tuning concept” and “if I go straight and start   │
│              │                            │       │           │ teaching you fine tuning I will not be able to teach you well,” but     │
│              │                            │       │           │ some parts are off-topic, like “a very famous topic,” “asked a lot in   │
│              │                            │       │           │ interviews,” and “30 people have asked out of 80 people,” which focus   │
│              │                            │       │           │ on popularity rather than the question.                                 │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The actual output captures the core idea that fine-tuning should come   │
│              │                            │       │           │ after understanding transformers, variants, and LLM pretraining,        │
│              │                            │       │           │ which matches the expected answer. However, it adds several             │
│              │                            │       │           │ unsupported points not present in the expected output, such as          │
│              │                            │       │           │ retraining terminology, compute considerations, RAG/agents/LLM Ops      │
│              │                            │       │           │ sequencing, and a dedicated session recommendation, making it more      │
│              │                            │       │           │ elaborate than an exact substitute.                                     │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, well-structured, and student-friendly, using     │
│              │                            │       │           │ bullet points and mostly plain language. It explains why fine-tuning    │
│              │                            │       │           │ comes after transformers and pretraining by linking it to               │
│              │                            │       │           │ prerequisite concepts, compute, and practical workflows, with only a    │
│              │                            │       │           │ few light technical terms like “RAG,” “LLM Ops,” and “inference” that   │
│              │                            │       │           │ are still understandable in context. Overall it is very simple to       │
│              │                            │       │           │ moderately simple rather than difficult.                                │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_50                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              When will fine-tuning/pre-training start vs current user-side LLM/AI engineering topics?                     │
│  │     Actual Output:      Fine-tuning/pre‑training hasn’t been scheduled yet. Right now the course is focused on user‑side LLM         │
│  │                         engineering (apps, RAG chatbots, agents, LLM Ops) and LLM evals start today (model evals covered in the      │
│  │                         next lecture). Fine‑tuning is currently not being taught because it’s harder to teach and requires deeper    │
│  │                         knowledge of transformers and pre‑training; no start date was given.                                         │
│  │     Expected Output:    Fine-tuning/pre-training will likely start in about **3–4 more months**, realistically **by around           │
│  │                         November/end of this year**.                                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Right now the focus is on **user-side LLM/AI engineering topics** like:                                      │
│  │                         - prompt engineering                                                                                         │
│  │                         - LangChain / LangGraph / MCP                                                                                │
│  │                         - RAG                                                                                                        │
│  │                         - agents                                                                                                     │
│  │                         - LLMOps / evals                                                                                             │
│  │                         - loop/context engineering                                                                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         The reason fine-tuning is delayed is that it makes more sense to teach it **after the basics and             │
│  │                         pre-reqs** like transformers, pre-training, and related concepts are covered properly.                       │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ aligns with LLM evals. Sentence 3 is also supported by node 3, which    │
│              │                            │       │           │ says fine-tuning is difficult to teach until transformer and            │
│              │                            │       │           │ pre-training basics are covered. However, sentence 1 is not             │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported, since no node in retrieval context gives the 3–4 months or   │

│              │                            │       │           │ teach" until pre-training concepts are understood. The score is not     │

│              │                            │       │           │ rag chat bots how to make agents how to evaluate them LLM Ops," but     │
