╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_51                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              What are LLM evals, plus targets, criteria, methods, and why are they repeatable?                            │
│  │     Actual Output:      What LLM evals are                                                                                           │
│  │                         - LLM evals are systematic, repeatable tests used to judge an LLM or an LLM-powered application against      │
│  │                         clear criteria. (They are tests, not just a single metric.)                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Targets                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - The evaluations are applied to an LLM itself or to systems/applications built on top of an LLM.            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Criteria                                                                                                     │
│  │                         - Evaluations judge performance against explicit, clear criteria (the context does not list specific         │
│  │                         criteria).                                                                                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Methods                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - The method is to run systematic, repeatable tests (the context does not give specific test types or        │
│  │                         metrics, only that the tests are systematic and repeatable).                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Why they are repeatable                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Repeatability is one of the main stated aspects of LLM evals: the tests are designed to be repeatable      │
│  │                         so the same evaluation procedure can be run multiple times (the context does not provide further detail      │
│  │                         on reasons beyond stating repeatability as a core property).                                                 │
│  │     Expected Output:    LLM evals are **systematic and repeatable tests** used to measure whether an LLM or LLM-based application    │
│  │                         is doing well according to a **clear definition of “good.”**                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Targets**: what you evaluate — e.g., the **model**, **components** like retriever/prompt/reranker,       │
│  │                         **workflows**, or the **final application**                                                                  │
│  │                         - **Criteria**: what aspects you judge — e.g., **quality**, **toxicity**, **bias**, **hallucination**,       │
│  │                         **latency**, **cost**, **reliability**, **drift**, etc.                                                      │
│  │                         - **Methods**: how you evaluate — **human judgment**, **automated checks**, or **LLM-as-a-judge**            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         They are **repeatable** because you can run the same evaluation setup again after making changes, and see    │
│  │                         whether the system improved or got worse.                                                                    │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is fully supported by nodes in     │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context 1 and 2 describing LLM evals as systematic,           │
│              │                            │       │           │ repeatable tests with a clear definition of good; sentence 2 is         │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by nodes 1, 2, and 3 covering targets like models,            │
│              │                            │       │           │ components, workflows, and final applications; sentence 3 is            │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by nodes 2 and 3 listing criteria such as quality,            │
│              │                            │       │           │ toxicity, bias, hallucination, latency, cost, reliability, and drift;   │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 4 is supported by nodes 2 and 3 naming methods like human      │
│              │                            │       │           │ judgment, automated checks, and LLM-as-a-judge; and sentence 5 is       │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by nodes 1, 2, and 3 all reinforcing repeatability. Nice      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and complete!                                                           │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the top three nodes in retrieval contexts     │
│              │                            │       │           │ are all relevant and consistently support the answer: the 1st node      │
│              │                            │       │           │ says "LLM evals are basically tests" against "some given clear          │
│              │                            │       │           │ criteria," the 2nd node says "LLM evals are systematic repeatable       │
│              │                            │       │           │ tests," and the 3rd node says they are "systematic and repeatable       │
│              │                            │       │           │ tests" used to measure performance. Since there are no irrelevant       │
│              │                            │       │           │ nodes ranked above relevant ones, the relevant nodes are already        │
│              │                            │       │           │ perfectly prioritized, which is why the score is at its maximum.        │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response is fully relevant to the input   │
│              │                            │       │           │ and contains no irrelevant statements. It directly addresses LLM        │
│              │                            │       │           │ evals, targets, criteria, methods, and repeatability in a clear,        │
│              │                            │       │           │ focused way.                                                            │

│              │                            │       │           │ core definition: "LLM evals are basically tests" and "systematic        │
│              │                            │       │           │ repeatable tests used to judge an LLM or LLM powered system against a   │
│              │                            │       │           │ clear criteria." However, it is only partially complete for the full    │
│              │                            │       │           │ query since the irrelevant notes say it "does not specify the           │

│              │                            │       │           │ repeatable tests and correctly covers targets, criteria, and            │

│              │                            │       │           │ toxicity, bias, hallucination, latency, cost, reliability, drift,       │

│              │                            │       │           │ criteria, methods, and repeatability. It uses very little jargon and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ directly answers the input in an easy-to-understand way, though terms   │
│              │                            │       │           │ like "systematic" and "LLM-powered application" are slightly            │
│              │                            │       │           │ technical.                                                              │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_52                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Define LLM evals; include systematic, repeatable, clear-good, plus JSON keys.                                │
│  │     Actual Output:      LLM evals: systematic, repeatable tests used to judge an LLM or LLM-powered system against a clear           │
│  │                         definition of "good" — the entire testing setup (what is tested, on what basis, when, and with what          │
│  │                         tools), not just metrics.                                                                                    │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         JSON keys (example structure; values describe expected content types):                                       │
│  │                         {                                                                                                            │
│  │                           "id": "string - unique evaluation identifier",                                                             │
│  │                           "name": "string - short evaluation name",                                                                  │
│  │                           "description": "string - brief purpose and scope",                                                         │
│  │                           "target_system": "string - LLM or LLM-based application being tested",                                     │
│  │                           "target_components": ["string"] - "list of components (API, prompt templates, UI, etc.)",                  │
│  │                           "use_case": "string - business use case or scenario driving the eval",                                     │
│  │                           "systematic": true,                                                                                        │
│  │                           "repeatable": true,                                                                                        │
│  │                           "clear_definition_of_good": "string or object - explicit pass/fail criteria or rubric",                    │
│  │                           "criteria": {"metric_name": "threshold or rubric description"},                                            │
│  │                           "metrics": ["string"] - "names of metrics collected",                                                      │
│  │                           "test_inputs": ["string or object"] - "datasets, prompts, or input cases used",                            │
│  │                           "test_procedure": "string or link - step-by-step procedure to run the test",                               │
│  │                           "schedule": "string - when to run (e.g., on release, nightly, on-demand)",                                 │
│  │                           "tooling": ["string"] - "tools or frameworks used for evaluation",                                         │
│  │                           "results": {"summary": "string", "raw": "object or link to raw outputs"},                                  │
│  │                           "reproducibility_notes": "string - how to reproduce the run",                                              │
│  │                           "version": "string - eval spec or test suite version",                                                     │
│  │                           "notes": "string - additional context or next steps"                                                       │
│  │                         }                                                                                                            │
│  │     Expected Output:    LLM evals are **systematic and repeatable tests** used to measure whether an LLM or LLM-based application    │
│  │                         is performing well according to a **clear definition of “good.”**                                            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         For a **JSON output** use case, the keys can be defined by the business goal, for example:                   │
│  │                         - `accuracy`                                                                                                 │
│  │                         - `format_validity`                                                                                          │
│  │                         - `completeness`                                                                                             │
│  │                         - `consistency`                                                                                              │
│  │                         - `safety`                                                                                                   │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ node 2 in retrieval context, which describe LLM evals as systematic     │
│              │                            │       │           │ and repeatable tests/ways to measure performance against a clear        │
│              │                            │       │           │ definition of good; sentence 2 is also fully supported by node 2,       │
│              │                            │       │           │ which lists the example JSON keys accuracy, format_validity,            │
│              │                            │       │           │ completeness, consistency, and safety.                                  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the top three nodes in retrieval contexts     │
│              │                            │       │           │ are all relevant and correctly ranked first. Node 1 explicitly says     │
│              │                            │       │           │ LLM evals are "systematic repeatable tests" with a "clear criteria,"    │
│              │                            │       │           │ Node 2 reinforces "systematic, repeatable way" and "There is a clear    │
│              │                            │       │           │ definition of good," and Node 3 further supports the concept as the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ broader testing setup. There are no irrelevant nodes placed above       │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant ones, so the ranking is already ideal.                         │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addressed the request,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ defining LLM evals and covering the requested qualities and JSON keys   │
│              │                            │       │           │ without any irrelevant content.                                         │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.04  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.04 because the actual output invents a detailed JSON     │
│              │                            │       │           │ eval specification with fields like id, name, description,              │
│              │                            │       │           │ target_system, target_components, use_case, criteria, metrics,          │
│              │                            │       │           │ test_inputs, test_procedure, schedule, tooling, results,                │
│              │                            │       │           │ reproducibility_notes, version, and notes, plus a final "JSON:"         │
│              │                            │       │           │ example structure, none of which appear in the retrieval context; it    │
│              │                            │       │           │ also claims a clear definition of "good" and criteria thresholds that   │
│              │                            │       │           │ the context does not provide.                                           │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.68  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.68 because the context includes strong relevant          │
│              │                            │       │           │ definitions like "LLM evals are systematic repeatable tests" and        │
│              │                            │       │           │ "Used to judge an LLM or LLM powered system against a clear             │
│              │                            │       │           │ criteria," which match the request for "systematic, repeatable,         │
│              │                            │       │           │ clear-good," but it also contains a lot of non-definitional chatter     │
│              │                            │       │           │ such as "I will first read the definition and then we will discuss      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the point" and "We will make our own custom evaluation pipeline in      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the future," so the fit is only partial.                                │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the core idea that LLM evals are systematic,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ repeatable tests with a clear definition of good, which matches the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ first part of the expected output. However, it diverges from the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ expected JSON-output guidance: instead of listing                       │
│              │                            │       │           │ business-goal-driven keys like accuracy, format_validity,               │
│              │                            │       │           │ completeness, consistency, and safety, it provides a much broader       │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation-spec template with many extra fields and omits those         │
│              │                            │       │           │ specific example keys. This is a meaningful mismatch in the requested   │
│              │                            │       │           │ details.                                                                │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear and student-friendly: it defines LLM evals as     │
│              │                            │       │           │ systematic and repeatable tests with a clear definition of good,        │
│              │                            │       │           │ matching the input. It also includes many JSON keys and example         │
│              │                            │       │           │ values, which satisfies the request, though the long code-like list     │
│              │                            │       │           │ adds some complexity and technical jargon that slightly reduces         │
│              │                            │       │           │ simplicity.                                                             │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_53                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Why is a clear “good” definition crucial for metrics and business use cases?                                 │
│  │     Actual Output:      - A clear “good” definition gives an objective, repeatable target to aim for — you know exactly what to      │
│  │                         measure and when something counts as good.                                                                   │
│  │                         - It makes evaluation reproducible: metrics become reproducible evidence you can execute and compare over    │
│  │                         time instead of doing things randomly.                                                                       │
│  │                         - It ties the metric to the business/application goal: since metrics are application-specific (and you       │
│  │                         can build custom ones), a precise definition ensures the metric actually captures the business value you     │
│  │                         care about.                                                                                                  │
│  │                         - It prevents confusion and inconsistent decisions: you won’t arbitrarily switch measures (e.g.,             │
│  │                         “correctness” vs “accuracy”) or judge things subjectively.                                                   │
│  │                         - It enables actionable requirements and thresholds: you can say “this chart board is good if it scores X    │
│  │                         on simplicity of explanation, provides example patterns Y times, and shows humor at level Z,” making         │
│  │                         acceptance and improvement measurable.                                                                       │
│  │     Expected Output:    A clear “good” definition is crucial because it tells you exactly what the system should optimize for in     │
│  │                         your specific business use case. Without it, you can’t choose the right metrics or judge whether the LLM     │
│  │                         is actually performing well.                                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         For example, in a CampusX chatbot, “good” might mean:                                                        │
│  │                         - simple explanations                                                                                        │
│  │                         - giving examples                                                                                            │
│  │                         - being humorous                                                                                             │
│  │                         - never being rude or abusive                                                                                │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         These become the basis for evaluation metrics, so the system can be tested systematically and repeatedly     │
│  │                         against what your business actually wants.                                                                   │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 0.91  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.91 because sentence 2 and most of sentence 3 are well    │
│              │                            │       │           │ grounded in node 3 in retrieval context, which mentions simple          │
│              │                            │       │           │ language, examples, and humor as criteria for “good,” and sentence 4    │
│              │                            │       │           │ is supported by node 1-2 via the idea of custom metrics, objectives,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ and a reproducible workflow. Sentence 1 is only partially supported     │
│              │                            │       │           │ by nodes 1-2, since they say metrics depend on the use case and         │
│              │                            │       │           │ should be well defined, but no node explicitly states that a clear      │
│              │                            │       │           │ “good” definition is crucial or that it tells you exactly what to       │
│              │                            │       │           │ optimize for, so a small part of the output remains unattributed.       │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the first three retrieval context nodes are   │
│              │                            │       │           │ all relevant and well ordered: node 1 supports that metrics are         │
│              │                            │       │           │ application-specific with "it depends on your application" and "you     │
│              │                            │       │           │ also build custom metrics ... according to your use case," node 2       │
│              │                            │       │           │ reinforces the need for an objective and something "well defined,"      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and node 3 gives concrete examples of what "good" means, like           │
│              │                            │       │           │ "simplicity of explanation," "giving examples," and "be humorous."      │
│              │                            │       │           │ There are no irrelevant nodes ranked above relevant ones, so the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ ordering is already ideal.                                              │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addresses why a clear      │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'good' definition matters for metrics and business use cases, with no   │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant content to reduce relevance. It is at the maximum score      │
│              │                            │       │           │ because everything provided stays directly on topic.                    │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.80 because the actual output adds ambiguity about        │
│              │                            │       │           │ metric selection and suggests confusion or inconsistent choices,        │
│              │                            │       │           │ while the context only says the metrics are clearly defined by an       │
│              │                            │       │           │ objective and methodology and does not mention those specific issues.   │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.96  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.96 because the context strongly supports why a clear     │
│              │                            │       │           │ “good” definition matters: it says metrics are application-specific,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ that “What is the criteria What is the methodology All this is well     │
│              │                            │       │           │ defined,” and that metrics provide “a reproducible workflow” rather     │
│              │                            │       │           │ than being “anything randomly.” The only irrelevant bit is the side     │
│              │                            │       │           │ remark, “is it clear? is there any issue guys? am I audible?,” which    │
│              │                            │       │           │ doesn’t affect the otherwise highly relevant explanation.               │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the core idea that defining "good" makes          │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation objective, repeatable, and tied to business/application      │
│              │                            │       │           │ goals. It also correctly mentions measurable criteria like              │
│              │                            │       │           │ simplicity, examples, and humor, which aligns with the CampusX          │
│              │                            │       │           │ chatbot example. However, it omits the explicit point that without      │
│              │                            │       │           │ such a definition you cannot choose the right metrics or judge          │
│              │                            │       │           │ whether the LLM is actually performing well, and it adds some extra     │
│              │                            │       │           │ phrasing not present in the expected output.                            │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, structured, and easy for a student to follow,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ with mostly plain language and few technical terms. It directly         │
│              │                            │       │           │ answers why a clear definition of “good” matters for metrics and        │
│              │                            │       │           │ business use cases by emphasizing objective measurement,                │
│              │                            │       │           │ reproducibility, business alignment, and consistent decisions. The      │
│              │                            │       │           │ example about thresholds is a bit oddly specific, but overall the       │
│              │                            │       │           │ explanation stays simple and understandable.                            │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_54                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How do probabilism and multi-criteria correctness make LLM evals tricky?                                     │
│  │     Actual Output:      Because LLMs are probabilistic and correctness is multi‑faceted, evaluation becomes much less                │
│  │                         straightforward than in traditional ML:                                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Probabilism / non‑determinism: the same input can produce different outputs, so you can’t rely on a        │
│  │                         single run or deterministic metrics. That variability breaks simple, repeatable comparisons and demands      │
│  │                         sampling, aggregation, or other methods to judge system behavior over multiple generations.                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Multi‑criteria correctness: there is no single definition of “right” — outputs are semantically            │
│  │                         flexible (not exact strings) and multiple aspects matter. You must decide what matters, build rubrics,       │
│  │                         and use human judgment to produce detailed textual reports.                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Together these mean LLM evaluation is subjective, costly, and requires bespoke rubrics and careful human     │
│  │                         evaluation, unlike traditional ML where automated mathematical metrics are cheap and deterministic.          │
│  │     Expected Output:    LLM evals are tricky because:                                                                                │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Probabilism:** the same input can produce different outputs each time, and all may be correct, so        │
│  │                         evaluation isn’t straightforward.                                                                            │
│  │                         - **Multi-criteria correctness:** you can’t check only “is it right?” You also need to check things like     │
│  │                         **safety, latency, cost, grounding in retrieved documents, simplicity, and style**.                          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So unlike software testing, LLM evaluation needs **systematic, repeatable tests with clear criteria**        │
│  │                         across multiple dimensions.                                                                                  │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is supported by node 1, which      │
│              │                            │       │           │ says LLM outputs can vary for the same input, and sentence 2 is         │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by nodes 1 and 3, which note there isn’t a single             │
│              │                            │       │           │ definition of correctness and that multiple factors must be             │
│              │                            │       │           │ considered. Sentence 3 is also fully grounded by nodes 1 and 2, which   │
│              │                            │       │           │ point to using clear rubrics and proper judgment for systematic,        │
│              │                            │       │           │ repeatable evaluation.                                                  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the retrieval contexts are perfectly          │
│              │                            │       │           │ ordered: the first node, second node, and third node all directly       │
│              │                            │       │           │ address the input with no irrelevant nodes ahead of them. The first     │
│              │                            │       │           │ node in retrieval contexts says LLM systems are probabilistic and       │
│              │                            │       │           │ that the 'Same input might give you different different output,' the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ second node says 'there is no single definition of correctness' and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'You have to look at multiple things,' and the third node adds that     │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'There is no single Correct answer' and outputs are 'Semantically       │
│              │                            │       │           │ flexible.' Since every node in retrieval contexts is relevant and       │
│              │                            │       │           │ already ranked in the right order, there is no need to move any         │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant nodes lower.                                                 │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addresses the question     │
│              │                            │       │           │ with no irrelevant statements, clearly explaining why probabilism and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ multi-criteria correctness make LLM evaluations difficult. It cannot    │
│              │                            │       │           │ be higher since 1.00 is already the maximum.                            │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.31  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.31 because the actual output adds several claims not     │
│              │                            │       │           │ explicitly supported by the context: that LLM evaluation is much less   │
│              │                            │       │           │ straightforward than traditional ML, that a single run or               │
│              │                            │       │           │ deterministic metrics are insufficient, that repeated                   │
│              │                            │       │           │ sampling/aggregation is needed, that outputs are semantically           │
│              │                            │       │           │ flexible rather than exact strings, and that evaluation must rely on    │
│              │                            │       │           │ bespoke rubrics or human judgment. It also implies                      │
│              │                            │       │           │ subjectivity/costliness and that multiple aspects matter in ways the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ context does not state.                                                 │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.85  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.85 because the context directly explains that LLM        │
│              │                            │       │           │ evals are hard since “LLM based systems are probabilistic” and “there   │
│              │                            │       │           │ is no single definition of correctness,” with “multiple things” to      │
│              │                            │       │           │ consider. However, some of the retrieved text is still off-topic,       │
│              │                            │       │           │ e.g. it focuses on “evaluate the applications properly” and creating    │
│              │                            │       │           │ “our own rubrics,” which does not directly answer the question.         │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the two main ideas from the expected output:      │
│              │                            │       │           │ probabilistic/non-deterministic outputs and multi-criteria              │
│              │                            │       │           │ correctness. However, it adds several unsupported points such as        │
│              │                            │       │           │ detailed claims about sampling, aggregation, subjective/costly          │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation, and contrasts with traditional ML that are not in the       │
│              │                            │       │           │ expected answer. It also misses key expected criteria like safety,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ latency, cost, grounding in retrieved documents, simplicity, and        │
│              │                            │       │           │ style, and does not explicitly state the need for systematic,           │
│              │                            │       │           │ repeatable tests across multiple dimensions.                            │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear and well structured, but it uses several          │
│              │                            │       │           │ technical terms like "non-determinism," "aggregation," "semantic        │
│              │                            │       │           │ flexibility," and "bespoke rubrics," which makes it a bit less          │
│              │                            │       │           │ student-friendly than very simple language. It does address the key     │
│              │                            │       │           │ ideas from the input—probabilistic outputs and multiple correctness     │
│              │                            │       │           │ criteria—but the explanation is more advanced than the easiest level.   │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_55                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              JSON schema for LLM evals: repeatability, criteria, system type?                                             │
│  │     Actual Output:      {                                                                                                            │
│  │                           "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",                                                      │
│  │                           "title": "LLM Evaluation Schema",                                                                          │
│  │                           "type": "object",                                                                                          │
│  │                           "properties": {                                                                                            │
│  │                             "id": {                                                                                                  │
│  │                               "type": "string",                                                                                      │
│  │                               "description": "Unique identifier for this eval/test suite."                                           │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "name": {                                                                                                │
│  │                               "type": "string",                                                                                      │
│  │                               "description": "Human-readable name for the eval."                                                     │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "description": {                                                                                         │
│  │                               "type": "string",                                                                                      │
│  │                               "description": "Short description of what this eval measures."                                         │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "repeatable": {                                                                                          │
│  │                               "type": "boolean",                                                                                     │
│  │                               "description": "Whether the evaluation is designed to be repeatable."                                  │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "repeatability_details": {                                                                               │
│  │                               "type": "object",                                                                                      │
│  │                               "properties": {                                                                                        │
│  │                                 "automation": {                                                                                      │
│  │                                   "type": "string",                                                                                  │
│  │                                   "enum": ["manual", "automated", "scheduled"],                                                      │
│  │                                   "description": "How the test is executed to ensure repeatability."                                 │
│  │                                 },                                                                                                   │
│  │                                 "seed_control": {                                                                                    │
│  │                                   "type": "boolean",                                                                                 │
│  │                                   "description": "Whether random seeds or deterministic settings are controlled to enable            │
│  │                         reproducibility."                                                                                            │
│  │                                 },                                                                                                   │
│  │                                 "when_run": {                                                                                        │
│  │                                   "type": "string",                                                                                  │
│  │                                   "enum": ["offline", "production", "continuous"],                                                   │
│  │                                   "description": "When these tests are run (e.g., offline during development or after deployment     │
│  │                         in production)."                                                                                             │
│  │                                 }                                                                                                    │
│  │                               },                                                                                                     │
│  │                               "additionalProperties": false                                                                          │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "systematic": {                                                                                          │
│  │                               "type": "boolean",                                                                                     │
│  │                               "description": "Whether the evaluation follows a systematic approach (not ad-hoc 'vibe' testing)."     │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "dataset": {                                                                                             │
│  │                               "type": "object",                                                                                      │
│  │                               "description": "Dataset(s) used to run the eval; systematic evals use prepared datasets.",             │
│  │                               "properties": {                                                                                        │
│  │                                 "dataset_id": { "type": "string" },                                                                  │
│  │                                 "description": { "type": "string" },                                                                 │
│  │                                 "schema_reference": { "type": "string" },                                                            │
│  │                                 "usage_notes": { "type": "string" }                                                                  │
│  │                               },                                                                                                     │
│  │                               "required": ["dataset_id"],                                                                            │
│  │                               "additionalProperties": false                                                                          │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "criteria": {                                                                                            │
│  │                               "type": "array",                                                                                       │
│  │                               "description": "Clear criteria by which the system is evaluated.",                                     │
│  │                               "items": {                                                                                             │
│  │                                 "type": "object",                                                                                    │
│  │                                 "properties": {                                                                                      │
│  │                                   "id": { "type": "string" },                                                                        │
│  │                                   "name": { "type": "string" },                                                                      │
│  │                                   "description": { "type": "string" },                                                               │
│  │                                   "type": {                                                                                          │
│  │                                     "type": "string",                                                                                │
│  │                                     "enum": ["accuracy", "robustness", "latency", "safety", "other"]                                 │
│  │                                   },                                                                                                 │
│  │                                   "metric": {                                                                                        │
│  │                                     "type": "object",                                                                                │
│  │                                     "properties": {                                                                                  │
│  │                                       "metric_name": { "type": "string" },                                                           │
│  │                                       "target_component": {                                                                          │
│  │                                         "type": "string",                                                                            │
│  │                                         "enum": ["retriever", "generator", "ranker", "end-to-end", "other"],                         │
│  │                                         "description": "Which part of the system the criterion targets (e.g., accuracy of the        │
│  │                         retriever)."                                                                                                 │
│  │                                       },                                                                                             │
│  │                                       "threshold": { "type": ["number", "string"], "description": "Pass/fail threshold or target     │
│  │                         value." },                                                                                                   │
│  │                                       "scoring_method": { "type": "string", "description": "How the metric is computed." }           │
│  │                                     },                                                                                               │
│  │                                     "required": ["metric_name", "target_component"],                                                 │
│  │                                     "additionalProperties": false                                                                    │
│  │                                   }                                                                                                  │
│  │                                 },                                                                                                   │
│  │                                 "required": ["id", "name", "metric"],                                                                │
│  │                                 "additionalProperties": false                                                                        │
│  │                               }                                                                                                      │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "system_type": {                                                                                         │
│  │                               "type": "string",                                                                                      │
│  │                               "enum": ["model-only", "llm-application", "rag-application", "chatbot", "other"],                      │
│  │                               "description": "Type of system under test (example: RAG application)."                                 │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "tools": {                                                                                               │
│  │                               "type": "array",                                                                                       │
│  │                               "description": "Tools or frameworks used in the evaluation (e.g., RAG toolchain).",                    │
│  │                               "items": { "type": "string" }                                                                          │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "run_configuration": {                                                                                   │
│  │                               "type": "object",                                                                                      │
│  │                               "properties": {                                                                                        │
│  │                                 "environment": {                                                                                     │
│  │                                   "type": "string",                                                                                  │
│  │                                   "enum": ["local", "staging", "production"],                                                        │
│  │                                   "description": "Execution environment used for the run."                                           │
│  │                                 },                                                                                                   │
│  │                                 "versioned_artifacts": {                                                                             │
│  │                                   "type": "array",                                                                                   │
│  │                                   "items": { "type": "string" },                                                                     │
│  │                                   "description": "Versions of model, datasets, or tooling used to ensure repeatability."             │
│  │                                 }                                                                                                    │
│  │                               },                                                                                                     │
│  │                               "additionalProperties": false                                                                          │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "notes": {                                                                                               │
│  │                               "type": "string",                                                                                      │
│  │                               "description": "Any clarifications (for example: eval is a test suite, not just a metric)."            │
│  │                             }                                                                                                        │
│  │                           },                                                                                                         │
│  │                           "required": ["id", "name", "repeatable", "systematic", "dataset", "criteria", "system_type"],              │
│  │                           "additionalProperties": false                                                                              │
│  │                         }                                                                                                            │
│  │     Expected Output:    LLM evals are **systematic, repeatable tests** used to judge an **LLM or LLM-powered application**           │
│  │                         against **clear criteria**.                                                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Repeatability:** the same test set can be run again even if the prompt, model, retriever, or chunking    │
│  │                         changes.                                                                                                     │
│  │                         - **Criteria:** evaluation is based on specific factors like correctness, safety, grounding, latency,        │
│  │                         cost, and explanation style.                                                                                 │
│  │                         - **System type:** they can be used for both **model evals** and **application evals**.                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So, LLM evals are not just metrics—they are the **full testing setup**.                                      │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 0.75  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.75 because sentence 1 is supported by nodes in           │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context 1 and 3, which describe LLM evals as systematic,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ repeatable tests with clear criteria; sentence 2 is supported by node   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 2, which gives concrete evaluation factors like                         │
│              │                            │       │           │ accuracy/grounding/latency/cost; and sentence 4 is supported by node    │
│              │                            │       │           │ 3, which frames LLM evals as the full testing setup. Sentence 3 is      │
│              │                            │       │           │ not supported, since no node explicitly states the                      │
│              │                            │       │           │ model-vs-application distinction.                                       │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the top 3 nodes in retrieval contexts are     │
│              │                            │       │           │ all relevant and well-ranked: node 1 says LLM evals are "very           │
│              │                            │       │           │ important characteristics" and that "these tests are repeatable" with   │
│              │                            │       │           │ "very clear criteria," node 2 asks, "Based on which criteria are we     │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluating it?" and supports criteria-focused evaluation, and node 3    │
│              │                            │       │           │ states "LLM evals are basically tests" for "LLM or LLM based            │
│              │                            │       │           │ applications" and are "repeatable and very systematic." Since there     │
│              │                            │       │           │ are no irrelevant nodes ranked above relevant ones, the score is        │
│              │                            │       │           │ perfect.                                                                │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addresses the request      │
│              │                            │       │           │ without any irrelevant statements, directly covering the JSON schema    │
│              │                            │       │           │ for LLM evals and the requested fields.                                 │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.09  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.09 because the actual output invents a detailed JSON     │
│              │                            │       │           │ schema with a title, draft version, required top-level object           │
│              │                            │       │           │ properties, repeatability_details, dataset, criteria, metric,           │
│              │                            │       │           │ system_type, tools, run_configuration, versioned_artifacts, and notes   │
│              │                            │       │           │ fields, none of which are explicitly described in the retrieval         │
│              │                            │       │           │ context. The context only gives high-level evaluation setup hints, so   │
│              │                            │       │           │ these specific field names, structures, and allowed values are          │
│              │                            │       │           │ unsupported.                                                            │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.71  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.71 because much of the context directly matches the      │
│              │                            │       │           │ query, including that LLM evals are "repeatable," have "very clear      │
│              │                            │       │           │ criteria," and are "very systematic," with details like "Based on       │
│              │                            │       │           │ which criteria are we evaluating it?" and "Are we running it offline    │
│              │                            │       │           │ or after being deployed in production?" However, some retrieved lines   │
│              │                            │       │           │ are clearly off-topic, such as "You got five questions in your mind"    │
│              │                            │       │           │ and "I used to think that eval means metric," which lowers the          │
│              │                            │       │           │ overall relevance.                                                      │
│        FAIL  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.10  │ 0.50      │ The actual output does not answer the prompt’s definition of LLM        │
│              │                            │       │           │ evals; it instead returns an unrelated JSON Schema describing an        │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation object. It misses the expected points that LLM evals are     │
│              │                            │       │           │ systematic, repeatable tests with clear criteria, and that they apply   │
│              │                            │       │           │ to both model and application evals, while also introducing             │
│              │                            │       │           │ unsupported schema fields and structure.                                │
│        FAIL  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.30  │ 0.50      │ The output is a detailed JSON schema rather than a simple,              │
│              │                            │       │           │ student-friendly answer to the prompt. It uses many technical schema    │
│              │                            │       │           │ terms like "$schema", "additionalProperties", "enum", and nested        │
│              │                            │       │           │ objects, which makes it harder to understand, even though it does       │
│              │                            │       │           │ address repeatability, criteria, and system type.                       │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_56                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How do model vs app evals differ in purpose, scope, criteria, setup, and practical questions?                │
│  │     Actual Output:      Short answer: model evals ask “can the model do this?”; application (app) evals ask “will my product         │
│  │                         built with the model work properly?” Note: these two labels aren’t official — in industry both are often     │
│  │                         called LLM evals and people infer model vs app from the use case.                                            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Purpose                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Model evals: judge the model’s capabilities (what the LLM itself can or cannot do).                        │
│  │                         - App evals: judge whether the LLM-based product or pipeline will meet the user / business needs.            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Scope                                                                                                        │
│  │                         - Model evals: focus on the model only.                                                                      │
│  │                         - App evals: cover the whole application stack (retriever, embedding model, re-ranker, prompt/chain          │
│  │                         logic, UI/UX, etc.) and how components interact for the target use case.                                     │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Criteria                                                                                                     │
│  │                         - Model evals: capability-driven criteria (does the model produce correct answers, follow instructions,      │
│  │                         reason, etc. — i.e., can it do the task).                                                                    │
│  │                         - App evals: product-driven criteria (does the system return relevant results, meet                          │
│  │                         latency/robustness/accuracy needs for the user scenario, satisfy end-to-end success metrics).                │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Setup                                                                                                        │
│  │                         - Model evals: simpler, centered on test prompts/tasks run directly against the LLM.                         │
│  │                         - App evals: more complex; require assembling and testing full pipelines, datasets, and integrations         │
│  │                         (retrieval, embeddings, re-ranking, orchestration, UI), and measuring end-to-end behavior for the target     │
│  │                         application.                                                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Practical questions each answers                                                                             │
│  │                         - Model evals: “Can the model perform X task?” (capability-focused).                                         │
│  │                         - App evals: “Will our product work properly?” Examples from the context: “Is my retriever working           │
│  │                         properly? Is my embedding model working properly? Is my re-ranker working properly?” or “Will the chart      │
│  │                         board for CampusX behave correctly for users?”                                                               │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         That captures the distinction: model evals test model ability; app evals test whether the assembled          │
│  │                         product meets requirements in its real use case.                                                             │
│  │     Expected Output:    Model evals focus on testing the LLM itself, while app evals focus on testing the full LLM-powered           │
│  │                         application.                                                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Purpose:**                                                                                               │
│  │                           - **Model evals:** Judge the model’s capabilities.                                                         │
│  │                           - **App evals:** Judge whether the whole system works for the use case.                                    │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Scope:**                                                                                                 │
│  │                           - **Model evals:** The LLM only.                                                                           │
│  │                           - **App evals:** Retriever, prompt, safety, latency, grounding, cost, and output quality.                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Criteria:**                                                                                              │
│  │                           - **Model evals:** Things like accuracy or task performance.                                               │
