│  │                           - **App evals:** Clear criteria like correctness, groundedness, safety, simplicity, explanation style,     │
│  │                         and latency.                                                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Setup:**                                                                                                 │
│  │                           - **Model evals:** Test the model on a dataset.                                                            │
│  │                           - **App evals:** Test the complete system with datasets, rubrics, and tools for the full workflow.         │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Practical questions:**                                                                                   │
│  │                           - **Model evals:** “How good is the model?”                                                                │
│  │                           - **App evals:** “Can we ship this?”, “Is it safe?”, “Is the answer from retrieved context?”, “Did the     │
│  │                         new prompt improve results?”                                                                                 │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because every sentence is fully supported by          │
│              │                            │       │           │ node(s) in retrieval context: sentence 1 is directly contrasted in      │
│              │                            │       │           │ nodes 1 and 3, sentence 2 is supported by nodes 2 and 3 on purpose,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 3 by node 2 on scope and node 3 on broader criteria,           │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 4 by nodes 1 and 3 on setup, and sentence 5 by node 2 on       │
│              │                            │       │           │ practical questions. Great coverage overall.                            │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the relevant nodes in retrieval contexts      │
│              │                            │       │           │ are ranked first through third, and each one directly supports the      │
│              │                            │       │           │ input: node 1 says 'model evals work is very simple. Given,             │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluating LLM' versus 'application evals is to evaluate LLM based      │
│              │                            │       │           │ applications,' node 2 adds app-eval scope with 'Whether my retriever    │
│              │                            │       │           │ is working properly or not' and 'whether our product will work          │
│              │                            │       │           │ properly or not,' and node 3 reinforces the same distinction. There     │
│              │                            │       │           │ are no irrelevant nodes placed above relevant ones, so nothing needs    │
│              │                            │       │           │ to be ranked lower, which is why the score is already perfect.          │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response is fully relevant to the         │
│              │                            │       │           │ question and contains no irrelevant statements; it directly addresses   │
│              │                            │       │           │ the differences between model and app evals.                            │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.67  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.67 because the actual output adds specific app-eval      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and model-eval details that the context does not explicitly state,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ such as retrievers, embedding models, re-rankers, prompt/chain logic,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ UI/UX, component interactions, and capability-driven criteria like      │
│              │                            │       │           │ correct answers, instruction following, reasoning, and task             │
│              │                            │       │           │ performance. It also introduces product-style metrics like relevance,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ latency, robustness, accuracy, end-to-end success, and CampusX chart    │
│              │                            │       │           │ board examples that are not in the retrieval context.                   │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.67  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.67 because the context does include some directly        │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant distinctions like 'This is the work of model evaluation' and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'in application eval, we don't ask can the model do this' versus 'we    │
│              │                            │       │           │ ask ... whether our product will work properly or not,' but it is       │
│              │                            │       │           │ weakened by irrelevant/meta content such as 'I have created these.'     │
│              │                            │       │           │ and 'This is a disclaimer,' which do not explain the requested          │
│              │                            │       │           │ differences in purpose, scope, criteria, setup, or practical            │
│              │                            │       │           │ questions.                                                              │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the core distinction between model evals and      │
│              │                            │       │           │ app evals: model-only capability testing versus full product/pipeline   │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation. It matches most categories in the expected answer,          │
│              │                            │       │           │ including purpose, scope, criteria, setup, and practical questions.     │
│              │                            │       │           │ Minor differences are present, such as extra examples and adding a      │
│              │                            │       │           │ note that the labels aren’t official, but there are no factual          │
│              │                            │       │           │ contradictions or missing key elements.                                 │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, student-friendly, and uses simple language to    │
│              │                            │       │           │ distinguish model evals from app evals across purpose, scope,           │
│              │                            │       │           │ criteria, setup, and practical questions. It stays easy to understand   │
│              │                            │       │           │ with short bullets and only a few necessary technical terms like        │
│              │                            │       │           │ retriever and re-ranker, which are explained by context.                │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_57                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Contrast LLM lifecycle vs LLM-based apps in needs, costs, and course focus.                                  │
│  │     Actual Output:      Needs                                                                                                        │
│  │                         - Traditional ML (lifecycles/evaluation): evaluation is done through mathematical formulas — cheap,          │
│  │                         automated metrics suffice.                                                                                   │
│  │                         - LLM-based apps: require human judgment and qualitative assessment; you need detailed reports on            │
│  │                         generated text and evaluation beyond simple metrics. LLM is a distinct component focused on generation,      │
│  │                         so evaluation must address generation quality, usability, and real-world behavior.                           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Costs                                                                                                        │
│  │                         - Traditional ML: evaluation is inexpensive and automatable.                                                 │
│  │                         - LLM-based apps: evaluation is costly because it depends on human judgment and detailed text-level          │
│  │                         reports; outputs are probabilistic and non-deterministic, increasing evaluation effort and cost.             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Course focus                                                                                                 │
│  │                         - Traditional ML topics emphasize automated, formula-driven evaluation methods.                              │
│  │                         - This course focuses on LLM evals — how to know if an LLM-based application is doing a good job, ready      │
│  │                         to launch, and usable by users; it covers the key complexities that make LLM app evaluation tricky and       │
│  │                         why many avoid studying it. Viewing the course end-to-end gives an edge over peers.                          │
│  │     Expected Output:    The **LLM lifecycle** focuses on training or fine-tuning models and needs **massive data, compute,           │
│  │                         infrastructure, technical expertise, and high cost**.                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         The **LLM-based apps** side focuses on building applications like **RAG** and **Agentic apps**, which is     │
│  │                         the **course focus** here because it is more practical and manageable.                                       │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 0.50  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.50 because sentence 2 is partly supported by node 1 in   │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context, which says the course is about evaluating            │
│              │                            │       │           │ LLM-based applications and aligns with the focus on practical apps      │
│              │                            │       │           │ like RAG and Agentic apps; however, sentence 1 is not supported by      │
│              │                            │       │           │ nodes 1-3 in retrieval context, since they discuss LLM evals and        │
│              │                            │       │           │ LLM-based applications but do not mention the LLM lifecycle,            │
│              │                            │       │           │ training/fine-tuning, massive data, compute, infrastructure,            │
│              │                            │       │           │ technical expertise, or high cost.                                      │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the retrieval contexts are all relevant       │
│              │                            │       │           │ nodes and they are already ranked well: the 1st node directly says      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the course will cover 'evaluating LLM based applications,' aligning     │
│              │                            │       │           │ with the course focus; the 2nd node reinforces why this matters by      │
│              │                            │       │           │ noting it is 'not straightforward' and 'very very tricky'; and the      │
│              │                            │       │           │ 3rd node adds that LLM-based applications need 'proper judgment' and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ are 'costly,' which supports the needs and costs contrast. Since        │
│              │                            │       │           │ there are no irrelevant nodes placed above relevant ones, the ranking   │
│              │                            │       │           │ is ideal.                                                               │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 0.81  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.81 because the response mostly addresses the requested   │
│              │                            │       │           │ contrast, but it includes some off-topic material about traditional     │
│              │                            │       │           │ ML evaluation and course-benefit claims that don’t directly compare     │
│              │                            │       │           │ LLM lifecycle vs. LLM-based apps in needs, costs, and course focus.     │
│              │                            │       │           │ It is still fairly relevant because the main discussion stays on the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ requested comparison overall.                                           │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.50  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.50 because the actual output adds claims not supported   │
│              │                            │       │           │ by the context, such as saying traditional ML evaluation uses           │
│              │                            │       │           │ mathematical formulas and that formulas are sufficient. It also goes    │
│              │                            │       │           │ beyond the context by describing detailed reports on generated text,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ generation quality, usability, real-world behavior, and why people      │
│              │                            │       │           │ avoid studying LLM app evaluation, even though the context only says    │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation is tricky, costly, and requires human judgment.              │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.82  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.82 because the context is strongly related to            │
│              │                            │       │           │ LLM-based applications, including that “Evaluating LLM based            │
│              │                            │       │           │ applications is not straightforward” and “Evaluation is cheap and       │
│              │                            │       │           │ automated” in traditional ML but “costly” and needing “Proper           │
│              │                            │       │           │ judgment” for LLM apps, yet it misses the input’s requested             │
│              │                            │       │           │ comparison of LLM lifecycle vs LLM-based apps and includes irrelevant   │
│              │                            │       │           │ course chatter like “you will get some edge over your competition”      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and “Ok”.                                                               │
│        FAIL  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.40  │ 0.50      │ The actual output is only partially aligned. It correctly identifies    │
│              │                            │       │           │ that the course focuses on LLM-based apps and mentions evaluation       │
│              │                            │       │           │ challenges, but it misses the expected distinction that the LLM         │
│              │                            │       │           │ lifecycle is about training or fine-tuning models and requires          │
│              │                            │       │           │ massive data, compute, infrastructure, technical expertise, and high    │
│              │                            │       │           │ cost. Instead, it talks about traditional ML versus LLM evals, human    │
│              │                            │       │           │ judgment, and launch readiness, which is not the same substance as      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the expected comparison between lifecycle work and practical app        │
│              │                            │       │           │ building like RAG and agentic apps.                                     │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear and student-friendly, with simple headings for    │
│              │                            │       │           │ needs, costs, and course focus. It directly contrasts traditional       │
│              │                            │       │           │ ML/LLM lifecycle evaluation with LLM-based apps, using mostly plain     │
│              │                            │       │           │ language and only a few mild technical terms like "probabilistic" and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ "non-deterministic." It addresses the test case well and is easy to     │
│              │                            │       │           │ understand.                                                             │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_58                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Draft syllabus for LLM-apps only; exclude foundation-model training, fine-tuning, and heavy compute.         │
│  │     Actual Output:      Course title: Building LLM-based Applications (LLM-Apps, excluding foundation-model                          │
│  │                         training/fine-tuning/heavy compute)                                                                          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Course objective: Teach how to design, build, evaluate, and operate LLM-based applications (LLM aux and      │
│  │                         surrounding systems). Focus on application architecture, agents, prompt engineering,                         │
│  │                         libraries/frameworks, and application-level evaluation and ops. Explicitly exclude training foundation       │
│  │                         models, fine-tuning, and heavy compute workflows.                                                            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Structure: 10 weeks (or 10 modules). Each week = lecture + hands-on lab / assignment. One dedicated          │
│  │                         lecture on model/application evaluation.                                                                     │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 1 — Course intro & scope                                                                                │
│  │                         - What is an LLM-based application vs. a foundation model                                                    │
│  │                         - Role of LLM aux (LLM as one component among many)                                                          │
│  │                         - Industry categorization of LLM apps and common application patterns                                        │
│  │                         - Deliverable: short write-up mapping a chosen industry use-case to an LLM-app architecture                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 2 — Application architectures & components                                                              │
│  │                         - Typical LLM-app components: orchestrator/agents, connectors, context management, caching, user-facing      │
│  │                         layers                                                                                                       │
│  │                         - Design patterns for composing LLMs into apps                                                               │
│  │                         - Deliverable: architecture diagram and component responsibilities for chosen use-case                       │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 3 — Prompt engineering for applications                                                                 │
│  │                         - Prompting strategies, prompt templates and prompt modularity for production apps                           │
│  │                         - Prompt versioning and reuse across components                                                              │
│  │                         - Lab: design prompt templates for core app flows and evaluate behavior                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 4 — Agents and orchestration fundamentals                                                               │
│  │                         - Agent concepts and why agents are used in apps                                                             │
│  │                         - Patterns for tool use, multi-step workflows, decision loops                                                │
│  │                         - Deliverable: design an agent workflow for a multi-step user task                                           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 5 — Libraries and frameworks for agents and orchestration                                               │
│  │                         - Survey and hands-on with practical libraries (examples covered in course materials: Langraph (YouTube      │
│  │                         intro), CRIO AI, Agno)                                                                                       │
│  │                         - How to pick a library for a given app need                                                                 │
│  │                         - Lab: build a small agent workflow using one framework                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 6 — Integrations, connectors, and auxiliary services                                                    │
│  │                         - Connecting to data sources, external APIs, and tools (design considerations)                               │
│  │                         - Context management, session/state handling, and caching strategies                                         │
│  │                         - Deliverable: implement a connector or mock integration for your app                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 7 — LLM Ops for applications (operations, not model training)                                           │
│  │                         - Deployment patterns for LLM-app components, latency and cost trade-offs (at application level)             │
│  │                         - Monitoring, logging, observability, and incident considerations for apps                                   │
│  │                         - Deliverable: monitoring/alerting plan and cost-aware deployment choices                                    │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 8 — Application evaluation (dedicated lecture)                                                          │
│  │                         - Why application-level evals differ from model evals                                                        │
│  │                         - Metrics and evaluation strategies for full apps (end-to-end correctness, task success, user                │
│  │                         satisfaction, reliability)                                                                                   │
│  │                         - Designing evaluation suites for app components and flows                                                   │
│  │                         - Lab: create an evaluation plan and test cases for your app                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 9 — Safety, reliability, and user experience in apps                                                    │
│  │                         - Application-level guardrails, graceful failure modes, and UX design for model uncertainty                  │
│  │                         - Handling hallucinations, recovery flows, and human-in-the-loop patterns                                    │
│  │                         - Deliverable: safety & reliability blueprint for app                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Week 10 — Capstone: build and evaluate an LLM-based application                                              │
│  │                         - Integrate agents, prompts, connectors, ops, and evaluation                                                 │
│  │                         - Final deliverable: working prototype (or detailed implementation plan + demo), architecture docs,          │
│  │                         evaluation report                                                                                            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Assessment                                                                                                   │
│  │                         - Weekly labs/mini-assignments (practical, framework-focused)                                                │
│  │                         - Mid-course architecture assignment                                                                         │
│  │                         - Final capstone project + evaluation report                                                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Tools & resources (course-provided)                                                                          │
│  │                         - Example code templates and starter projects using agent frameworks (Langraph material, CRIO AI, Agno)      │
│  │                         - Prompt template library and evaluation checklist                                                           │
│  │                         - Ops/monitoring checklist and sample dashboards                                                             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Constraints and exclusions (explicit)                                                                        │
│  │                         - No coverage of training foundation models from scratch                                                     │
│  │                         - No fine-tuning workflows requiring heavy compute                                                           │
│  │                         - Course focuses on application-side design, orchestration, evaluation, and ops                              │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Outcome                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Students will be able to design and build LLM-centered applications using agent patterns and available     │
│  │                         libraries, operate them in production settings, and perform application-level evaluation and reliability     │
│  │                         planning.                                                                                                    │
│  │     Expected Output:    Here’s a concise syllabus for **LLM-apps only**:                                                             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         1. **Introduction to LLM Ops**                                                                               │
│  │                            - What LLM Ops means in application development                                                           │
│  │                            - Difference between foundation-model training and LLM-apps                                               │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         2. **LLM-App Types**                                                                                         │
│  │                            - **RAG-based applications**                                                                              │
│  │                            - **Agentic applications**                                                                                │
│  │                            - When to use each                                                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         3. **RAG Pipeline Basics**                                                                                   │
│  │                            - Query → retrieval → context assembly → prompt augmentation → LLM response                               │
│  │                            - Working with vector databases and metadata                                                              │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         4. **Building LLM Applications**                                                                             │
│  │                            - Designing app workflows                                                                                 │
│  │                            - Prompting for context-based answers                                                                     │
│  │                            - Integrating LLM responses into web apps/chatbots                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         5. **Deployment and Integration**                                                                            │
│  │                            - Taking the app from local build to end-user use                                                         │
│  │                            - Website/app integration basics                                                                          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         6. **Monitoring and Maintenance**                                                                            │
│  │                            - Tracking app behavior                                                                                   │
│  │                            - Debugging and improving LLM-app performance                                                             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         7. **Practical Project Work**                                                                                │
│  │                            - Build a **RAG-based project** during learning                                                           │
│  │                            - Build an **agentic application** as the final project                                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         **Excluded:** foundation-model training, fine-tuning, massive compute/infrastructure-heavy work.             │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │

│              │                            │       │           │ plus “After that we have also touched on LLM Ops.” However, it also     │

│              │                            │       │           │ training/fine-tuning/heavy compute, and includes architecture,          │

│              │                            │       │           │ the RAG-specific track entirely, including the RAG pipeline basics,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ vector databases/metadata, and the required RAG-based project. It       │

│              │                            │       │           │ requested. However, it still includes several technical terms like      │
│              │                            │       │           │ "orchestrator/agents," "observability," and "application-level          │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation," which makes it slightly less simple than a very basic      │
│              │                            │       │           │ syllabus.                                                               │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_59                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Why avoid fine-tuning and training LLMs from scratch in this course?                                         │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Because teaching fine-tuning or training from scratch requires prior coverage (pre‑training concepts)      │
│  │                         and a sequence of topics; jumping straight to fine‑tuning would be hard to teach and hard for students to    │
│  │                         absorb.                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - The course is aimed at building LLM‑based applications, not re‑training models, so most students don’t     │
│  │                         need to change model parameters.                                                                             │
│  │                         - Only a small subset of students would realistically train foundation models from scratch, so it’s not      │
│  │                         practical to cover here; if demand grows, a separate session or instructor can be arranged.                  │
│  │                         - The instructors explicitly avoid retraining because “we don't touch most of the parameters of the model    │
│  │                         here” — many parameters capture core NLU/inner abilities (reasoning, input transformation) that are not      │
│  │                         what the course’s application‑level outputs depend on.                                                       │
│  │     Expected Output:    Because fine-tuning and training from scratch need massive data, compute, infrastructure, technical          │
│  │                         expertise, and cost. In this course, we focus on the LLM-based applications branch instead, since it’s       │
│  │                         more practical and relevant for learning LLM Ops.                                                            │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
