│              │                            │       │           │ because you do not have your real dataset, automation for many          │
│              │                            │       │           │ examples, a pool of 50–100 examples, reliability varying, and a         │
│              │                            │       │           │ required workflow to review, edit, and enrich examples before           │
│              │                            │       │           │ treating them as ground truth. It further introduces an "I don't        │
│              │                            │       │           │ know" field and a "JSON:" ending label that are not mentioned in the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ context.                                                                │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.91  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.91 because most of the context matches the topic of      │
│              │                            │       │           │ synthetic Q-A generation, including useful guidance like "These are     │
│              │                            │       │           │ some of the Best practices on synthetic data" and "Edit and enrich      │
│              │                            │       │           │ generated examples before treating them as ground truth." However, a    │
│              │                            │       │           │ few lines are off-topic or too vague, such as "See what is written      │
│              │                            │       │           │ here," "This point is of our use.," and "Never be greedy," which        │
│              │                            │       │           │ lowers the score slightly.                                              │
│        FAIL  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.30  │ 0.50      │ The response diverges substantially from the expected answer. It        │
│              │                            │       │           │ misses the key framing that synthetic Q-A generation is a smart,        │
│              │                            │       │           │ automated approach for creating a question paper from a knowledge       │
│              │                            │       │           │ base, and it omits the exact required JSON fields: question, answer,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ and metadata/source info. While it mentions some generic pros and       │
│              │                            │       │           │ cons, several are invented or shifted in emphasis, and the final “I     │
│              │                            │       │           │ don't know” fails to provide the expected structured conclusion.        │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response is mostly clear and student-friendly, with short bullet    │
│              │                            │       │           │ points and plain language. However, it fails the input’s request for    │
│              │                            │       │           │ the required JSON fields by saying “I don't know,” so it is             │
│              │                            │       │           │ incomplete despite being easy to understand.                            │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_65                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Why maximize coverage in a 15-question paper for balanced, high-impact assessment?                           │
│  │     Actual Output:      - To test multiple aspects (reasoning power, aptitude, factual presentation) within the strict               │
│  │                         15-question limit so each skill is sampled rather than omitted.                                              │
│  │                         - To make the paper “worthy of testing” — wider coverage increases the paper’s validity and fairness by      │
│  │                         representing the syllabus rather than focusing narrowly.                                                     │
│  │                         - To get high impact from each question: maximizing coverage raises the discriminative power of the whole    │
│  │                         paper, so you can better judge different students’ strengths.                                                │
│  │                         - To avoid the dilemma of depth vs. breadth: a deliberately varied 15-item paper balances range and          │
│  │                         manageability.                                                                                               │
│  │                         - To compensate for limited subject familiarity: broad coverage reduces the risk that gaps in your own       │
│  │                         knowledge lead to untested parts of the book.                                                                │
│  │     Expected Output:    Because with only 15 questions, you need to cover as many important concepts as possible while keeping       │
│  │                         the paper balanced and high-impact. This helps test different aspects of the student properly instead of     │
│  │                         focusing on just a few chapters or one type of question.                                                     │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because both sentences are fully supported by         │
│              │                            │       │           │ node(s) in retrieval context: sentence 1 is backed by node 1’s “only    │
│              │                            │       │           │ 15 questions” and nodes 2–3’s “cover different aspects,” “full          │
│              │                            │       │           │ coverage,” and “maximize my coverage,” while sentence 2 is also         │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by node 1 and nodes 2–3 through the emphasis on testing       │
│              │                            │       │           │ different aspects rather than concentrating on just a few chapters or   │
│              │                            │       │           │ one question type. Great match!                                         │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because all retrieval contexts are relevant nodes     │
│              │                            │       │           │ and they are ranked appropriately at the top: node 1 supports           │
│              │                            │       │           │ covering "different aspects" and "cover my student on multiple          │
│              │                            │       │           │ aspects," node 2 states there should be only 15 questions and asks      │
│              │                            │       │           │ for "full coverage," and node 3 directly captures the goal to           │
│              │                            │       │           │ "maximize my coverage" while keeping the paper to "not be more than     │
│              │                            │       │           │ 15 questions." There are no irrelevant nodes placed above relevant      │
│              │                            │       │           │ ones, so the ranking is perfect.                                        │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response is fully aligned with the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ input and there are no irrelevant statements. It directly addresses     │
│              │                            │       │           │ why maximizing coverage in a 15-question paper helps create a           │
│              │                            │       │           │ balanced, high-impact assessment.                                       │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.40  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.40 because the actual output adds claims not supported   │
│              │                            │       │           │ by the context, such as saying the paper is "worthy of testing" and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ discussing validity and fairness, discriminative power and comparing    │
│              │                            │       │           │ students’ strengths, and limited subject familiarity or the             │
│              │                            │       │           │ test-maker’s own knowledge, none of which appear in the retrieval       │
│              │                            │       │           │ context. It only loosely matches the context’s points about             │
│              │                            │       │           │ maximizing coverage, widening question range, and balancing full        │
│              │                            │       │           │ coverage with a 15-question limit.                                      │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.86  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.86 because most of the context directly supports the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ goal of maximizing coverage in a 15-question paper, such as "I have     │
│              │                            │       │           │ to set a paper where I can maximize my coverage" and "first pain        │
│              │                            │       │           │ point is that I want full coverage but I have only limited              │
│              │                            │       │           │ questions." The few irrelevant bits like "ok" and "I don't have full    │
│              │                            │       │           │ knowledge of this book" don't change the overall strong relevance.      │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The actual output preserves the main idea that, with only 15            │
│              │                            │       │           │ questions, the paper should cover many important concepts, stay         │
│              │                            │       │           │ balanced, and test different aspects of the student. However, it adds   │
│              │                            │       │           │ several unsupported points such as reasoning power, factual             │
│              │                            │       │           │ presentation, validity/fairness, discriminative power, and              │
│              │                            │       │           │ compensating for the examiner’s limited subject familiarity, which      │
│              │                            │       │           │ are not present in the expected output. The wording is also more        │
│              │                            │       │           │ elaborate than the original concise explanation, so it is mostly        │
│              │                            │       │           │ correct but not exact.                                                  │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear and well-structured with bullet points, and it    │
│              │                            │       │           │ explains why broad coverage matters in a 15-question paper. However,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ it uses several technical or academic terms like "validity,"            │
│              │                            │       │           │ "discriminative power," and "manageability," which makes it less        │
│              │                            │       │           │ student-friendly and not as simple as the rubric's top range.           │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_66                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              List 3 post-deploy LLM risks; contrast offline eval vs online eval.                                          │
│  │     Actual Output:      I don't know the specific three post-deploy LLM risks from the provided context.                             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Contrast — Offline eval vs Online eval (from the provided context):                                          │
│  │                         - Timing: Offline eval is done before deployment; online eval happens after deployment (on live system).     │
│  │                         - Data: Offline uses a fixed, created "golden" dataset; online works on live production traffic (no fixed    │
│  │                         golden set).                                                                                                 │
│  │                         - Traffic / users: Offline evaluates on pre-collected examples; online evaluates as real users interact      │
│  │                         with the system.                                                                                             │
│  │                         - Purpose / coverage: Offline evals are the foundation and useful pre-deploy checks, but they cannot         │
│  │                         evaluate many production risks; online evals are necessary to catch issues that only appear in               │
│  │                         production.                                                                                                  │
│  │     Expected Output:    After deployment, 3 main LLM risks are:                                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         1. **Unanticipated user inputs** — users can ask anything.                                                   │
│  │                         2. **Emergent system failures** — e.g. bias, latency spikes under load.                                      │
│  │                         3. **Drift** — the system’s behavior changes over time.                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         **Offline eval vs online eval:**                                                                             │
│  │                         - **Offline eval** happens **before deployment** on a **fixed golden dataset** with answer keys. It is       │
│  │                         good for **testing correctness**, **gating**, and **regression testing**.                                    │
│  │                         - **Online eval** happens **after deployment** on **live production traffic**. It works **without a          │
│  │                         golden dataset** and is used to detect **drift** and see whether the system is **running normally** in       │
│  │                         production.                                                                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         In short: **offline eval checks correctness; online eval checks production behavior.**                       │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 0.67  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.67 because sentences 1-2 are well supported by node(s)   │
│              │                            │       │           │ in retrieval context 1-2: they cover post-deployment LLM risks and      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the offline/online framing, including online eval on live production    │
│              │                            │       │           │ traffic after deployment and offline eval before deployment with        │
│              │                            │       │           │ fixed/golden data. However, sentences 3-4 are only partially            │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported: nodes 1-2 do not state that offline eval is for              │
│              │                            │       │           │ correctness, gating, or regression testing, and they also do not say    │
│              │                            │       │           │ online eval works without a golden dataset or is used to detect drift   │
│              │                            │       │           │ / confirm normal production behavior.                                   │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the first node in retrieval contexts says     │
│              │                            │       │           │ "After deploying, There are many types of risks in production setup,"   │
│              │                            │       │           │ and the second and third nodes in retrieval contexts both support the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ offline vs online eval contrast with "offline eval, Is before           │
│              │                            │       │           │ deployment" and "online eval is evaluating your system, On live         │
│              │                            │       │           │ production traffic, After deployment," so the relevant nodes are        │
│              │                            │       │           │ already ranked above any irrelevant nodes and there is no need to       │
│              │                            │       │           │ move anything lower.                                                    │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.90 because the response is mostly on-topic, but it       │
│              │                            │       │           │ includes a vague statement about lacking knowledge instead of           │
│              │                            │       │           │ directly listing three post-deploy LLM risks and contrasting offline    │
│              │                            │       │           │ vs online evaluation. It is still fairly high because it remains        │
│              │                            │       │           │ close to the asked topic and likely covers most of the core request.    │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.50  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.50 because the actual output adds claims not supported   │
│              │                            │       │           │ by the context, such as saying online eval does not use a fixed         │
│              │                            │       │           │ golden set and that offline eval relies on pre-collected examples or    │
│              │                            │       │           │ is the foundation of pre-deploy checks. It also states specific         │
│              │                            │       │           │ post-deploy LLM risks and says online eval is necessary to catch        │
│              │                            │       │           │ production-only issues, none of which are explicitly mentioned in the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context.                                                      │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.93  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.93 because the context is mostly on point: it explains   │
│              │                            │       │           │ that after deployment there are “many types of risks in production      │
│              │                            │       │           │ setup” and that “you cannot evaluate those risks, With the help of      │
│              │                            │       │           │ offline eval,” which directly supports contrasting offline vs online    │
│              │                            │       │           │ eval and post-deploy LLM risks. The only irrelevant part is the         │
│              │                            │       │           │ mention of “an UPSC application” and “an LLM application,” which does   │
│              │                            │       │           │ not address the request.                                                │
│        FAIL  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.40  │ 0.50      │ The response correctly distinguishes offline eval as pre-deployment     │
│              │                            │       │           │ on a fixed golden dataset and online eval as post-deployment on live    │
│              │                            │       │           │ production traffic, with the right high-level contrast. However, it     │
│              │                            │       │           │ fails to provide the three required post-deploy LLM risks and instead   │
│              │                            │       │           │ says it doesn't know them, and it adds unsupported phrasing like        │
│              │                            │       │           │ "foundation" and "cannot evaluate many production risks." The core      │
│              │                            │       │           │ answer is only partially complete.                                      │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.60  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear and student-friendly, with simple language and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ a direct offline vs online comparison using timing, data, traffic,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and purpose. However, it fails the first part of the input by not       │
│              │                            │       │           │ listing the 3 post-deploy LLM risks, instead saying it does not know    │
│              │                            │       │           │ them, so it only partially answers the task.                            │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_67                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Offline vs online eval: timing, data, errors caught, cost, purpose?                                          │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Timing                                                                                                     │
│  │                           - Offline: before deployment.                                                                              │
│  │                           - Online: after deployment; runs consistently on live system.                                              │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Data                                                                                                       │
│  │                           - Offline: fixed, created "golden" data set.                                                               │
│  │                           - Online: live production traffic (real questions as they arrive).                                         │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Answer key / errors caught                                                                                 │
│  │                           - Offline: you mostly have an answer key (golden labels) so you can catch correctness issues relative      │
│  │                         to that key before deployment.                                                                               │
│  │                           - Online: no answer key — you must estimate on the go. It catches issues that only appear in production    │
│  │                         (real-world inputs, distribution shifts, runtime/user-interaction problems) that offline may miss.           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Cost                                                                                                       │
│  │                           - Offline: uses a fixed dataset (lower recurring cost).                                                    │
│  │                           - Online: can be more expensive since it runs on live traffic; you can run it on sampled/random traffic    │
│  │                         to bring the cost down a little.                                                                             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Purpose                                                                                                    │
│  │                           - Offline: pre-deployment validation using known correct answers.                                          │
│  │                           - Online: post-deployment monitoring and evaluation on real traffic. Both are complementary and            │
│  │                         important to have together.                                                                                  │
│  │     Expected Output:    - **Timing:** Offline eval happens **before deployment**; online eval happens **after deployment** on        │
│  │                         live traffic.                                                                                                │
│  │                         - **Data:** Offline uses a **fixed golden dataset with answer keys**; online uses **real production          │
│  │                         inputs** with no golden answers.                                                                             │
│  │                         - **Errors caught:** Offline catches **regressions** and checks correctness; online catches **drift,         │
│  │                         emergent failures, bias, latency issues, and unexpected user inputs**.                                       │
│  │                         - **Cost:** Offline is **fast, cheap, and repeatable**; online is usually **more expensive** because it      │
│  │                         monitors live traffic at scale, often via sampling.                                                          │
│  │                         - **Purpose:** Offline eval checks **whether the application works correctly**; online eval checks           │
│  │                         **whether it is running normally in production**.                                                            │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because all 5 sentences are supported by nodes in     │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context: sentence 1 is backed by node 1 and node 2 on         │
│              │                            │       │           │ timing (offline before deployment, online on live production            │
│              │                            │       │           │ traffic); sentence 2 is backed by node 2 and node 3 on data (fixed      │
│              │                            │       │           │ golden dataset vs no golden answers in live traffic); sentence 3 is     │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by nodes 1, 2, and 3 on error types and monitoring;           │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 4 is supported by nodes 1, 2, and 3 on cost/scale; and         │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 5 is supported by nodes 1, 2, and 3 on purpose (correctness    │
│              │                            │       │           │ vs production monitoring).                                              │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the top-ranked nodes in retrieval contexts    │
│              │                            │       │           │ are all directly relevant: node 1 says offline eval is "before          │
│              │                            │       │           │ deployment" and online eval is on "live production traffic," node 2     │
│              │                            │       │           │ repeats that offline eval is "before deployment" while online eval      │
│              │                            │       │           │ uses "live production traffic," and node 3 adds that offline uses a     │
│              │                            │       │           │ "fixed data set" and "Golden data set." There are no irrelevant nodes   │
│              │                            │       │           │ ranked above relevant ones, so nothing needs to be pushed lower, and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ the relevant nodes are correctly ordered at the top.                    │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully and directly addressed     │
│              │                            │       │           │ the comparison between offline and online evaluation—covering timing,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ data, errors caught, cost, and purpose—with no irrelevant content.      │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.38  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.38 because the actual output adds several details not    │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by the context: it says online eval runs consistently on      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the live system, uses sampled or random traffic to reduce cost, and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ is post-deployment monitoring, while also claiming offline eval uses    │
│              │                            │       │           │ golden labels, catches correctness issues against that key, has lower   │
│              │                            │       │           │ recurring cost, and is pre-deployment validation with known correct     │
│              │                            │       │           │ answers. The context only says online eval happens after deployment     │
│              │                            │       │           │ on live production traffic and offline eval uses a fixed dataset with   │
│              │                            │       │           │ an answer key, without those extra specifics.                           │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.95  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.95 because the context strongly matches the query,       │
│              │                            │       │           │ covering timing, data, errors caught, cost, and purpose with lines      │
│              │                            │       │           │ like 'Offline eval is before deployment.' and 'After online eval        │
│              │                            │       │           │ deployment.' and 'In offline eval, you have a fixed data set...         │
│              │                            │       │           │ Golden data set.' However, it includes a stray irrelevant statement —   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'Randomly.' — which slightly lowers the relevance.                      │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response matches the main distinctions: offline before deployment   │
│              │                            │       │           │ with a golden dataset/answer key, online after deployment on live       │
│              │                            │       │           │ production traffic without gold answers, and it notes offline           │
│              │                            │       │           │ correctness versus online production monitoring. It also correctly      │
│              │                            │       │           │ mentions higher online cost and live-traffic sampling. However, it is   │
│              │                            │       │           │ slightly incomplete versus the expected output because it does not      │
│              │                            │       │           │ explicitly mention online catching bias, latency issues, or             │
│              │                            │       │           │ unexpected user inputs, and the purpose wording is less direct about    │
│              │                            │       │           │ "running normally in production."                                       │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, well-structured, and easy for a student to       │
│              │                            │       │           │ follow, with simple headings for timing, data, answer key/errors,       │
│              │                            │       │           │ cost, and purpose. It directly answers the offline vs online            │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation comparison in the input and avoids heavy jargon, though a    │
│              │                            │       │           │ few terms like "distribution shifts" and "production traffic" are       │
│              │                            │       │           │ slightly technical.                                                     │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_68                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Why can’t offline eval handle post-deploy risks like unanticipated inputs, emergent failures, and drift?     │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Offline eval depends on a fixed "golden" dataset with pre-specified inputs and correct answers. That       │
│  │                         dataset can only represent known, anticipated cases — it cannot cover every possible user question or        │
│  │                         future input.                                                                                                │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Real users are uncontrolled: they can ask anything. Unanticipated inputs and new usage patterns simply     │
│  │                         won't be present in the offline test set, so offline metrics won't reveal failures on those cases.           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Drift changes the input distribution after deployment. Once drift occurs the offline test set becomes      │
│  │                         obsolete and its results no longer predict live behavior.                                                    │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Emergent systematic failures (e.g., new biases, failure modes that arise from scale or from                │
│  │                         interactions with real users) often only appear in production and won't be exposed by offline examples       │
│  │                         prepared beforehand.                                                                                         │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Offline eval also cannot observe long-running interaction effects, feedback loops, or                      │
│  │                         environment-dependent behavior that only manifest on live traffic.                                           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         For these reasons offline eval is necessary pre-launch but insufficient to detect and measure many           │
│  │                         post-deploy risks — online evaluation on real production traffic is required.                                │
│  │     Expected Output:    Offline eval can’t handle post-deploy risks because it relies on a fixed golden dataset with known           │
│  │                         answers. After deployment, user inputs are unpredictable, so you can’t prepare or score every future case    │
│  │                         in advance. Also, issues like emergent failures, latency spikes, bias, and drift only appear in live         │
│  │                         production traffic. That’s why these risks need online eval, which monitors real-world behavior without      │
│  │                         requiring a golden answer key.                                                                               │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because every sentence is fully supported: sentence   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 1 matches node 3’s fixed golden dataset with known answers, sentence    │
│              │                            │       │           │ 2 aligns with nodes 1-3 about post-deployment unpredictability and      │
│              │                            │       │           │ lack of control, sentence 3 is supported by nodes 1-3 on emergent       │
│              │                            │       │           │ production risks like latency, bias, and drift, and sentence 4 is       │
│              │                            │       │           │ backed by nodes 2-3 describing online eval on live production traffic   │
│              │                            │       │           │ without a golden answer key. Great coverage overall.                    │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the retrieval contexts are perfectly          │
│              │                            │       │           │ ordered: the first node in retrieval contexts says offline eval         │
│              │                            │       │           │ becomes obsolete after drift starts and mentions post-deployment        │
│              │                            │       │           │ risks like "the user will ask any kind of question" and "emergent       │
│              │                            │       │           │ systematic failures" such as "bias"; the second node in retrieval       │
│              │                            │       │           │ contexts adds that "After deploying" there are "many types of risks     │
│              │                            │       │           │ in production setup" that "you cannot evaluate... with offline eval,"   │
│              │                            │       │           │ contrasting this with live traffic; and the third node in retrieval     │
│              │                            │       │           │ contexts reinforces that offline eval depends on "a golden data set"    │
│              │                            │       │           │ with "the correct answer," which fits the input. There are no           │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant nodes ranked above relevant ones, so the precision is        │
│              │                            │       │           │ maximized.                                                              │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addressed why offline      │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluation cannot capture post-deploy risks such as unanticipated       │
│              │                            │       │           │ inputs, emergent failures, and drift, with no irrelevant content.       │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.70 because the actual output adds details not            │
│              │                            │       │           │ explicitly supported by the context, such as offline eval depending     │
│              │                            │       │           │ on a fixed “golden” dataset, only covering known anticipated cases,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ and failing to capture long-running interaction effects or feedback     │
│              │                            │       │           │ loops; the context only says offline evaluation becomes obsolete with   │
│              │                            │       │           │ drift and cannot assess post-deployment risks.                          │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.79  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.79 because most of the context is relevant to            │
│              │                            │       │           │ post-deploy risk, noting that "after deploying, There are many types    │
│              │                            │       │           │ of risks in production setup" and "you cannot evaluate those risks,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ With the help of offline eval." However, some parts are generic or      │
│              │                            │       │           │ off-topic, like "Is the point clear?" and "What is the way to work      │
│              │                            │       │           │ with offline eval?", which lowers the relevance a bit.                  │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response matches the expected idea that offline eval uses a fixed   │
│              │                            │       │           │ golden dataset and cannot cover unpredictable post-deployment inputs,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ and it correctly notes production-only issues like drift and emergent   │
│              │                            │       │           │ failures. However, it adds several details not explicitly present in    │
│              │                            │       │           │ the expected output, such as long-running interaction effects and       │
│              │                            │       │           │ feedback loops, and it slightly shifts the framing from specific        │
│              │                            │       │           │ post-deploy risks like latency spikes to broader production behavior.   │
│              │                            │       │           │ Overall it is mostly correct with minor differences, not a perfect      │
│              │                            │       │           │ match.                                                                  │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, well structured, and easy to understand, with    │
│              │                            │       │           │ mostly simple wording. It directly addresses the test case by           │
│              │                            │       │           │ explaining why offline eval misses unanticipated inputs, drift, and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ emergent failures, and it avoids heavy technical jargon. The final      │
│              │                            │       │           │ point about needing online evaluation is also student friendly.         │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_69                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              What do feedback, demand, and surprises mean in the Insider tier?                                            │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Feedback: Insider members get the power of feedback — i.e., the ability to give feedback to the team (a    │
│  │                         formal “power” of the Insider tier).                                                                         │
│  │                         - Demand: Insider members get the power to demand — you can request what paid course should be made and      │
│  │                         what topic should be taught on the next YouTube playlist; when many members signal the same thing, the       │
│  │                         team will listen.                                                                                            │
│  │                         - Surprises: These are gifts from the organization (not a power) given to Insiders over the next two         │
│  │                         years; a few examples will be shown at the end of the orientation and a big surprise is in today’s           │
│  │                         session.                                                                                                     │
│  │     Expected Output:    In the Insider tier:                                                                                         │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Feedback** means you can openly share any concerns or suggestions, and they’ll read and act on them.     │
│  │                         - **Demand** means you can request what paid course or YouTube topic they should make next.                  │
│  │                         - **Surprises** means you’ll get extra, unpromised benefits from them over the next two years.               │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is fully supported by nodes 1      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and 2 in retrieval context, which describe the insider tier’s           │
│              │                            │       │           │ feedback/demand powers; sentence 2 is supported by node 3, which        │
│              │                            │       │           │ matches requesting the next paid course or YouTube topic; and           │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 3 is also supported by node 1 and node 3, which mention        │
│              │                            │       │           │ surprises and extra gifts over the next two years.                      │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the top-ranked retrieval context in node 1,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ with the reason "You will get the power of feedback," directly          │
│              │                            │       │           │ matches feedback, node 2, with the reason "what paid course should we   │
│              │                            │       │           │ make" and "what topic should we teach on the next YouTube playlist,"    │
│              │                            │       │           │ directly matches demand, and node 3, with the reason "we will call it   │
│              │                            │       │           │ surprises" and "you will get a lot of surprises from our side over      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the next two years," directly matches surprises. All relevant nodes     │
│              │                            │       │           │ are ranked above any irrelevant nodes, so there is nothing to push      │
│              │                            │       │           │ lower.                                                                  │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the answer stayed fully on topic and          │
│              │                            │       │           │ addressed the Insider tier terms directly, with no irrelevant           │
│              │                            │       │           │ statements.                                                             │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.29  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.29 because the actual output adds unsupported details:   │
│              │                            │       │           │ it says insiders’ surprises are gifts, that they will be given over     │
│              │                            │       │           │ the next two years, that surprises are a power, and that examples       │
│              │                            │       │           │ will be shown at the end of orientation, plus it mentions a big         │
│              │                            │       │           │ surprise in today’s session. The context only says insiders will        │
│              │                            │       │           │ receive surprises and demand power, and it does not explain what        │
│              │                            │       │           │ happens when many insiders choose the same demand.                      │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.80 because the context directly explains the terms,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ stating that an insider gets "the power of feedback," "the power to     │
│              │                            │       │           │ demand," and that the third item "is not a power... we will call it     │
│              │                            │       │           │ surprises." However, some lines are unrelated to the question, such     │
│              │                            │       │           │ as "You will have to come in the sessions" and "If you are not coming   │
│              │                            │       │           │ in the sessions, you will have to see the recordings," which are        │
│              │                            │       │           │ about attendance rather than meanings.                                  │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the three Insider-tier items, but it introduces   │
│              │                            │       │           │ several deviations from the expected wording and meaning. For           │
│              │                            │       │           │ Feedback, it reframes the meaning as a formal “power” rather than       │
│              │                            │       │           │ simply openly sharing concerns or suggestions that will be read and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ acted on. For Demand, it adds extra specifics about a YouTube           │
│              │                            │       │           │ playlist and collective signaling that are not in the expected          │
│              │                            │       │           │ output, which only mentions requesting the next paid course or          │
│              │                            │       │           │ YouTube topic. For Surprises, it mostly matches the idea of             │
│              │                            │       │           │ unpromised benefits over the next two years, but the added              │
│              │                            │       │           │ orientation/session details are unsupported. Overall it is mostly       │
│              │                            │       │           │ aligned but not fully equivalent.                                       │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, student-friendly, and uses simple explanations   │
│              │                            │       │           │ for feedback, demand, and surprises in the Insider tier. It avoids      │
│              │                            │       │           │ heavy jargon overall, though it includes a few formal phrases like      │
│              │                            │       │           │ "power" and "orientation" that make it slightly less simple than the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ easiest level.                                                          │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_70                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Extract the insider-tier JSON for feedback, demand, and surprise perks.                                      │
│  │     Actual Output:      {                                                                                                            │
│  │                           "feedback": {                                                                                              │
│  │                             "name": "Power of feedback",                                                                             │
│  │                             "description": "Insiders can provide feedback without hesitation and will help improve the product.",    │
│  │                             "channel": "WhatsApp group",                                                                             │
│  │                             "expectation": "Attend sessions or watch recordings and show commitment"                                 │
│  │                           },                                                                                                         │
│  │                           "demand": {                                                                                                │
│  │                             "name": "Power to demand",                                                                               │
│  │                             "description": "Insiders have the ability to make demands (no further details provided in the            │
│  │                         context)."                                                                                                   │
│  │                           },                                                                                                         │
│  │                           "surprise": {                                                                                              │
│  │                             "name": "Surprises",                                                                                     │
│  │                             "description": "Insiders will receive many surprises, including things not previously promised.",        │
│  │                             "timing": "One or two examples shown at the end of the orientation session; a big surprise in today's    │
│  │                         session; additional surprises over the next two years"                                                       │
│  │                           }                                                                                                          │
│  │                         }                                                                                                            │
│  │     Expected Output:    {                                                                                                            │
│  │                           "insider_tier": {                                                                                          │
│  │                             "feedback": {                                                                                            │
│  │                               "power": "Share any concerns, dislikes, or suggestions directly in the WhatsApp group without          │
│  │                         hesitation.",                                                                                                │
│  │                               "channel": "WhatsApp group",                                                                           │
│  │                               "promise": "Messages will be read and considered for changes where possible."                          │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "demand": {                                                                                              │
│  │                               "power": "Request future paid courses or YouTube playlist topics.",                                    │
│  │                               "how_it_works": "If many insiders ask for the same thing, it will be taken seriously and likely        │
│  │                         included."                                                                                                   │
│  │                             },                                                                                                       │
│  │                             "surprises": {                                                                                           │
│  │                               "gift": "Unexpected extra benefits and additions beyond what was promised.",                           │
│  │                               "note": "Some surprises will be revealed during the orientation session, including a big surprise      │
│  │                         today."                                                                                                      │
│  │                             }                                                                                                        │
│  │                           }                                                                                                          │
│  │                         }                                                                                                            │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 0.88  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.88 because sentence 1 is well supported by node 1 and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ node 2 in retrieval context: the power of feedback, telling concerns    │
│              │                            │       │           │ in the WhatsApp group without hesitation, and messages being            │
│              │                            │       │           │ considered for changes all align closely. Sentence 2 is mostly          │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by node 1, which covers the power to demand requests, but     │
│              │                            │       │           │ the exact details about future paid courses or YouTube playlist         │
│              │                            │       │           │ topics are not stated. Sentence 3 is supported by node 1 and its        │
│              │                            │       │           │ mention of surprises and a big surprise today, but the part saying      │
│              │                            │       │           │ some surprises will be revealed during orientation is only partially    │
│              │                            │       │           │ matched. The only clear gap is that node 2 does not explicitly say      │
│              │                            │       │           │ requests for the same thing will be taken seriously and likely          │
│              │                            │       │           │ included, so the recall is high but not perfect.                        │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the first, second, and third nodes in the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval contexts are all relevant and directly support the            │
│              │                            │       │           │ requested insider-tier JSON: the first node says 'As an insider, we     │
│              │                            │       │           │ will give you many surprises' and 'a big surprise is in today's         │
│              │                            │       │           │ session,' the second node says 'you will get the power of feedback'     │
│              │                            │       │           │ and 'you can tell us without hesitation,' and the third node confirms   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'There are three membership tiers' and 'Third is insider.' Since no     │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant nodes appear above any relevant ones, the ranking is         │
│              │                            │       │           │ already perfect.                                                        │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 0.64  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.64 because the response partially addressed the          │
│              │                            │       │           │ insider-tier JSON request, but several statements drifted into          │
│              │                            │       │           │ attendance, commitment, timing, and session-specific details instead    │
│              │                            │       │           │ of focusing strictly on the feedback, demand, and surprise perks JSON   │
│              │                            │       │           │ structure. It is not higher because those irrelevant references         │
│              │                            │       │           │ reduce direct relevance, but it remains fairly solid since the core     │
│              │                            │       │           │ topic still centered on the requested insider-tier perks.               │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.18  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.18 because the actual output invents sections like       │
│              │                            │       │           │ “Power of feedback,” “Power to demand,” and “Surprises” that the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ context does not name, adds unsupported claims such as a WhatsApp       │
│              │                            │       │           │ feedback channel, no-hesitation feedback, and attendance/recording      │
│              │                            │       │           │ commitment details, and says examples were shown when the context       │
│              │                            │       │           │ only says they would be given at the end of the orientation session.    │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.63  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.63 because the context does contain key insider-tier     │
│              │                            │       │           │ perk details like "You will get the power of feedback," "You will get   │
│              │                            │       │           │ the power to demand," and "As an insider, we will give you many         │
│              │                            │       │           │ surprises," but it also includes a lot of unrelated tier/explanatory    │
│              │                            │       │           │ text such as "The lowest tier is learner," "Second is aspirant," and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ "what it means to be an insider," which only partially matches the      │
│              │                            │       │           │ request to extract insider-tier JSON.                                   │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.50  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the general ideas of feedback, demand, and        │
│              │                            │       │           │ surprises, and correctly mentions the WhatsApp group and a big          │
│              │                            │       │           │ surprise today. However, it changes the structure and introduces        │
│              │                            │       │           │ incorrect details: the feedback promise about messages being read and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ considered is replaced with unrelated attendance/commitment text, the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ demand section omits the key explanation that repeated requests will    │
│              │                            │       │           │ be taken seriously and likely included, and the surprise section adds   │
│              │                            │       │           │ unsupported timing/details such as "over the next two years" and "one   │
│              │                            │       │           │ or two examples at the end."                                            │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The output is easy to read and uses plain language with simple JSON     │
│              │                            │       │           │ keys for feedback, demand, and surprise. It stays student-friendly      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and avoids heavy technical jargon, though a few details like            │
│              │                            │       │           │ "expectation" and the long timing field add some complexity.            │
│                                                                                                                                         │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_71                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How do upskilling and AI-updates differ in Insider membership’s goals and methods?                           │
│  │     Actual Output:      Goal                                                                                                         │
│  │                         - Upskilling: teach practical skills so members can design, optimize, fine‑tune and secure AI systems        │
│  │                         (e.g., planned courses on ML system design and AI engineering).                                              │
│  │                         - AI‑updates: keep members informed about the latest developments in the AI world (new models, research,     │
│  │                         announcements).                                                                                              │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Method                                                                                                       │
│  │                         - Upskilling: structured learning content — courses, playlists and sessions focused on system design and     │
│  │                         applied AI engineering; available free to Insiders.                                                          │
│  │                         - AI‑updates: regular (weekly) update sessions run by a dedicated content team whose job is to               │
│  │                         continuously research and report new models and developments.                                                │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Common point: both offerings are free for Insider members.                                                   │
│  │     Expected Output:    In Insider membership, **upskilling** and **AI-updates** are two different goals:                            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Upskilling**: focused on helping you learn relevant skills through **courses and paid notes**, both      │
│  │                         available to you for free in the membership.                                                                 │
│  │                         - **AI-updates**: focused on keeping you **current with fast-changing AI trends** through **weekly live      │
│  │                         sessions** like AI Pulse, where they discuss news, tools, and give tutorials.                                │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So, upskilling is about **learning**, while AI-updates are about **staying updated**.                        │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ sentence 2 is supported by nodes 1 and 2 through the references to      │
│              │                            │       │           │ courses being free and weekly sessions keeping users up to date with    │
│              │                            │       │           │ AI; sentence 3 is also supported by node 1 since it directly            │
│              │                            │       │           │ contrasts learning with staying updated. Great match overall!           │

│              │                            │       │           │ are ranked first and second: the 1st node clearly states, "our first    │
│              │                            │       │           │ primary goal" is free content and "The second goal is that we will      │
│              │                            │       │           │ have weekly sessions" to keep members up to date with AI, while the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ 2nd node adds, "we are planning a course around ML system design" and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ that future courses will be free, supporting the upskilling goal. The   │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant node in the 3rd position is about AI jobs and model work,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ so it is correctly placed lower since it does not address the           │
│              │                            │       │           │ membership goals and methods distinction.                               │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addresses the comparison   │
│              │                            │       │           │ between upskilling and AI-updates in Insider membership’s goals and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ methods, with no irrelevant statements to detract from relevance.       │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.67  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.67 because the actual output adds details not            │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by the context, such as teaching practical skills to          │
│              │                            │       │           │ design, optimize, fine-tune, and secure AI systems, plus implying       │
│              │                            │       │           │ playlists or applied AI engineering sessions. It also overstates the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ role of the separate content team by saying it runs the weekly update   │
│              │                            │       │           │ sessions, which the context does not explicitly say.                    │
