│              │                            │       │           │ question: it does mention a goal to “keep you up to date with the       │
│              │                            │       │           │ world of AI” via “weekly sessions” and a team that will “keep           │
│              │                            │       │           │ researching what new things have come,” which relates to AI-updates,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ but much of the rest is off-topic, like “We have recently hired         │
│              │                            │       │           │ people who are joining our team” and “ask for some session or a         │
│              │                            │       │           │ playlist on system design.”                                             │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the broad distinction that upskilling is for      │
│              │                            │       │           │ learning and AI-updates is for staying informed, and it correctly       │
│              │                            │       │           │ notes both are free for Insider members. However, it adds unsupported   │
│              │                            │       │           │ specifics such as ML system design, AI engineering, playlists, and a    │
│              │                            │       │           │ dedicated content team, while missing key expected details like paid    │
│              │                            │       │           │ notes and the weekly live sessions/AI Pulse focus on news, tools, and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ tutorials.                                                              │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear, well-organized, and easy to follow with simple   │
│              │                            │       │           │ headings for Goal and Method. It directly answers the comparison        │
│              │                            │       │           │ between upskilling and AI-updates for Insider membership, and mostly    │
│              │                            │       │           │ uses student-friendly language, though a few phrases like               │
│              │                            │       │           │ "fine-tune," "ML system design," and "dedicated content team" are a     │
│              │                            │       │           │ bit technical.                                                          │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_72                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Compare AI Pulse’s 4 offerings by purpose, format, and schedule.                                             │
│  │     Actual Output:      - News Roundup / News discussion                                                                             │
│  │                           - Purpose: keep you up to date with AI (news discussion).                                                  │
│  │                           - Format: news discussion led by the team.                                                                 │
│  │                           - Schedule: every Saturday, starting at 8:00 PM IST; runs from 8 PM to whatever time is required.          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Technical deep dives                                                                                       │
│  │                           - Purpose: deeper technical coverage of AI topics.                                                         │
│  │                           - Format: instructor-led technical deep dives (the speaker has booked themselves for these).               │
│  │                           - Schedule: part of the Saturday AI Pulse slot (starts 8:00 PM IST); exact occurrences depend on the       │
│  │                         instructor’s availability.                                                                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Interviews                                                                                                 │
│  │                           - Purpose: bring external perspectives/insights via invited guests.                                        │
│  │                           - Format: interview format conducted by the instructor (host will invite guests and be present).           │
│  │                           - Schedule: held within AI Pulse sessions on Saturdays at 8:00 PM IST as arranged—timing depends on        │
│  │                         guest/host availability.                                                                                     │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Master classes of tools                                                                                    │
│  │                           - Purpose: practical/tool-focused teaching (master classes).                                               │
│  │                           - Format: master-class style sessions on tools; the instructor may participate or lead.                    │
│  │                           - Schedule: delivered during AI Pulse Saturday sessions (8:00 PM IST); exact timing and frequency          │
│  │                         depend on availability.                                                                                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    Here’s a concise comparison of AI Pulse’s 4 offerings:                                                       │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         1. News Roundup                                                                                              │
│  │                         - Purpose: Keep members updated on important AI developments and separate signal from noise.                 │
│  │                         - Format: Discussion of recent AI news with deeper analysis and second-order thinking.                       │
│  │                         - Schedule: Every alternate Saturday, 8 pm IST.                                                              │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         2. Tools Tutorial                                                                                            │
│  │                         - Purpose: Help members become power users of new AI tools.                                                  │
│  │                         - Format: Live tutorial sessions on tools like Claude for Excel, Claude Code, 11 Labs, Pinecone, etc.        │
│  │                         - Schedule: About 1–2 sessions per month, usually on Saturday at 8 pm IST.                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         3. Guest Lecture                                                                                             │
│  │                         - Purpose: Expose members to real-world AI careers, industry insights, and experiences.                      │
│  │                         - Format: Live interview/Q&A with guests like industry leaders, successful job candidates, or other          │
│  │                         creators.                                                                                                    │
│  │                         - Schedule: One session per month, on Saturday at 8 pm IST.                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         4. Technical Deep Dives                                                                                      │
│  │                         - Purpose: Build deep understanding and perspective on complex AI topics.                                    │
│  │                         - Format: In-depth sessions on one topic, like voice AI landscape or foundation model training economics.    │
│  │                         - Schedule: One session per month, on Saturday at 8 pm IST.                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Overall, all four are live Saturday sessions, but they differ in focus: updates, tools, people, and deep     │
│  │                         technical understanding.                                                                                     │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ node in retrieval context mentions a distinct “Tools Tutorial”          │

│              │                            │       │           │ pm.' The irrelevant node 3 is correctly placed lower since it is        │
│              │                            │       │           │ mainly about background ('I am working on the Data Analytics and AI     │
│              │                            │       │           │ part on CampusX' and 'I am doing my doctorate in frugal AI') and does   │

│              │                            │       │           │ exact 8 PM to end-time timing, instructor-led technical deep dives      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and speaker bookings, interviews driven by invited guests and           │

│              │                            │       │           │ interviews, and tool master classes on Saturdays at 8 pm IST, without   │
│              │                            │       │           │ those extra specifics.                                                  │

│              │                            │       │           │ Pulse" and its purpose "We want to keep you up to date with AI," plus   │
│              │                            │       │           │ schedule info like "AI Pulse live sessions will be on Saturday" and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ "Every Saturday at 8pm." However, much of the context is irrelevant     │
│              │                            │       │           │ to comparing all 4 offerings by purpose, format, and schedule, such     │
│              │                            │       │           │ as "The statement about schedule ('every Saturday from 8 pm to          │

│              │                            │       │           │ and "The statement 'What is AI Pulse? What is going to happen in it?'   │

│              │                            │       │           │ purposes, but it changes several key details from the expected          │
│              │                            │       │           │ output. It incorrectly says News Roundup happens every Saturday         │
│              │                            │       │           │ instead of every alternate Saturday, omits the specific tool examples   │

│              │                            │       │           │ description. It also adds unsupported claims about                      │
│              │                            │       │           │ instructor-led/booked sessions and availability-based timing, so it     │
│              │                            │       │           │ is only partially aligned with the expected comparison.                 │

│              │                            │       │           │ It uses fairly simple language overall, though it includes a few        │
│              │                            │       │           │ slightly technical or awkward phrases like “instructor-led technical    │
│              │                            │       │           │ deep dives” and repeated scheduling caveats that make it a bit less     │
│              │                            │       │           │ student-friendly than it could be.                                      │

│  ❌ test_case_73                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How do AI Pulse sessions compare with YouTube Sandbox in frequency, day, and time?                           │
│  │     Actual Output:      AI Pulse: mostly on Saturdays — live AI Pulse sessions are every Saturday at 8:00 PM IST (weekly).           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         YouTube Sandbox: I don't know the frequency, day, or time from the provided context (the context only        │
│  │                         says the name and time are written, but doesn't give the actual schedule).                                   │
│  │     Expected Output:    AI Pulse sessions are held **every Saturday at 8 pm IST**.                                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         YouTube Sandbox sessions are held **mainly on Wednesday and Friday at 6 pm**, with **at least two            │
│  │                         sessions per week** and sometimes more depending on availability.                                            │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ retrieval context, which explicitly states AI Pulse sessions are        │
│              │                            │       │           │ every Saturday at 8 pm IST, but sentence 2 is not supported by nodes    │
│              │                            │       │           │ 1-3 since none mention YouTube Sandbox being mainly on Wednesday and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ Friday at 6 pm or having at least two sessions per week.                │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the relevant nodes in retrieval contexts      │
│              │                            │       │           │ are ranked first and second, while the only irrelevant node is          │
│              │                            │       │           │ already pushed to rank 3. The first node is useful since it notes AI    │
│              │                            │       │           │ Pulse sessions "will be mostly on Saturday" and mentions "YouTube       │
│              │                            │       │           │ Sandbox," and the second node directly states "AI Pulse live sessions   │
│              │                            │       │           │ will be on Saturday" and "Every Saturday at 8pm Indian Standard         │
│              │                            │       │           │ Time," so the needed comparison is supported early. The third node is   │
│              │                            │       │           │ lower because it only says "I will be taking most of your AI Pulse      │
│              │                            │       │           │ sessions" and "Today's session is going to be on AI Pulse" without      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the frequency, day, or time needed.                                     │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addresses the comparison   │
│              │                            │       │           │ of AI Pulse sessions and YouTube Sandbox by frequency, day, and time,   │
│              │                            │       │           │ with no irrelevant content. It is already at the maximum since          │
│              │                            │       │           │ everything provided is directly relevant to the input.                  │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.33  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.33 because the actual output wrongly claims the          │
│              │                            │       │           │ context only gives a name and time and does not know the schedule,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ even though the context does provide the AI Pulse schedule (every       │
│              │                            │       │           │ Saturday at 8pm IST) and only says some sessions are part of a          │
│              │                            │       │           │ YouTube Sandbox.                                                        │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.50  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.50 because the context contains some scheduling          │
│              │                            │       │           │ details like 'AI Pulse live sessions will be on Saturday' and 'Every    │
│              │                            │       │           │ Saturday at 8pm. Indian Standard Time.' and mentions 'YouTube           │
│              │                            │       │           │ Sandbox,' but it still lacks a direct, fully clear comparison for all   │
│              │                            │       │           │ requested aspects, since many statements are unrelated, e.g., 'The      │
│              │                            │       │           │ statement is just "Purple sessions."' and 'this statement about "our    │
│              │                            │       │           │ goal is very simple" does not provide any comparison or scheduling      │
│              │                            │       │           │ details.'                                                               │
│        FAIL  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.30  │ 0.50      │ The AI Pulse detail matches the expected schedule, but the YouTube      │
│              │                            │       │           │ Sandbox answer is incorrect because it says the frequency, day, and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ time are unknown, while the expected output specifies Wednesday and     │
│              │                            │       │           │ Friday at 6 pm with at least two sessions per week. This is a           │
│              │                            │       │           │ significant omission of key facts from the test case.                   │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The response is easy to understand and student friendly, with simple    │
│              │                            │       │           │ wording and clear comparison points. It directly answers the            │
│              │                            │       │           │ frequency, day, and time for AI Pulse, and appropriately notes that     │
│              │                            │       │           │ the YouTube Sandbox schedule cannot be determined from the provided     │
│              │                            │       │           │ context, keeping jargon to a minimum.                                   │
│                                                                                                                                         │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_74                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Confirm YouTube Sandbox starts 6 pm and mainly Wed/Fri, plus minimum weekly lectures?                        │
│  │     Actual Output:      Yes.                                                                                                         │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - YouTube Sandbox sessions will start at 6 pm (for this month).                                              │
│  │                         - They will be held mainly Wednesday and Friday (two days a week, but can be more).                          │
│  │                         - The instructor will try to deliver a minimum of two lectures per week (may take 3–4 some weeks).           │
│  │                         - A feedback/poll will be taken later to decide future timing.                                               │
│  │     Expected Output:    Yes. YouTube Sandbox starts at **6 pm**, and it will mainly be on **Wednesday and Friday**. The goal is      │
│  │                         to have **at least 2 lectures per week**, though it can sometimes be more.                                   │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is fully supported by node 1 and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ node 3 in retrieval context: node 1 says it starts at 6 pm onward,      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and node 3 says the sessions are generally on Wednesday and Friday.     │
│              │                            │       │           │ Sentence 2 is also supported by node 1, which implies at least 2        │
│              │                            │       │           │ lectures per week and sometimes more. Great match overall.              │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the retrieval contexts contain only           │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant nodes, and they are ranked well: the first node in retrieval   │
│              │                            │       │           │ contexts supports the 6 pm start with "will start at 6 pm onwards";     │
│              │                            │       │           │ the second node supports the weekly minimum with "I will try to do      │
│              │                            │       │           │ minimum two lectures in a week"; and the third node confirms the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ schedule with "Generally, these sessions will be Wednesday and          │
│              │                            │       │           │ Friday." There are no irrelevant nodes that need to be ranked lower,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ so the ordering is already ideal.                                       │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 0.62  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.62 because the response is partially relevant and        │
│              │                            │       │           │ confirms some of the requested timing details, but it loses points      │
│              │                            │       │           │ for adding unnecessary future-focused discussion and extra              │
│              │                            │       │           │ affirmation that don’t address the specific confirmation about          │
│              │                            │       │           │ YouTube Sandbox start time, Wed/Fri schedule, and minimum weekly        │
│              │                            │       │           │ lectures. It’s not higher because those irrelevant statements dilute    │
│              │                            │       │           │ the directness of the answer, but it remains fairly decent since the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ core topic is still on track.                                           │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.75  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.75 because the actual output adds details not            │
│              │                            │       │           │ supported by the context, such as naming an instructor and claiming a   │
│              │                            │       │           │ minimum number of lectures per week, even though the context only       │
│              │                            │       │           │ says the sessions are generally intended for Wednesday and Friday and   │
│              │                            │       │           │ could be more than two days a week.                                     │
│        FAIL  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.39  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.39 because the context does contain the key              │
│              │                            │       │           │ confirmation that “These sessions, YouTube sandbox ones, will start     │
│              │                            │       │           │ at 6 pm onwards,” are “Generally… Wednesday and Friday,” and that “I    │
│              │                            │       │           │ will try to do minimum two lectures in a week.” However, much of the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ rest is generic or unrelated, like “This is the program that we have    │
│              │                            │       │           │ discussed” and “You can interact with me,” so it only partially         │
│              │                            │       │           │ answers the input.                                                      │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response matches the expected answer on the key points: YouTube     │
│              │                            │       │           │ Sandbox starts at 6 pm, is mainly on Wednesday and Friday, and aims     │
│              │                            │       │           │ for at least two lectures per week. It adds extra details about         │
│              │                            │       │           │ feedback/poll and possible 3–4 lectures some weeks, which are not in    │
│              │                            │       │           │ the expected output, but they do not contradict the main facts.         │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The reply is simple and student-friendly, using short bullet points     │
│              │                            │       │           │ and only a few easy terms. It directly confirms the 6 pm start,         │
│              │                            │       │           │ mainly Wednesday/Friday timing, and the minimum of two lectures per     │
│              │                            │       │           │ week, while avoiding technical jargon. The extra note about a future    │
│              │                            │       │           │ feedback poll is clear and does not make it difficult to understand.    │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_75                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How does post-filtration evolution use depth/comparison/breadth probs and 3 stages to complicate             │
│  │                         questions?                                                                                                   │
│  │     Actual Output:      1) Filtration (first step)                                                                                   │
│  │                         - Pull a set of base questions from your LLM (e.g., 10 questions). These are the inputs you will evolve.     │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         2) Determine per-stage evolution probabilities                                                               │
│  │                         - Use the fixed probabilities for each evolution type: depth 0.3, comparison 0.6, breadth 0.1.               │
│  │                         - For 10 questions that maps to ~3 depth, 6 comparison, 1 breadth at each evolution stage.                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         3) Apply stage-by-stage evolution (3 stages total)                                                           │
│  │                         - Stage 1: From the filtered set choose which questions to evolve and apply the chosen transformation per    │
│  │                         the probabilities (make 3 questions deeper, 6 comparative, 1 broader).                                       │
│  │                         - Stage 2: Take the evolved iterations from Stage 1 as the new inputs. Re-apply the same probabilities to    │
│  │                         each of those inputs to decide the next evolution type for each (e.g., a question that was made deeper in    │
│  │                         Stage 1 might be selected as comparison in Stage 2).                                                         │
│  │                         - Stage 3: Repeat once more (final evolution), again using 0.3/0.6/0.1 on the Stage 2 outputs.               │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         4) How the tree/pathing works                                                                                │
│  │                         - Each question follows a path through up to 3 evolution steps (a tree branch). After each evolution the     │
│  │                         resulting iteration becomes the input for the next stage, so a question can accumulate changes (e.g.,        │
│  │                         depth → comparison → comparison).                                                                            │
│  │                         - The “tree” grows downward as you keep evolving chosen iterations; the per-stage probabilities decide       │
│  │                         branching at each node.                                                                                      │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         5) Control knobs                                                                                             │
│  │                         - You can pick how many questions to evolve at each stage (not necessarily all).                             │
│  │                         - Probabilities remain the same each stage (0.3, 0.6, 0.1), so distribution of depth/comparison/breadth      │
│  │                         choices is re-sampled at each evolution stage.                                                               │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Example: With 10 questions, Stage 1 gives 3 depth / 6 comparison / 1 breadth. Pick one of the                │
│  │                         depth-evolved questions; Stage 2 the probabilities pick comparison for it, so it becomes                     │
│  │                         depth→comparison. Stage 3 the probabilities pick comparison again, so final path is                          │
│  │                         depth→comparison→comparison (three-stage evolution making the question progressively trickier).              │
│  │     Expected Output:    Post-filtration evolution takes the filtered questions and rewrites each one **3 times**.                    │
│  │                         At each stage, it samples from the evolution probabilities — for example **depth: 0.3, comparison: 0.6,      │
│  │                         breadth: 0.1** — and makes the question more complex along that path.                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So a question may go through:                                                                                │
│  │                         1. **Stage 1**: slightly trickier                                                                            │
│  │                         2. **Stage 2**: more refined                                                                                 │
│  │                         3. **Stage 3**: even more human-like and realistic                                                           │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         The goal is to turn raw synthetic questions into **more complex, real-world-like questions** for better      │
│  │                         testing.                                                                                                     │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because sentence 1 is fully supported by nodes 1      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and 3 in retrieval context, and sentence 2 is supported by nodes 1      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and 2 with the exact 3-time rewrite and sampled probabilities.          │
│              │                            │       │           │ Sentences 3 and 4 are also covered by node 2’s staged refinement and    │
│              │                            │       │           │ node 3’s ‘make it a little trickier/evolve it’ description, so every    │
│              │                            │       │           │ sentence in the expected output is attributable to the retrieval        │
│              │                            │       │           │ context.                                                                │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the top-ranked nodes in the retrieval         │
│              │                            │       │           │ contexts are all directly relevant and already capture the needed       │
│              │                            │       │           │ details: node 1 quotes the 'depth probability value Is 30%',            │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'Comparison probability value Is 60%', and 'breath is 10%' to explain   │
│              │                            │       │           │ how questions evolve, node 2 notes 'this question in the second step    │
│              │                            │       │           │ of refinement' and 'we have set as 3' to support the three-stage        │
│              │                            │       │           │ progression, and node 3 adds that you 'First of all you do              │
│              │                            │       │           │ Filtration' and then 'you evolve it' to make it 'more tricky'. Since    │
│              │                            │       │           │ there are no irrelevant nodes above relevant ones, nothing needs to     │
│              │                            │       │           │ be ranked lower, which is why the score is perfect.                     │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response is fully aligned with the        │
│              │                            │       │           │ input and contains no irrelevant statements. It directly addresses      │
│              │                            │       │           │ how post-filtration evolution uses depth, comparison, breadth           │
│              │                            │       │           │ probabilities, and the three stages, so it cannot reasonably be         │
│              │                            │       │           │ scored higher.                                                          │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.39  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.39 because the actual output invents a three-stage       │
│              │                            │       │           │ evolution pipeline with Stage 1/2/3, tree-like branching, and           │
│              │                            │       │           │ repeated per-stage probability sampling, while the context only says    │
│              │                            │       │           │ filtration comes first, then questions are evolved and can be further   │
│              │                            │       │           │ evolved. It also adds a breadth question at each stage and a            │
│              │                            │       │           │ depth-to-comparison path, even though the context only specifies that   │
│              │                            │       │           │ for 10 questions, 3 should be depth-based and 6 comparison-related,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ without those stage-specific details.                                   │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.79  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.79 because most of the context directly explains the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ mechanism, e.g. "Let's say depth probability value is 30%",             │
│              │                            │       │           │ "Comparison probability value is 60%", "And breath is 10%", and "We     │
│              │                            │       │           │ have set as 3", which matches the input about using                     │
│              │                            │       │           │ depth/comparison/breadth probs and 3 stages. A few fragments are        │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant or vague, such as "Now let's say", "Now again", and "First   │
│              │                            │       │           │ of all you do", so the context is relevant overall but not perfectly    │
│              │                            │       │           │ focused.                                                                │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response captures the core idea of stage-by-stage post-filtration   │
│              │                            │       │           │ evolution and correctly mentions the 0.3/0.6/0.1 probabilities and      │
│              │                            │       │           │ three stages. However, it adds specifics that are not in the expected   │
│              │                            │       │           │ answer, such as a 10-question example, exact per-stage counts of 3      │
│              │                            │       │           │ depth/6 comparison/1 breadth, and a tree/branching explanation. It      │
│              │                            │       │           │ also omits the expected emphasis that each filtered question is         │
│              │                            │       │           │ rewritten 3 times and that the goal is to make questions more           │
│              │                            │       │           │ human-like and realistic for better testing.                            │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.90  │ 0.50      │ The response is structured and understandable, with numbered steps      │
│              │                            │       │           │ and an example that directly addresses the 3 stages and the             │
│              │                            │       │           │ depth/comparison/breadth probabilities from the prompt. It uses some    │
│              │                            │       │           │ technical terms like "tree/pathing" and "branching," but overall the    │
│              │                            │       │           │ explanation is still fairly student-friendly and simple to follow. It   │
│              │                            │       │           │ could be even simpler by avoiding extra implementation-like detail,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ but it meets the request well.                                          │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_76                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How does filtered synthetic golden evolve over 3 stages, and what does each stage add?                       │
│  │     Actual Output:      Stage 1 — initial generation (implicit in context)                                                           │
│  │                         - Produces the raw synthetic "golden" items (questions). These are the unfiltered, simple outputs from       │
│  │                         the generator.                                                                                               │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Stage 2 — filtration                                                                                         │
│  │                         - Pass the generated items through a quality filter/mesh that enforces generation standards.                 │
│  │                         - Remove any bad questions or those that fail the set quality threshold (example: global threshold 0.5;      │
│  │                         an item with quality 0.4 is dropped, one with 0.9 is passed).                                                │
│  │                         - Only the filtered (acceptable) goldens move to the next stage. You do not evolve raw input before this     │
│  │                         step.                                                                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Stage 3 — evolution                                                                                          │
│  │                         - Rewrite the filtered goldens to increase their complexity and realism.                                     │
│  │                         - The goal is to make them more complex and indistinguishable from human-curated goldens, improving          │
│  │                         realism and difficulty compared with the original raw questions.                                             │
│  │     Expected Output:    The filtered synthetic golden is rewritten 3 times, and each stage adds more complexity.                     │
│  │                         At each evolution, a path is sampled from a probability distribution, such as:                               │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Depth**: makes the question deeper                                                                       │
│  │                         - **Comparison**: adds comparison-related traits                                                             │
│  │                         - **Breadth**: broadens the question                                                                         │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         For example, a question may first go to the **depth** path, then **comparison**, and then again              │
│  │                         **comparison**. After 3 stages, the final question becomes more tricky, realistic, and human-like,           │
│  │                         removing raw, simple features.                                                                               │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        FAIL  │ Contextual Recall          │ 0.40  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.40 because sentence 2 is only loosely supported by       │
│              │                            │       │           │ node 3 in the node(s) in retrieval context, which mentions rewriting    │
│              │                            │       │           │ the filtered golden to look more complex, more realistic, and           │
│              │                            │       │           │ human-curated; sentence 3 is also partially supported by node 3’s       │
│              │                            │       │           │ evolution/rewrite description. However, sentence 1 is not supported     │
│              │                            │       │           │ since no node states it is rewritten 3 times or that each stage adds    │
│              │                            │       │           │ more complexity, and sentence 4 is unsupported because no node names    │
│              │                            │       │           │ paths like Depth, Comparison, or Breadth or explains sampling from a    │
│              │                            │       │           │ probability distribution. Sentence 5 is likewise unsupported because    │
│              │                            │       │           │ the retrieval context does not describe a depth→comparison→comparison   │
│              │                            │       │           │ sequence, only general filtration/evolution.                            │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because all three nodes in retrieval contexts are     │
│              │                            │       │           │ relevant and already ranked well: the first node directly answers the   │
│              │                            │       │           │ evolution step with 'Evolve the filtered synthetic golden' and          │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'increase complexity', the second node provides supporting filtration   │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_77                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How do generation, filtration, and evolution stages transform goldens, filtered goldens, and evolved         │
│  │                         inputs?                                                                                                      │
│  │     Actual Output:      Generation: produce initial synthetic "goldens" (questions) from the LLM — raw candidate items that may      │
│  │                         vary in quality.                                                                                             │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Filtration: subject those generated goldens to a quality test (a filter mesh with a set threshold, e.g.      │
│  │                         0.5). Any golden scoring below the threshold (e.g. 0.4) is removed; any above (e.g. 0.9) is passed on.       │
│  │                         This step filters out bad or unacceptable questions so only acceptable "filtered goldens" remain.            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Evolution: take the filtered goldens (not raw input) and transform them to increase complexity and           │
│  │                         realism — make them trickier and more lifelike. The output of this stage are evolved inputs ready for        │
│  │                         downstream use.                                                                                              │
│  │     Expected Output:    Generation creates **synthetic goldens** from the context using an LLM.                                      │
│  │                         Filtration removes any low-quality or non-compliant goldens using a **quality threshold**.                   │
│  │                         Evolution then takes the **filtered goldens** and rewrites them multiple times to make them **more           │
│  │                         complex, realistic, and human-like**.                                                                        │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So the flow is: **goldens → filtered goldens → evolved inputs**.                                             │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_78                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              Explain latency telemetry flow: logging, dashboarding, and >4ms threshold alerts.                            │
│  │     Actual Output:      Latency telemetry flow (captured quantity):                                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         1. Capture and log                                                                                           │
│  │                         - Latency is treated as a captured quantity (no extra computation needed).                                   │
│  │                         - Each observed latency value is logged and sent directly to your dashboard.                                 │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         2. Dashboarding                                                                                              │
│  │                         - The dashboard presents latency as a time-series so you can inspect recent and historical behavior          │
│  │                         (examples: last 1 hour, 24 hours, 1 week, 6 months).                                                         │
│  │                         - You can use the graph to understand trends and spot spikes.                                                │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         3. Alerting (>4 ms threshold)                                                                                │
│  │                         - You set an alert rule on the dashboard: if latency exceeds 4 ms, an alert is triggered.                    │
│  │                         - The concerned team/engineer receives an alert email.                                                       │
│  │                         - The engineer takes corrective action (for example, starting or adjusting the load balancer and managing    │
│  │                         traffic), and over time the latency will come down automatically.                                            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Flow summary: log the captured latency → present it on a dashboard (time-based views) → set alerting         │
│  │                         (e.g., >4 ms) to notify engineers to normalize the system.                                                   │
│  │     Expected Output:    Latency telemetry flow is:                                                                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         1. **Logging**: record the latency value in tracing/logs.                                                    │
│  │                         2. **Dashboarding**: show the latency over time on a monitoring dashboard.                                   │
│  │                         3. **Alerting**: if latency crosses the set threshold, e.g. **more than 4 ms**, trigger an alert on          │
│  │                         Slack, email, PagerDuty, etc., so the team can act quickly.                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         This is for a **captured quantity** like latency, where you store it directly and monitor it without         │
│  │                         computation.                                                                                                 │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ time, and sentence 3 is supported by nodes 1 and 3 for alerting after   │

╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ❌ test_case_79                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How do logs, dashboards, alerts, and evaluators differ for captured vs computed quantities?                  │
│  │     Actual Output:      Captured quantities (from the context)                                                                       │
│  │                         - Logging: recorded directly (example: traced latency values like 2.03 s).                                   │
│  │                         - Dashboarding: sent straight to the dashboard and plotted (you inspect the graph to see behavior).          │
│  │                         - Alerting: alerts are set on the dashboard/monitoring (e.g., if latency > 4 ms an alert email is sent).     │
│  │                         - Evaluator: not needed — no computation step before logging/dashboarding/alerting.                          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Computed quantities                                                                                          │
│  │                         - The excerpt only says “this is not the flow for computed quantities” and does not give the full flow.      │
│  │                         It implies the flow differs (and by contrast suggests an evaluation step would be involved), but the         │
│  │                         exact logging/dashboard/alert/evaluator details for computed quantities are not provided in the given        │
│  │                         context — I don't know them from this text.                                                                  │
│  │     Expected Output:    For **captured quantities**, you just **log** the value, show it on a **dashboard**, and set up              │
│  │                         **alerts** if it crosses a threshold.                                                                        │
│  │                         Examples: **latency, token usage, cost per conversation**.                                                   │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         For **computed quantities**, you first **sample conversations**, then run an **evaluator** to calculate      │
│  │                         the metric, and after that you can **dashboard** and **alert** on the aggregated score.                      │
│  │                         Examples: **faithfulness, hallucination, correctness, toxicity**.                                            │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So the key difference is:                                                                                    │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         - **Captured**: directly recorded, no evaluator needed                                                       │
│  │                         - **Computed**: needs an evaluator before dashboarding and alerting                                          │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                            │       │           │ in retrieval context: nodes 1-3 describe captured quantities as         │
│              │                            │       │           │ directly logged, shown on a dashboard, and alerted on, and they also    │
│              │                            │       │           │ contrast computed quantities as requiring an evaluator before           │
│              │                            │       │           │ dashboarding/alerting. Sentence 1 is strongly supported by the          │
│              │                            │       │           │ captured flow and examples like latency, and sentence 2 is supported    │
│              │                            │       │           │ by the computed flow requiring sampling and evaluation before           │
│              │                            │       │           │ aggregation. Sentence 3 is mostly supported by the                      │
│              │                            │       │           │ captured-vs-computed contrast, but some phrasing is only loosely        │
│              │                            │       │           │ matched: node 3 is a generic monitoring/dashboard example, and no       │
│              │                            │       │           │ node mentions the unsupported wording about “chart board,” so the       │
│              │                            │       │           │ recall is high but not perfect.                                         │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the top-ranked retrieval context nodes are    │
│              │                            │       │           │ all relevant to the question: node 1 says "you log, you present it on   │
│              │                            │       │           │ a dashboard, and you set up an alert system" and that "This is not      │
│              │                            │       │           │ the flow for computed quantities," node 2 says there is "no need to     │
│              │                            │       │           │ set up an evaluator" for captured quantities and they are "directly     │
│              │                            │       │           │ recorded," and node 3 adds that captured quantities can be "send[ ]     │
│              │                            │       │           │ directly to your dashboard." Since there are no irrelevant nodes        │
│              │                            │       │           │ ranked above relevant ones, the ordering is perfectly precise.          │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00 because the response fully addresses the comparison   │
│              │                            │       │           │ of logs, dashboards, alerts, and evaluators for captured vs. computed   │
│              │                            │       │           │ quantities without introducing any irrelevant content. It’s concise,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ on-topic, and complete.                                                 │
│        FAIL  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.22  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.22 because the actual output adds unsupported details    │
│              │                            │       │           │ like a logging step, a specific latency value of 2.03 s, direct         │
│              │                            │       │           │ plotting of captured quantities on the dashboard, and an alert email    │
│              │                            │       │           │ when latency exceeds 4 ms. It also overstates the flow by implying      │
│              │                            │       │           │ computed quantities use an evaluation step, even though the context     │
│              │                            │       │           │ only describes captured quantities and does not provide that            │
│              │                            │       │           │ computed-quantity process.                                              │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Relevancy       │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.80 because most of the context directly explains         │
│              │                            │       │           │ captured quantities, e.g. “If you are talking about a captured          │
│              │                            │       │           │ quantity, the flow is like this: you log, you present it on a           │
│              │                            │       │           │ dashboard, and you set up an alert system” and “There is no need to     │
│              │                            │       │           │ set up an evaluator.” However, a few parts are irrelevant or noisy,     │
│              │                            │       │           │ such as “So he will quickly normalize things.” and the example about    │
│              │                            │       │           │ “suddenly you launched a course” and “50,000 people came to the         │
│              │                            │       │           │ website together,” so the match is strong but not perfect.              │
│        PASS  │ answer correctness [GEval] │ 0.70  │ 0.50      │ The response is mostly aligned with the captured-quantities flow: it    │
│              │                            │       │           │ says they are logged directly, dashboarded, and alerted on, with no     │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluator needed. However, it misses the expected computed-quantities   │
│              │                            │       │           │ details entirely: the expected output says computed quantities          │
│              │                            │       │           │ require sampling conversations, running an evaluator, then              │
│              │                            │       │           │ dashboarding and alerting on the aggregated score, with examples like   │
│              │                            │       │           │ faithfulness, hallucination, correctness, and toxicity. Instead, it     │
│              │                            │       │           │ states the excerpt does not provide the full flow, which is an          │
│              │                            │       │           │ omission relative to the expected answer.                               │
│        PASS  │ simple explanation [GEval] │ 0.80  │ 0.50      │ The response is clear and easy to follow, with simple bullet points     │
│              │                            │       │           │ and mostly student-friendly language. It correctly explains the         │
│              │                            │       │           │ captured quantities flow and notes that computed quantities are not     │
│              │                            │       │           │ fully specified in the excerpt, which is honest and grounded in the     │
│              │                            │       │           │ provided text. However, it includes a small amount of technical         │
│              │                            │       │           │ phrasing like "evaluator" and "dashboard/monitoring," so it is simple   │
│              │                            │       │           │ but not maximally basic.                                                │
│                                                                                                                                         │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

│                                                                                                                                         │
│  ✅ test_case_80                                                                                                                        │
│  ├──   Input:              How does LangSmith online eval work: capture, stratified sampling, evaluator, dashboard, alerts?             │
│  │     Actual Output:      - Capture: LangSmith captures online runs via tracing/logging — if you run an evaluator on tracing or        │
│  │                         logging it becomes an online evaluator.                                                                      │
│  │                         - Sampling: the evaluator runs on sampled conversations (the context says sampled conversations are          │
│  │                         used). Whether the sampling is stratified (or how sampling is implemented) is not specified in the           │
│  │                         provided context — I don't know.                                                                             │
│  │                         - Evaluator: the evaluator performs the evaluation on those sampled conversations and produces a metric.     │
│  │                         You can set up multiple evaluators and multiple evaluation pipelines.                                        │
│  │                         - Dashboard/aggregation: the produced metric is aggregated over a time period and shown on the dashboard.    │
│  │                         - Alerts: if an aggregated metric crosses a configured threshold, the alerting system is triggered.          │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         Additional note from context: running on a dataset (offline setup) makes the evaluator an offline            │
│  │                         evaluator. LangSmith provides the full platform for creating datasets, running experiments, logging,         │
│  │                         monitoring, and alerting.                                                                                    │
│  │     Expected Output:    LangSmith online eval works like this:                                                                       │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         1. **Capture** production signals in tracing, like latency, token usage, or cost.                            │
│  │                         2. **Stratified sampling** picks more examples from important/problematic groups, like thumbs-down chats,    │
│  │                         escalations, refund/admission queries, or abrupt conversations.                                              │
│  │                         3. **Evaluator** runs on the sampled conversations to compute metrics like hallucination, faithfulness,      │
│  │                         correctness, toxicity, etc.                                                                                  │
│  │                         4. **Dashboard** aggregates those metrics over time and shows trends.                                        │
│  │                         5. **Alerts** trigger when a metric crosses a threshold, sending notifications via Slack, email,             │
│  │                         PagerDuty, or a custom API.                                                                                  │
│  │                                                                                                                                      │
│  │                         So the flow is: **capture → sample → evaluate → dashboard → alert**.                                         │
│  └── Metrics                                                                                                                            │
│       Status ┃ Metric                     ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason                                                                  │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall          │ 0.83  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.83 because sentences 1, 3, 4, and 5 are supported by     │
│              │                            │       │           │ node(s) in retrieval context: node 1 and node 2 mention tracing         │
│              │                            │       │           │ signals, evaluation on sampled conversations, dashboard aggregation,    │
│              │                            │       │           │ and alerting on thresholds, matching                                    │
│              │                            │       │           │ capture/evaluate/dashboard/alert; sentence 2 is the only gap, since     │
│              │                            │       │           │ no node in retrieval context mentions stratified sampling or            │
│              │                            │       │           │ selecting more examples from problematic groups.                        │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Precision       │ 0.83  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.83 because the most relevant node in retrieval           │

│              │                            │       │           │ with 'Evaluator performs the evaluation on those sampled                │
│              │                            │       │           │ conversations', 'aggregating that metric over a time period and         │
│              │                            │       │           │ showing it on the dashboard', and 'if a threshold is crossing then I    │
│              │                            │       │           │ am triggering the alerting system.' A less relevant node appears at     │
│              │                            │       │           │ rank 2 before a relevant node at rank 3; it focuses on 'offline         │
│              │                            │       │           │ experiments' and 'run your online and offline evaluators,' so it        │
│              │                            │       │           │ should be ranked lower since it does not specifically explain the       │
│              │                            │       │           │ online eval pipeline. The rank-3 node is still useful because it says   │
│              │                            │       │           │ 'If you run it on tracing or logging then this evaluator will become    │
│              │                            │       │           │ an online evaluator,' which supports the capture step, but it is less   │
│              │                            │       │           │ complete than the first node, so the score is high but not perfect.     │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy           │ 0.92  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.92 because the response mostly addresses LangSmith       │
│              │                            │       │           │ online eval and covers the key ideas, but it includes a minor           │
│              │                            │       │           │ irrelevant note about the sampling method being unspecified, which      │
│              │                            │       │           │ does not directly explain how the feature works. This keeps it from     │
│              │                            │       │           │ being a perfect score, though the answer is still highly relevant       │
│              │                            │       │           │ overall.                                                                │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness               │ 0.73  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.73 because the actual output slightly overstates the     │

│              │                            │       │           │ context only says it supports logging, monitoring, offline              │
│              │                            │       │           │ evaluators, and data sets without making those links explicit. It       │
